title: Introduction to Clinical Anesthesia
description: This course introduces students to the operating room, emphasizing the fundamental procedures and techniques used in administering an anesthetic. The course prepares and educates students to work within the Anesthesia Care Team (ACT) as an Anesthesiologist Assistant (AA).  There will be an introduction to the process of induction, maintenance, and emergence from anesthesia. Beyond didactic lectures and coursework, students will gain hands on experience through clinical immersion weeks. Coursework will parallel to laboratory and simulation-based courses by introducing the student to the operating room and the various elements of providing a safe anesthetic.

title: Medical Terminology
description: This online asynchronous course will teach students to understand medical terminology as it pertains to each body system, including an overview of how the different organ systems function, information about various medical disorders, and information about diagnostic and treatment procedures. Using graphics and study tips students will not only learn definitions and root terms, but also their application in the medical world. Students will be provided with fun and unusual facts to help remember the medical terminology in unique ways. There will be opportunities to see how medical terminology is used in the real world through case study assignments. The knowledge from this course will prepare students for a career as an anesthesiologist assistant through providing a framework to the medical terminology that is used daily.

title: General Anatomy & Physiology
description: This course covers basic human anatomy and physiology from an integrative perspective. Material will focus on the cellular and tissue levels and their integration into the systems of the body. Students will gain knowledge through lectures, hands on models, and interactive software.

title: Introduction to Anesthesia Delivery Systems
description: Introduction to anesthesia delivery systems is grounded in the physical principles involved in the delivery of anesthesia. Coursework in the class will provide knowledge and skills relevant to the mechanical, electrical, and pneumatic components of anesthesia monitoring, delivery systems, and their safety mechanisms. This course will equip students with the knowledge and skills to prevent and troubleshoot critical incidents in anesthesia delivery. Student learning will be obtained through readings, media assignments, lectures, and hands-on activities.

title: Principles of Airways
description: This course will provide foundational knowledge and skills required to perform patient airway assessment, airway management, and artificial ventilation.  Facilitation of learning in this course will be through readings, skill videos, lectures, in class discussions, small group exercises, course assignment, and skills exercises.

title: Pharmacology for Anesthesia I
description: Pharmacology for Anesthesia I introduces students to the study of pharmacology with clinical anesthesia applications. This course focuses on the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of major drug classifications used in the practice of anesthesia, including inhaled anesthetics, opioids, barbiturates, benzodiazepines, anticholinesterases, anticholinergics, neuromuscular blockers, and adrenergic agonists and antagonists.  Students will be given an overview of drug actions, interactions, metabolism, methods of administration, dosages, side effects, precautions, and contraindications.

title: Basic Technical Skills & Patient Assessment
description: The basic technical skills and patient assessment course will prepare students with the knowledge and skills to assess and plan for anesthetic management of patients. Through small group activities and the use of patient actors, students will learn how to effectively communicate with patients, perform a basic preoperative evaluation, and obtain informed consent. Students will gain knowledge on anesthetic risk assessment, diagnostic testing and the information needed for the formulation of a basic anesthetic care plan. Using lectures and hands-on workshops, students will develop fundamental anesthesia skill techniques. Students will gain the technical skills of cardiac and pulmonary auscultation, and manually obtaining vital signs. Coursework will be completed through lectures, assignments, group activities, and role playing with patient actors.

title: Anesthesia Skills Lab & Simulation I
description: This course is a simulation and laboratory-based course, integrating immersive experience in clinical anesthesia.  The coursework focuses on an introduction to experiences in the operating room with emphasis on the fundamental procedures and techniques used in administering an anesthetic. The knowledge and skills learned will prepare students to work within the anesthesia care team. Skills learned will be airway management, intubation, IV and arterial line placement, monitoring and placement, anesthesia machine, medication safety, sterile techniques, and key steps in induction, management and emergence of an anesthetic.

title: Independent Study
description:
Specific intensive directed study at the masters level relevant to health sciences studies pertaining to anesthesia.
Restriction: permission of program director

title: Clinical Decision Making I
description: This seminar course focuses on the anesthetic considerations and perioperative management that must be accounted for in patients with co-existing diseases due to physiological changes. Using didactic instruction, clinical data, and case scenarios, students will improve their understanding of how pathophysiological changes within patients can impact the administration of perioperative anesthetic management.

title: Principles of Clinical Instrumentation & Patient Monitoring I
description: This Instrumentation and Monitoring course will teach students the clinical application of anesthesia instrumentation through knowledge in circuits and engineering concepts. Monitors and devices used in the operating room are studied with respect to the fundamentals of operation, calibration, and interpretation of data. Students will learn principles, application, and interpretation of various monitoring modalities including ECG, invasive and non-invasive blood pressure, oximetry, cardiac output, respiratory gas analysis, and respiration.  The course covers advanced concepts of arterial pressure monitoring and intracranial pressure monitoring.

title: Respiratory Physiology
description: This respiratory physiology course focuses on the human respiratory physiology and pathophysiology. Students will learn the functions and common disease states of the human pulmonary system in the context of anesthesia management.

title: Pharmacology for Anesthesia II
description: The course is a continuation of ANES 505 with emphasis placed on the integration of clinical therapeutics of various anesthesia medications as well as basic principles and pharmacologic properties of clinically relevant medications. The course content provides an opportunity for students to deepen their understanding of the clinical use of medications. Basic principles of drug action; absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion of drugs; mechanisms of drug action; toxicity.  Basis for the use of medicines in pharmacologic therapy of specific diseases.

title: Anesthesia Principles & Practice I
description: This course prepares students for clinical rotations and anesthetic management in specialty cases through a focus on the clinical relevance of human physiology that pertains to patients in the perioperative period. This course builds on students’ knowledge of pharmacology, physiology, anatomy, and anesthesiology to create anesthetic perioperative management plans that is individualized for surgical and patient specific needs. Using didactic instruction, clinical data, and case scenarios, students will improve their understanding of how the administration of anesthetic agents and the stress of surgery impacts both normal, acute, and chronic physiology in the perioperative period.

title: Anesthesia Skills Lab & Simulation II
description: This course is a simulation and laboratory-based course, integrating immersive experience in clinical anesthesia.  The coursework focuses on an introduction to experiences in the operating room with emphasis on the fundamental procedures and techniques used in administering an anesthetic. The knowledge and skills learned will prepare students to work within the anesthesia care team. Skills learned will be advanced airway management, ultrasound line placement, neuraxial technical skills, medication safety, sterile techniques, and key steps in induction, management and emergence of an anesthetic.

title: Clinical Anesthesia Experience I
description: Clinical anesthesia experience utilizes a practicum approach to provide students with the opportunity to apply the principles of anesthesia to direct patient care. Through one-on-one supervised instruction students will develop skills and foundations for perioperative anesthesia case management in operating room and ancillary anesthetizing locations. Expectations and responsibilities increase throughout each semester as students’ knowledge and skills develop within didactic, simulation, and lab courses.

title: Medical Spanish
description: This course will provide a foundation of Spanish for the healthcare professional specializing in anesthesia. Through grammar structure, relevant vocabulary, and cultural knowledge students will learn and utilize language to facilitate their daily interactions with Spanish-speaking patients.

title: Clinical Decision Making II
description: This seminar course focuses on the anesthetic considerations and perioperative management that must be accounted for in patients with co-existing diseases due to physiological changes. Using didactic instruction, clinical data, and case scenarios, students will improve their understanding of how pathophysiological changes within patients can impact the administration of perioperative anesthetic management.

title: Principles of Clinical Instrumentation & Patient Monitoring II
description: Students will learn the clinical application of anesthesia instrumentation through knowledge in circuits and engineering concepts. Monitors and devices used in the operating room are studied with respect to the fundamentals of operation, calibration, and interpretation of data. Students will learn principles, application, and interpretation of various monitoring modalities including intraoperative neurophysiology monitoring, temperature, renal function, coagulation/hemostasis, neuromuscular junction, transesophageal echocardiography, and point of care ultrasound testing. The course covers advanced concepts of transesophageal echocardiography, electric and radiation safety, and the hazards and complications of monitoring patients during anesthesia.

title: Cardiac Electrophysiology
description: The course focus is the physiology and pathophysiology of the cardiovascular system. Students will gain knowledge on cardiovascular structures, electrical innervation, and hemodynamics of the heart and vascular system. Emphasis will be made on physiological cardiac responses, electrocardiography interpretation, and anesthetic risk factors.

title: Advanced Technical Skills & Patient Assessment
description: The advanced technical skills and patient assessment course prepares students with the knowledge and skills to assess and plan for critically ill and emergency patients. Students also learn advanced anesthesia skill techniques. Through small group activities and the use of patient actors, students enhance their skills in patient assessment. By utilizing skill trainers students learn how to place spinals, epidurals, and central lines. Airway mannequins are used for skill development in fiberoptic intubations and cricothyrotomy airway placement. Coursework is completed through lectures, assignments, group activities, and hands-on skill trainers.

title: Anesthesia Principles & Practice II
description: This course prepares students for their clinical rotations and anesthetic management in specialty cases through a focus on the clinical relevance of human physiology that pertains to patients in the perioperative period. This course builds on students’ knowledge of pharmacology, physiology, anatomy, and anesthesiology to create anesthetic perioperative management plans that is individualized for surgical and patient specific needs. Using didactic instruction, clinical data, and case scenarios, students will improve their understanding of how the administration of anesthetic agents and the stress of surgery impacts both normal, acute, and chronic physiology in the perioperative period.

title: Anesthesia Skills Lab & Simulation III
description: This course is a simulation and laboratory-based course, integrating immersive experience in clinical anesthesia.  The coursework focuses on an introduction to experiences in the operating room with emphasis on the fundamental procedures and techniques used in administering an anesthetic. The knowledge and skills learned will prepare students to work within the anesthesia care team. Skills learned will be advanced airway management, pediatric skills and case management, medication safety, sterile techniques, and key steps in induction, management and emergence of an anesthetic.

title: Clinical Anesthesia Experience II
description: Clinical anesthesia experience utilizes a practicum approach to provide students with the opportunity to apply the principles of anesthesia to direct patient care. Through one-on-one supervised instruction students will develop skills and foundations for perioperative anesthesia case management in operating room and ancillary anesthetizing locations. Expectations and responsibilities increase throughout each semester as students’ knowledge and skills develop within didactic, simulation, and lab courses.

title: Regional Anesthesia
description: This regional anesthesia course develop students’ fundamental understanding and practical skills of regional anesthesia and utilization of ultrasound technology to enhance patient outcomes. Lectures, multimedia assignments, and case-based scenarios provide students with in-depth knowledge of local anesthetics, regional block equipment, and types of regional anesthesia. Using hands-on practice with ultrasounds and mannequin trainers, students gain technical skills and learn the steps of regional blocks.

title: Liver, Endocrine, & Renal Physiology
description: Liver, Endocrine & Renal Physiology teaches the functions of the selected body systems at a level required for clinical anesthesia management. This course covers the normal physiology and selected diseases of the liver, endocrine and renal organ systems. Students develop an understanding of the integrated functions of the normal body, while also gaining skills in problem solving and critical thinking to evaluate and manage clinical events in the perioperative setting.

title: Medical Humanities & Sociology I
description: This graduate level seminar explores the ethnic, economic, demographic and geographic variables impacting public health in New Mexico and the Southwest. This course challenges the notion that health outcomes are the sole product of an individual’s choice. Through investigation and weaving together the impacts of social and institutional variables on health behavior, students will understand the social determinants of health and social constructions of disease. Topics include access to health care; local alternatives to medical treatment; cultural definitions of health, illness and death.

title: Anesthesia Principles & Practice III
description: This course prepares students for their clinical rotations and anesthetic management in specialty cases through a focus on the clinical relevance of human physiology that pertains to patients in the perioperative period. This course builds on students’ knowledge of pharmacology, physiology, anatomy, and anesthesiology to create anesthetic perioperative management plans that is individualized for surgical and patient specific needs. Using didactic instruction, clinical data, and case scenarios, students improve their understanding of how the administration of anesthetic agents and the stress of surgery impacts both normal, acute, and chronic physiology in the perioperative period.

title: Anesthesia Skills Lab & Simulation IV
description: This course is a simulation and laboratory-based course, integrating immersive experience in clinical anesthesia.  The coursework focuses on an introduction to experiences in the operating room with emphasis on the fundamental procedures and techniques used in administering an anesthetic. The knowledge and skills learned prepares students to work within the anesthesia care team. Skills learned are central line placement, ACLS case management, utilization of the Stanford Emergency Manual and advanced anesthetic case management, medication safety, sterile techniques, and key steps in induction, management, and emergence of an anesthetic.

title: Clinical Anesthesia Experience III
description: Clinical anesthesia experience utilizes a practicum approach to provide students with the opportunity to apply the principles of anesthesia to direct patient care. Through one-on-one supervised instruction students will develop skills and foundations for perioperative anesthesia case management in operating room and ancillary anesthetizing locations. Expectations and responsibilities increase throughout each semester as students’ knowledge and skills develop within didactic, simulation, and lab courses.

title: Ethics, Law, & Diversity
description: This graduate level seminar will examine the ethical and personal effects on decisions, conducts and policies of healthcare.  Students will do self-examination to understand how their thoughts, actions, inactions, and professionalism can affect patient care while gaining understanding and skills on how to navigate challenges as an anesthesia provider in the interprofessional healthcare setting.

title: Medical Humanities & Sociology II
description: Medical Humanities & Sociology II explores the ethnic, economic, demographic and geographic variables impacting public health in New Mexico and the Southwest. As future health professionals, students gain an understanding of the cultural and social environment that shapes individual and collective experiences related to health and illness, specifically within the context of the diverse patient population in New Mexico. Students will learn concepts, ideas, and theories from social epidemiology, public health, and health communication.

title: Clinical Anesthesia IV
description: Clinical anesthesia experience utilizes a practicum approach to provide students with the opportunity to apply the principles of anesthesia to direct patient care. Through one-on-one supervised instruction students develop skills and foundations for perioperative anesthesia case management in operating room and ancillary anesthetizing locations. Expectations and responsibilities increase throughout each semester as students’ knowledge and skills develop within didactic, simulation, and lab courses.

title: Anesthesia Review I
description: This course provides a complete review of current anesthesia practice framed against the primary topic areas of the anesthesiologist assistant certification exam.  Through lectures, assigned readings, literature review, and student presentations, students are be provided a thorough update on issues and topics specific to the professional practice of the anesthesiologist assistant, including the principles of anesthesia, pathophysiology, technology and monitoring, pharmacology, and subspecialty areas of anesthesiology. Course assignments, unit quizzes and full-length practice exams will provide students a culminating review and practice of material likely to be addressed on the national certifying exam.

title: Clinical Anesthesia V
description: Clinical anesthesia experience utilizes a practicum approach to provide students with the opportunity to apply the principles of anesthesia to direct patient care. Through one-on-one supervised instruction students develop skills and foundations for perioperative anesthesia case management in operating room and ancillary anesthetizing locations. Expectations and responsibilities increase throughout each semester as students’ knowledge and skills develop within didactic, simulation, and lab courses.

title: Anesthesia Review II
description: This course provides a complete review of current anesthesia practice framed against the primary topic areas of the anesthesiologist assistant certification exam.  Through lectures, assigned readings, literature review, and student presentations, students are provided a thorough update on issues and topics specific to the professional practice of the anesthesiologist assistant, including the principles of anesthesia, pathophysiology, technology and monitoring, pharmacology, and subspecialty areas of anesthesiology. Course assignments, unit quizzes and full-length practice exams will provide students a culminating review and practice of material likely to be addressed on the national certifying exam.

title: Senior Seminar I
description: This course is designed to prepare students for their future roles within the anesthesiologist assistant profession. Course material will focus on transitioning from student to anesthetist roles, leadership and education opportunities for the professional, and enforce the life-long learning needed within medical professions. Students will gain communication skills in giving and receiving feedback by examining the language of anesthesia non-technical skills, the role of emotional intelligence at work, what our personal DiSC profile tells us about our communication and how our learning preference informs educational tendencies in the clinical environment. Students will also examine the role of conflict styles and how to respond to microaggressions.

title: Clinical Anesthesia VI
description: Clinical anesthesia experience utilizes a practicum approach to provide students with the opportunity to apply the principles of anesthesia to direct patient care. Through one-on-one supervised instruction students develop skills and foundations for perioperative anesthesia case management in operating room and ancillary anesthetizing locations. Expectations and responsibilities increase throughout each semester as students’ knowledge and skills develop within didactic, simulation, and lab courses.

title: Anesthesia Review III
description: This course provides a complete review of current anesthesia practice framed against the primary topic areas of the anesthesiologist assistant certification exam.  Through lectures, assigned readings, literature review, and student presentations, students are provided a thorough update on issues and topics specific to the professional practice of the anesthesiologist assistant, including the principles of anesthesia, pathophysiology, technology and monitoring, pharmacology, and subspecialty areas of anesthesiology. Course assignments, unit quizzes and full-length practice exams will provide students a culminating review and practice of material likely to be addressed on the national certifying exam.

title: Senior Seminar II
description: This course is designed to prepare students for their future roles within the anesthesiologist assistant profession. Course material will focus on transitioning from student to anesthetist roles, leadership and education opportunities for the professional and enforce the life-long learning needed within medical professions. This course will focus on information for profession licensure, student loan payback, financial literacy after graduation, and educator skills to optimize, promote, and assess active student learning in the didactic and clinical environment. Students will learn how to create goals, objectives, and learning plans for both didactic learning and clinical experiences.

title: Arabic I
description: Introduction to Arabic for students with no prior exposure. Following this class, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Novice level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency scale. All five modes of communication are addressed (interpersonal, presentational speaking, presentational writing, interpretive reading, and interpretive listening). Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) and an Arabic dialect are taught using an integrated approach. Students will also develop their understanding of Arabic speaking cultures.

title: Arabic I Intensive
description: Following this class, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Novice High to Intermediate Low level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency scale. All five modes of communication are addressed (interpersonal, presentational speaking, presentational writing, interpretive reading, and interpretive listening). Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) and an Arabic dialect are taught using an integrated approach. Students will continue to develop their understanding of Arabic-speaking cultures.

title: Arabic II Intensive
description: Continuation of 1130. Following this class, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Intermediate level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency scale. All five modes of communication are addressed (interpersonal, presentational speaking, presentational writing, interpretive reading, and interpretive listening). Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) and an Arabic dialect are taught using an integrated approach. Students will continue to develop their understanding of Arabic-speaking cultures.

title: Intermediate Arabic I Intensive
description: Intensive Arabic language course that develops students' Arabic skills to the ACTFL Intermediate Mid level and expands their cultural knowledge of the Arab world.

title: Intermediate Arabic II Intensive
description: Intensive Arabic language course that develops students' Arabic skills to the ACTFL Intermediate High level and expands their cultural knowledge of the Arab world.

title: Advanced Arabic I
description: Improves functional use of Arabic language and culture. Class is for students with four years of Arabic study or for heritage speakers. Students will attend language laboratory to enhance their listening, comprehension, and pronunciation skills.

title: Advanced Arabic II
description: Improves functional use of Arabic language and culture. Class is for students with four semesters of Arabic study or for heritage speakers. Students will attend language laboratory to enhance their listening, comprehension, and pronunciation skills.

title: Arabic Study Abroad
description: An introduction to Arabic cultures and language through study abroad. Course locations vary according to course content.

title: Topics in Arabic Literature and Culture in Translation
description: This course involves the study of Arabic literature and culture in translation. No knowledge of Arabic is required.

title: Topics in Literature and Culture in Arabic
description: This course involves the study of Arabic literature and culture in the original language. Advanced abilities in Arabic are required.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Architectural Graphics
description: Introduction to manual and digital drawing as well as modeling techniques for architectural and interior design. Students will learn how to represent composition, form and space by orthographic drawing, paraline and perspective views, and freehand sketching. Three-dimensional model building techniques will also be introduced.

title: Introduction to Architecture
description: This course provides students the tools and vocabulary to analyze, interpret and discuss the built environment from the social, historical, perceptual and technical determinants. Students are introduced to elements, principles, and theories of architecture through their social, historical, and technical determinants. The course seeks to lay a foundation in architectural studies, including introducing students to fundamental vocabulary and concepts.

title: Design Fundamentals
description: Introduces fundamental principles and processes of two-, three-, and four-dimensional design. Design aesthetics, perception, technique, composition, evaluation of materials and methods, practicing design methodologies, exploring design principles and theories, and graphic authorship are explored through various types of assignments.

title: Building Materials and Methods
description: An examination of common building systems and subsystems. Topics include structural materials, thermal and moisture protection, roofing, glazing, finish systems, and equipment systems that are in common use within the industry.

title: Physics and Math for Designers
description: Physics and Math for Design is a lecture/demonstration course for students who need scientific and mathematical concepts for their future design work. The course aims at a largely descriptive and qualitative introduction of selected (based on the needs and objectives of the future career in the field of design) topics of math and physics. No previous knowledge of physics is assumed: practicing the introduced topics (solving/discussing/analyzing problems) is scheduled based on (already) known elementary math and on the introduced concepts of math and physics. Emphasis is given to conceptual understanding of chosen topics related to future professional work areas with numerical application where it is important and useful. Topics as follows: Elements of Statistics, Elements of Geometry (in one- (x), two- (x, y), and three- (x, y, z → u, v, normal, dimensions), Elements of Trigonometry, Vectors, Statics, Kinematics and Dynamics (including Loads), Energy, Elements of Thermodynamics, Waves and Acoustics plus selected topics of geology/astronomy like Earthquakes or the Greenhouse Effect, are covered in the one semester introductory course. The International Units System and the fundamental rules related to the mathematical and physical formulas and units will be introduced at the beginning of the semester.

title: Technical Documentation with Auto CAD I
description: An introduction to basic computer-aided drafting concepts using AutoCAD. Students will learn drafting techniques that are in common use within the industry. Emphasis is placed on drawing setup, creating and modifying geometry, storing and retrieving files, placing, rotating, and scaling objects, adding text and dimensions, using layers, creating blocks, and interpreting and constructing plan, elevation, and section views.

title: Sketching Workshop
description: An introduction to sketching strategies and techniques using various media through instructor demonstration, experimentation, and coaching. Students first meet in the studio and then meet on location to practice sketching and review and assess together while enjoying the sights and sounds of Santa Fe. Students plan and prepare for a group gallery show /exhibit as the last meeting.

title: Introduction to Environmental Planning
description: Development of the major issues, concepts and methods emerging from the relationship of social systems and the natural environment.

title: Physics and Math for Designers
description: An introductory hands-on course. Assignments will help students understand and apply scientific and mathematical concepts in their design work. Topics include motion; stress and moment, structural loads, energy, heat flow, acoustics, descriptive geometry, and statistics.

title: Architectural Visualization I
description: Introduction to the history, theory, and emerging practices of architectural drawing. Students will learn technical skills and workflows for fabrication design software to analyze, critique, augment, and produce architectural drawings. This course will cover strategies for prescribing drawing conventions and modes of representation, including measured drawings, diagrams, orthographic views, perspectives, image-making, and rendering.

title: Architectural Visualization II
description: Students will advance on technical skills and workflows obtained in Architectural Visualization I to observe, document, transform and create multi-scalar ideas into irresistible visual expressions. This course will cover architectural drawing conventions of plan, section, elevation, axonometric projection, perspective construction, diagramming, mapping and imaging to effectively communicate design ideas through visual means.

title: World Architecture I
description: Lecture survey of the architectural and urban traditions of world cultures from prehistory to the Enlightenment.

title: World Architecture II
description: A survey of modern architectural and urban traditions from 1700 to the present.

title: Design Thinking
description: Lecture course introduces the theory and practice of Design Thinking. Students will explore frameworks and strategies for solving problems and develop an understanding of the disciplines that are influenced by them.

title: Architectural Design I
description: This course introduces fundamental principles and processes of architectural design. Students will explore topics such as design aesthetics, perception, technique, composition, evaluation of materials and methods, design methodologies, design principles and theories, and graphic authorship. Students will be exposed to interconnected architectural concepts of process, organizational strategies, and analysis of material methodology, while critically utilizing abstract and practiced graphical architectural conventions.

title: Architectural Design II
description: This Architectural Design Studio introduces principles of urban design and planning through an exploration of a series of multi-scaled architectural projects that examine public goals and constraints, urban infrastructure and fabric, sustainability, historical and socio-cultural issues.

title: Sustainability I
description: Lectures present the framework for creative analysis, including systems thinking and synergistic integration of the three pillars of sustainability, environments, equity, and economy, and their relationship to building systems.

title: Architectural Design III
description:
Studio investigates issues of private space and ownership emphasizing spatial, programmatic, typological and tectonic architectural issues. Small to medium scale projects range from domestic spaces to institutional or commercial environments. 
Prerequisite: 201 and 202 with minimum grades of "C+". 
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture.

title: Architectural Design IV
description:
Studio investigates the development of multi-family housing on an urban site. Students analyze contemporary housing precedents and programming, and address urban contexts, housing types and universal design. 
Prerequisite: 301 with minimum grade of "C+".  
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture.

title: Architecture and Context
description:
A combined lecture and lab course that introduces historical and contemporary concepts of architectural context, and develops verbal and graphic skills for analyzing architecture as a practice of place-making.
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture.

title: Architectural Theory
description: This course is a survey of architectural thought in the 20th and 21st centuries. Students will be required to read, discuss, and write about architectural ideas, and connect them to contemporary architecture.

title: Construction I
description:
Lab and lectures-introduction of technological aspects of building design and construction. 
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture.

title: Architectural Structures I
description:
Lectures present principles of mechanics, equilibrium conditions, properties of structural materials, structural properties of areas, shear and moment, flexural stresses, shearing stresses, deflection, trusses and funicular structures. 
Prerequisite: 202. 
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture or B.S.C.M. Construction Management.

title: Architectural Structures II
description:
Lectures present structural form and behavior, deflected shapes, approximate and simplified methods of analysis, graphic analysis, trusses cables and arches, simple beams, columns, continuous structures, three-dimensional structures, structural design issues. 
Prerequisite: 332.
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture or B.S.C.M. Construction Management.

title: Architectural Design V
description:
Studio investigates urban theories and strategies focusing on civic buildings, urban design, landscape and infrastructural responses and architectural form. Projects are multiple buildings and urban forms in complex urban settings. 
Prerequisite: 2125 and (302 with minimum grade of "C+").
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture.

title: Architectural Design VI
description:
Studio explores contemporary architectural topics. Topics will vary. At least one section will engage community clients and may be offered in conjunction with Landscape Architecture and Community and Regional Planning students. 
Prerequisite: 2125.
Restriction: admitted to B.A.A. Architecture.

title: Sustainability II
description:
Explores the application of theoretical foundations of sustainability to building and landscape design including concepts of passive solar design, daylighting, water conservation, and green building materials and construction methods. Introduces environmental rating systems such as LEED. 
Prerequisite: 233.

title: Design Leadership
description: Lecture/seminar course explores the historical and contemporary relationships between political, cultural and economic theories that affect and are affected by built environments, along with the design movements and practices that result.

title: Problems
description:
Students wishing to undertake a special study project must have instructor approval. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Seminar
description:
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study. Individually listed topics vary each semester. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Design Visualization
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Topics course. Credits will vary according to content offered.

title: Graduate Architectural Design I
description:
Studio introducing architectural design. Investigation of landscape, site and social contexts, private space, programmatic and tectonic issues. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Graduate Architectural Design II
description:
Projects emphasize the design of mid-size buildings with complex programming, and the spaces they compose in the urban realm. Students are introduced to designing within historical and socio-cultural contexts, sustainable design, and universal design. 
Prerequisite: 500.
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Graduate Architectural Design III
description:
Projects emphasize the design of civic buildings, addressing theories and precedents in urban or rural sites, and developing knowledge and deployment of building tectonics and construction materiality. 
Prerequisite: 501. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Graduate Architectural Visualization I
description: Introduction to the history, theory, and emerging practices of architectural drawing. Students will learn technical workflows for 1D, 2D, 3D, and fabrication design software to analyze, critique, augment, and produce architectural drawings.

title: Graduate Architectural Visualization II
description: Students will advance on technical skills and workflows obtained in Architectural Visualization I to observe, document, transform and create multi-scalar ideas into irresistible visual expressions. This course will cover architectural drawing conventions of plan, section, elevation, axonometric projection, perspective construction, diagramming, and processes that transition graphic visualization into fabrication to communicate design ideas through visual means.

title: World Architecture I: History of the Built Environment From Prehistory to 1800 CE
description: Lecture survey of the architectural and urban traditions of world cultures from prehistory to the Enlightenment.

title: World Architecture II: History of the Built Environment from 1800 CE to the Present
description:
(Also offered as ARTH 324) 
Survey of the architectural and urban traditions of the modern world from the Enlightenment to the present.

title: Graduate Construction I
description:
Lab and lectures, introduction of technological aspects of building design and construction. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Architectural Structures I
description:
Lectures present principles of mechanics, equilibrium conditions, properties of structural materials, structural properties of areas, shear and moment, flexural stresses, shearing stresses, deflection, trusses and funicular structures. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Architectural Structures II
description:
Lectures present structural form and behavior, deflected shapes, approximate and simplified methods of analysis, graphic analysis, trusses cables and arches, simple beams, columns, continuous structures, three-dimensional structures, structural design issues. 
Prerequisite: 532.

title: Introduction to Visualization
description:
Seminar/lab introduces issues of architectural representation with an emphasis on digital visualization, representation and modeling as it relates to design process and presentation. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Topics in Design Visualization
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Topics course. Credits will vary according to content offered.

title: Project-Thesis Preparation
description:
Foundational independent study where thesis or final project dimensions are explored, program or project principles established and search for available and manageable information completed. Feasibility of proceeding with the project is decided with faculty advisor(s). Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Restriction: permission of Director.

title: Master's Project
description: Development of an advanced architectural project based on research and program developed in ARCH 596. Once initiated, continuous enrollment is required (excluding summer) until project is approved by faculty committee. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. Prerequisite: 572 and 596. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Development of a research project reflective of advanced inquiry into an architectural topic. Plan I only. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Prerequisite: 596 and (602 or 603).  Restriction: permission of Director.

title: Masters Architectural Design I
description:
Studio investigates building tectonics, structure, technical documentation, sustainability and construction. The medium size designs will initiate investigations into net-zero building strategies, while developing a theoretical framework for contemporary integrated design. 
Prerequisite: 502.
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Masters Architectural Design II
description:
Studio explores contemporary architectural topics. Topics will vary. At least one section will typically engage community stakeholders and may be offered in conjunction with Landscape Architecture and/or Community and Regional Planning students. 
Prerequisite: 601.
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Masters Architectural Design III
description:
Studio developing an integrated design project that must be clearly resolved and highly articulated. Students must integrate building systems and content from prior coursework. Projects should demonstrate excellence in critical thinking craft, and visual presentation. 
Prerequisite: 602.  
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Masters Architectural Design IV
description:
Studio developing an integrated design project. Projects must be clearly resolved and highly articulated. Students should integrate building systems content from prior course work. Projects should demonstrate excellence in critical thinking, craft and visual presentation. 
Prerequisite: 603.  
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Research Methodology
description:
Lecture/seminar studies principles and types of design research. Approaches to refining a research question and developing a research strategy will be introduced. 
Prerequisite: 502. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Architectural Analysis
description:
Seminar introduces the range of categories and contexts specific to the production and representation of architecture. Open only to students in the 3 1/2 year program. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Architectural Theory
description: This course is a survey of architectural thought in the 20th and 21st centuries. Students will be required to read, discuss, and write about architectural ideas, and connect them to contemporary architecture.

title: Sustainability II
description:
Explores the application of theoretical foundations of sustainability to building and landscape design including concepts of passive solar design, daylighting, water conservation, and green building materials and construction methods. Introduces environmental rating systems such as LEED. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Systems Integration I
description:
Lecture course examines aesthetic stances and technical approaches to the integration of building systems. Emphasis is placed on structure, material selection and construction detailing. 
Prerequisite: 533.
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Systems Integration II
description:
Lecture course presents a comprehensive and integrative process to examine the interrelationship of the properties of materials, building envelope, environmental systems, and construction technology, as they influence design decision making. 
Prerequisite: 634.
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Professional Practice
description:
An overview of practice including the architectural profession, licensure, and ethics; the organization and management of the professional office; contracts; and the fundamentals of project management and delivery.
Prerequisite: 602.

title: Pre-Design and Architectural Programming
description:
Seminar explores components necessary to prepare a comprehensive architectural program that includes assessing client/user needs and site conditions, determining space requirements, identifying relevant codes, standards, sustainability requirements, and estimating pre-design project costs. 
Restriction: admitted to M.Arch. Architecture.

title: Problems
description:
Students wishing to undertake a special study project must have instructor approval. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Seminar
description:
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.
Individually listed topics vary each semester. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Master's Project or Thesis Documentation and Dissemination
description:
Documentation and dissemination of Master's Project or Master's Thesis. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Prerequisite: 597 or 599.

title: Introduction to Anthropology
description: Anthropology is the systematic study of the humanity both past and present. The course introduces students to the four subfields of anthropology, which include archaeology, biological, linguistic and cultural anthropology. Students will learn about the concepts and methods that anthropologists use to study our species and gain a broader perspective on the human experience.

title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology
description:
This course provides a basic introduction to the broad field of biological anthropology. The research interests of biological anthropologists include the history and development of modern evolutionary biology, molecular and population genetics, modern primates, the primate and human fossil record, and modern human biological diversity.
Biological Anthropology concentration students are required, and others are encouraged, to enroll concurrently in 1135L.

title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology Laboratory
description:
This laboratory course expand on the topics covered in lecture course and uses scientific methods and principles to examine evidence for the process of evolution, the nature of heredity, human evolutionary history and family tree relationships, primate ecology and behavior, and modern human diversity. Hands-on experience with fossil and skeletal material will be an important part of the learning process. 
Recommended, but not required, that this be taken concurrently with 1135. Two hours lab.

title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
description: This is an introductory course that provides an overview of cultural anthropology as a subfield within the broader discipline of anthropology and as a research approach within the social sciences more generally. The course presents core concepts and methods of cultural anthropology that are used to understand the ways in which human beings organize and experience their lives through distinctive cultural practices. More specifically, this course explores social and cultural differences and similarities around the world through a variety of topics such as: language and communication, economics, ways of making a living, marriage and family, kinship and descent, race, ethnicity, political organization, supernatural beliefs, sex and gender, and globalization. This course ultimately aims to present a broad range of perspectives and practices of various cultural groups from across the globe.

title: Introduction to Linguistic Anthropology
description: This is an introductory course which provides an overview of the discipline of Linguistic Anthropology. The course will discuss the implications of language within anthropology, as well as within the sciences and social sciences more generally. The course explores the core concepts and methods of linguistic anthropology, such as the basic structure of language, first and second language acquisition, bilingualism, and social and regional variations that are used to help students understand what it means to be human and the role of language in human societies.

title: Human Life
description: Biology and behavior of the human life course, including the evolution of the life history patterns specific to humans and the impact of population growth and of adaptation to local conditions in promoting human diversity. Students are encouraged, but not required, to enroll concurrently in 1170L.

title: Archaeology in Practice
description: This class explores different ways of studying and interpreting the past through a survey of archaeology’s historical, theoretical, and methodological development. If you are interested in archaeology as a career (or even just trying out an archaeological field school), this course is critical: it will provide you with the basic tools for conducting archaeological research and with an understanding of the background of the discipline. For this reason, this course is required for Anthropology majors with an Archaeology concentration. Even if you know you don’t want to be an archaeologist, though, this class may interest you. Have you ever wondered how archaeologists know what they know about the past? Are you skeptical about some archaeological claims? Have you ever read a statement about the past and thought, “is this really true?” This class will provide you with the skills, both conceptual and practical, to answer those questions.

title: Archaeology in Practice Laboratory
description: This course introduces students to a variety of archaeological field and laboratory methods.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Human Biology: Evolution, Life History, and Health
description: This course explores the core principles of human biology from an integrated evolutionary framework, including perspectives from anatomy and physiology, ethnology, primatology, paleoanthropology, ecology, neuroscience, evolutionary medicine, and more.

title: World Archaeology
description: Archaeology is the systematic study of the human past through material remains. This course introduces students to the physical remains of past societies and compares and contrasts archaeological development in different regions. Students will explore the dynamics of the human past and its influences on contemporary society.

title: Forensic Anthropology
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the forensic investigation of death. Emphasis will be on current methods and techniques and include the role of the anthropologist as an integral member of the investigation process.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Language and Culture
description:
(Also offered as CJ 319; LING 359) 
Examination of the interrelations of language and speech with other selected aspects of culture and cognition.  
Prerequisite: 114 or 1155 or LING 2110 or LING 301.

title: Strategy of Archaeology
Southwest Archaeology
description: A survey of past archaeological cultures across the Southwestern United States and Northwestern Mexico beginning with the initial occupation of humans in the region but focusing on the period from 500 to 2500 years ago. This course provides students with an overview of broad patterns in subsistence, technology, sociopolitical organization, and ritual practice. An emphasis is placed on major culture areas and sites. May include fieldtrips and hands-on activities.

title: South American Archaeology
description: Archaeology of South America from the Paleo-Indian to the European colonial period. Emphasizes the origins and evolution of Andean civilization and associated interpretive problems.

title: Archaeology of Prehistoric Europe
description: An overview of the prehistory of Europe from its initial settlement through the first farming societies.

title: African Prehistory
Near Eastern Archaeology
description: A survey of the Near Eastern culture area from the origins of agriculture to the development of Bronze Age civilization. {Offered periodically}

title: Principles of Cultural Anthropology
description: Development of ideas and theories in sociocultural anthropology; focus on topics such as integration of human societies, sources of change in economic and cultural systems.

title: Indigenous Peoples of South America
description: Culture and history of indigenous peoples of South America. Selected examples from lowland and highland regions.

title: Ritual Symbols and Behavior
description: Ethnographic studies and a variety of anthropological approaches to ritual are read to examine the defining characteristics of ritual activity and its contemporary significance in peoples' lives. {Offered periodically}

title: Colombia In War and Peace: Race, Class, and Gender at the Epicenter of Conflict and Peacemaking
description: This class contextualizes issues of complex social violence in Colombia by both a broad attention to Colombian history, society and culture, and by literatures that address various kinds of identity formation and dynamism.

title: Environmental Conflict: Anthropological Perspectives
description: By looking at the cultural perspectives of diverse groups alongside ethnographies of environmental conflicts, this course examines the complexities of nature and culture on a rapidly changing planet Earth.

title: Topics in Cultural Anthropology
description: Current topics in sociocultural anthropology to be explored in experimental courses.

title: Settler Colonialism in Palestine in the 21st Century
description: Our goals are to better understand the lived realities on the ground in Palestine, and to develop skills of critical analysis to better understand colonialism, decolonization, and indigeneity in across the globe.

title: Human Genetics and Genomics
Anthropology of the Skeleton
description: A laboratory course in the identification of human skeletal materials with attention to problems in the evolution of primates. Three lectures, 2 hours lab. {Fall}

title: Human Evolution
description: Events and processes in the emergence and evolution of the human lineage-from the origins of Australopithecus, through the emergence of the genus Homo, to the evolution of early modern humans-based on the human fossil record. Offered every other spring.

title: Human Behavioral Ecology
description:
Introduces students to the fundamental principles of evolutionary theory and their application to human behavior.  It surveys current research on human sexuality, mate choice, reproduction and parenting from the perspective of human evolutionary ecology.  
Prerequisite: 1170 or 1135 or BIOL 1110.

title: Primate Social Behavior
description:
Special emphasis will be on strategies of survival, reproduction, mating and rearing, in the complex social systems of apes and monkeys. The costs and benefits of alternative strategies are used to understand individual life histories.
Restriction: upper division standing.

title: Pre-Columbian Cultures of Ancient Mexico
description:
Archaeological survey of the cultures of ancient Mexico from earliest inhabitants to the period of the Spanish Conquest. This course explores environmental, social, and political aspects of the rise and fall of societies across Mexico.

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Archaeology Field Session
description: Intensive instruction in archaeological field and laboratory techniques and the opportunity for independent student research. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Maya Prehistory and Archaeology
description:
Surveys the development of the Maya civilizations in Mesoamerica from the origins of agriculture through the Spanish Conquest. The course will explore archaeological, ethno-historical, linguistic, and environmental data and accounts.  
Prerequisite: 1115 or (1211 and 1211L) or 2175 or 320.

title: The Anthropology of Heritage
description: This class explores ethical issues and debates surrounding heritage-making practices and discourses through lens of ethnological, archaeological, and evolutionary anthropology. It problematizes the boundaries between different constructions of the past and present.

title: Human Evolutionary Physiology and Anatomy
description: How and why do human bodies work the way they do? This course explores human physiology and anatomy from an integrated evolutionary framework, including perspectives from comparative biology, paleoanthropology, biomechanics, embryology, exercise physiology, and more.

title: Paleoindians: Colonizing the Americas
description:
Presents and interprets the earliest archeology of North America from the terminal Pleistocene through the early Holocene including relevant archeological evidence from eastern Eurasia and South America. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Field and Laboratory Research
description:
Directed study under the supervision of a faculty member. 
Restriction: permission of instructor. {Offered upon demand}

title: Native American Art I
description: (Also offered as ARTH 402/502) Archaeological and historic art forms of the Arctic Northwest coast and the eastern woodlands of North America. {Fall}

title: Native American Art II
description: (Also offered as ARTH 406/506) Archaeological and historic art forms of the Plains, Southwest and western regions of North America. {Spring}

title: Topics in Linguistic Anthropology
description: Topics from various areas of anthropological linguistics including, but not limited to, ethnosemantics, the ethnography of communication and the biology of language.

title: Topics in Archaeology
description: Topics of archaeological interest including gender in archaeology, European contact and post-processualism.

title: Interdisciplinary Museum Science
description:
(Also offered as ANTH 544, BIOL 444/544, EPS 444/544, GEOG 444/544, MSST 444/544.) Interdisciplinary overview of how science museum resources are used to study change in human communities, natural, and physical systems over space and time. Course activities emphasize materials science, data science, and team science.
Restriction: instructor permission.

title: The Anthropology of Music and Sound
description: (Also offered as MUS 448/548) The cultural study of music and sound. Course materials are drawn from written and audio music ethnographies of contemporary indigenous, diasporic, refugee, exile, and industrial communities.

title: Topics in Biological Anthropology
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Bioarcheology
description:
The analysis of the skeletal remains from past human populations, oriented at the mortality, morbidity and genetic affinities of those extinct populations.  
Prerequisite: *351L. {Alternate years}

title: Primate Evolution
description: This seminar reviews issues in primate taxonomy, functional and behavioral reconstructions, phylogenetic relationships, and macroevolutionary patterns. The intent of this course is to put primates into a broader evolutionary perspective.

title: Paleoecology Lab
description: This course explores reconstruction of paleoecosystems, climates, and ecologies. Students receive laboratory training in techniques including dental microwear and stable isotope analyses. We also examine paleoecological studies through readings of recent literature.

title: Human Paleopathology
description: Ancient disease through the study of normal and abnormal bone remodeling processes and dental conditions. Population health evaluated by descriptive and radiologic analyses of human remains. Prerequisite: *351L. {Alternate years}

title: Anthropological Genetics
description:
This course examines theory, data and methods used by genetic anthropologists to address questions about human origins and prehistory, race, natural selection, disease, and the social and scientific implications of research in genetic anthropology. 
Prerequisite: 1135 or BIOL 1110 or BIOL 1140 or BIOL 2110C or BIOL 2410C.

title: Paleoanthropology
description: Events and processes leading from the appearance of the human lineage to the beginnings of agriculture, with discussions of Australopithecus and the genus Homo, through Homo sapiens. Prerequisite: *351L. {Alternate years}

title: Dental Anthropology
description:
A laboratory class that explores the application of data from human and non-human dentition to anthropological questions. Topics covered include dental anatomy, development, evolution, variation, and pathology. 
Prerequisite: 1135.

title: Human Behavioral Evolution
description:
Behavioral transitions throughout human evolution, including social systems, diet, life history, intelligence and locomotion. Focus on hominid origins, the transition from ape-like to human-like hominid, and the origin of our own species. 
Prerequisite: 357.

title: Analyzing Archaeological Collections
description: A hands-on introduction to analyzing archaeological collections. This class covers basic analysis methods used in archaeological laboratory settings. Students will gain a wide range of practical analytical skills and will conduct an independent research project on an archaeological assemblage.

title: Ceramic Analysis
description:
Basic concepts, methods and approaches used in the analysis of archaeological pottery. Lectures cover concepts and strategies. Labs give practical experience with techniques of analysis.
{Spring}

title: Geoarchaeology
description: Application of geological concepts to archaeological site formation with emphasis on pre-ceramic prehistory of the southwestern United States. Quaternary dating methods, paleoenvironment, landscape evolution, depositional environments. Quaternary stratigraphy, soil genesis, sourcing of lithic materials, site formation processes. Required field trip.

title: Zooarchaeology
description: Basic concepts, methods and approaches in the analysis of animal remains from archaeological sites. Lectures cover history, theory and current applications of zooarchaeology. Labs provide practical experience in zooarchaeological identification and analysis.

title: Seminar in Museum Methods
description:
(Also offered as ARTH, MSST 485) Theoretical and practical work in specific museum problems. May be repeated as subject matter changes. 
Prerequisite: ARTH 407 or MSST 407. {Offered upon demand}

title: Practicum: Museum Methods
description:
(Also offered as ARTH, MSST 486/586) Practicum in museum methods and management.  
Prerequisite: ARTH 407 or MSST 407. Restriction: permission of instructor. {Offered upon demand}

title: Population Genetics
description: (Also offered as BIOL 491/591) This course investigates how genetic variation is patterned within and between populations, and how these patterns change over time. Topics include neutral theory, population structure, phylogenetics, coalescent theory, the molecular clock, and laboratory methods.

title: Individual Study

Readings and discussions concerning anthropological research methods, sources, goals and professional ethics. Open to upper division majors and concentrators whose applications for the honors program have been approved.

title: Field Research

Field research for qualified advanced undergraduate or graduate students with previous experience in archaeology, biological anthropology, human evolutionary ecology, linguistics or general ethnology. Problems are selected on the basis of student-faculty interest and field research opportunities.  
Restriction: permission of instructor. {Offered upon demand}

title: Native American Art I
description: (Also offered as ARTH 502/402) Archaeological and historic art forms of the Arctic Northwest coast and the eastern woodlands of North America. {Fall}

Students will be exposed to cutting edge isotope-focused interdisciplinary research in a lecturer, discussion and constructive feedback setting.

description: The seminar introduces the field of social semiotics as developed in contemporary linguistic anthropology, introduces concepts in general linguistics, and develops a social semiotic perspective on the role of communication in constructing and transforming society.

title: Lithic Analysis
description:
Examines how flaked stone artifacts can be studied and contribute to archaeological research. Includes such topics as raw material procurement; manufacturing technology; tool morphology, function, and style; debitage; and theoretical frameworks. 
Restriction: admitted to Anthropology graduate program.

title: Quantitative Analysis in Archaeology
description: Application of quantitative methods to archaeological questions and data. Lectures cover statistical tests common in archaeological analysis and their proper use. Labs provide hands-on experience in quantitative data analysis.

description: Archaeology of South America from the Paleo-Indian to the European colonial period. Emphasizes the origins and evolution of Andean civilization and associated interpretive problems.

Varying interdisciplinary topics taught by collaborative scientists from UNM, SFI, and LANL.
