description: This course will provide students a mechanistic understanding of modern drug action in the human body. The course is designed to aid particularly those students on healthcare-related curriculum paths. Fulfills the CMP breadth requirement.

title: Virology
description: Structure, properties and molecular biology of viruses; virus-host interactions, viral replication,, pathology, epidemiology, classification, and vaccine development..  Fulfills the CMP requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Microbial Ecology
description:
Role of microorganisms in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems. Emphasis on biogeochemistry and nutrient cycling. Three lectures. 
Prerequisite: 304 and 304L.

title: Microbiomes
description: Peer into the exciting world of microbiome research and dive deep into how microorganisms shape nearly every aspect of daily life for animals. Fulfills the CMP breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Ethology: Animal Behavior
description: A survey of behavior patterns in animals, with emphasis on adaptive significance. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the BS/BA Biology degree.

title: Immunology
description: Immunoglobulin structure, antigen-antibody reactions, immunity and hypersensitivity; experimental approach will be emphasized. Three lectures. Fulfills the CMP requirement of the BS/BA Biology degree.

title: Diversity and Evolution of Animal Sexual Strategies
description: The course surveys the diversity of strategies in animal sexual systems and examines the evolutionary derivation of those strategies. The influence of varying sexual strategies on animal behavior, morphology, diversity and other attributes is examined.

title: Introduction to Tropical Biology
description: Marine and terrestrial tropical environments, primarily in the Caribbean; topics stressed may include organisms, communities, structure, function, distribution, geology, history, politics, ecology and others. Two lectures, 2 hours lab, one-week field trip to the Caribbean and field trip fee is required. Open to majors and/or non-majors. Fulfills the INT breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Emerging Model Organisms
description: Recent genomics advances have expanded biology to include more unusual model organisms. The new technologies that facilitate studying emerging models and the discoveries made from them will be explored. Fulfills the CMP breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Flora of New Mexico
description: Identification, classification, nomenclature, and geography of vascular seed plants in New Mexico. Survey of adaptations and evolutionary trends in plants of the Southwest. Field trips. Three lectures, 3 hours lab. Fulfills EEO breadth requirement.

title: General Botany
description: Overview of plant anatomy, physiology, classification, evolution and ecology. Covers both higher and lower plants. Two lectures, 4 hours lab. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Plants and People
description: An overview of the past, current, and future ways humans use plants and the biological reasons for these different uses. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement.

title: Plant Physiological Ecology
description: Interaction of plants with their environment, covering plant water relations, carbon gain and utilization and soil mineral nutrition. Common research methodologies will be demonstrated in class.  Fulfills the INT breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Community Ecology
description: This course provides detailed content on the organization, functioning, and development of ecological theory on community ecology, along with common methods to survey communities, and analyze and interpret community ecology data.. Field trip to Sevilleta LTER required. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement for the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Ecology of the Past
description: Understanding how natural ecosystems are responding to pressing environmental issues is a major challenge in contemporary science. Yet, many of these events have an analog in Earth history. Paleoecology can provide unique insights into these events. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Forest Ecology
description: This course will cover forest ecology topics ranging from individual organisms to ecosystems. You will learn about how disturbances, climate, and humans influence forest structure and function. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Conservation Biology
description: Importance of biological diversity from ecological, aesthetic, economic and political viewpoints. Extinction as a past, present and future process, and the roles of genetics, levels of biological organization, reserves and laws in the protection and recovery of endangered organisms. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Global Change Biology
description:
An advanced ecology course that gives students a broad overview of biological responses to global change at multiple levels of organization, and the scientific approaches used to study these responses. 
Prerequisite: 304 and 304L.

title: Parasitology
description: Parasite biology (protozoa, Platyhelminthes, Nematoda, Arthropoda), discovery, diagnosis, health impact, (post-)genomics findings. Lab covers lifecycles, morphological/molecular diagnosis, field collection. Two 1h15m lectures, one 3h lab. Fulfills CMP breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Discovering Arthropods
description: Introduction to the non-insect arthropods (millipedes, centipedes, arachnids, crustaceans and their relatives), their diversity and natural history.

title: Biology of Fungi
description: Systematics, reproduction and ecology of fungi. Biology of economically and medically important fungi, and the roles of introduced fungi in shaping human history and natural environments. Fulfills the CMP breadth requirement.

title: Entomology
description: Classification, phylogeny, natural history and literature of insects. Three lectures, 3 hours lab.

title: Ornithology
description:
Classification phylogeny, natural history and literature of birds. Field trips required. Three lectures, 3 hours lab.
Prerequisite: 304 and 304L.

title: Ichthyology
description:
Classification, phylogeny, natural history and literature of fishes. All-day field trips and one or more overnight field trips required. Three lectures, 3 hours lab. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement.

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Herpetology
description:
Classification, phylogeny, natural history and literature of reptiles and amphibians. All-day field trips and one or more overnight field trips required. Two lectures, 6 hours lab. 
Prerequisite: 386L.

title: Mammalogy
description:
Conservation, phylogeny, natural history, and biogeography of mammals. All-day field trips and one or more overnight field trips required. Three lectures, 3 hours lab. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.. 

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Biology of Infectious Organisms
description:
The full spectrum of infectious entities including prions, viruses and parasitic prokaryotes and eukaryotes will be discussed with respect to their transmissibility, interactions with immune systems and their influences on evolutionary processes and biodiversity issues.

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Population Genetics
description: (Also offered as ANTH 491/591) This course investigates how genetic variation is patterned within and between populations, and how these patterns change over time. Topics include neutral theory, population structure, phylogenetics, coalescent theory, the molecular clock, and laboratory methods.

title: Introductory Mathematical Biology
description: Application of mathematics to models of biological systems, from genes to communities. Emphasis placed on model development, qualitative solution techniques and numerical simulation. Laboratory exercises introduce students to the R programming language. Fulfills the INT breadth requirement.

title: Computational Genetics & Evolution
description: The course covers population and complex trait genetics with a focus on theory, evolutionary processes, modeling, and computational approaches, including statistical analysis of polygenic traits. Fulfills the EEO breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Biogeography
description: Geographical distributions of organisms: patterns and their ecological and historical causes.

title: Limnology
description: Limnology is the study of inland waters and has, as its central focus, the structure and function of lakes and rivers.  Freshwater ecology topics include lake and river formation, light and heat, hydrology, water chemistry, organismal diversity, and food webs. Fulfills the INT breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree.

title: Principles of Gene Expression
description: A detailed and critical study of how different genes are regulated during the life of an organism, principally at the level of transcription.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
A maximum of 2 credit hours may be applied toward the Biology major. Not accepted toward the Biology minor. 
Restriction: junior or senior standing and permission of instructor.

title: New Graduate Student Seminar
description:
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Topics in Biology
description:
A maximum of 4 credit hours may be applied toward the Biology major.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Immunology Discussion
description: Discussion group in which papers from the contemporary immunological literature are analyzed and critiqued.

title: Topics in Physiology
description: Continually changing selection of topics.

title: Topics in Organismal Biology
description: Continually changing selection of topics.

title: Bosque Internship
description: UNM students train as interns with the Bosque Ecosystem Monitoring program to learn bosque ecology and mentor K-12 students and teachers in monthly data collection at field sites along the Rio Grande. Study includes ecosystem dynamics, data use, and environmental education components. Weekly on- and off-campus meetings (K-12 interaction limited in summer session).

title: Topics in Ecology-Evolution
description: Continually changing selection of topics.

title: Macroecology
description: A large-scale statistical approach to study the abundance, distribution and diversity of organisms.

title: Data Science for Biology
description: In this course, students will learn how to examine and analyze the data, assert confidence in their parameter estimates, predict future data, and present the results, while mastering the R software.

title: Basic Graduate Ecology
description: Major themes in current ecological research, with in-depth exploration of the theoretical and empirical literature of individual, population, community, ecosystem and landscape ecology. Recommended for all Biology Department graduate students in any field of ecology, evolution and behavior. Three lectures, 1.5 hours lab/discussion.

title: Topics in Interdisciplinary Science
description: Continually changing selections of topics.

title: Topics in Interdisciplinary Biological and Biomedical Sciences
description:
(Also offered as ANTH 620, CS 520, ECE 620, STAT 520) 
Varying interdisciplinary topics taught by collaborative scientists from UNM, SFI, and LANL.

title: Astrobiology the Science and Speculations About Life in the Universe.
description: Astrobiology studies the origins, evolution, detection, distribution, and future of life in the universe. We will also explore how we define life, the limitations of life based on the natural laws of the universe, and the  implications of discovering life from microbes to advanced civilizations.

title: Symbiosis: The evolution of organismal interactions
description:
7/19/11 abdivett: Changed status to "S", deactivation period
expired.

title: Department of Biology Seminar
description: This course provides engagement between UNM students and faculty with the wider scientific community. Each week a different scientist is invited by UNM Biology faculty or students to present their research to the department. Each seminar concludes with a short audience question period.

title: Department of Biology Brown Bag Research Seminar
description: This course provides engagement between UNM biology graduate students, UNM biology majors, and the wider UNM biology community. Graduate students in the Department of Biology present their research each week.

title: Comparative Animal Physiology: A Survey of Animal Function
description:
The function of organ systems in animals, emphasizing neuromuscular, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal and renal physiology. Fulfills the CMP breadth requirement of the Biology BS/BA degree. 

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Fantastic Fungi
description: This combined lecture/laboratory course explores the fundamentals of fungal biology, identification, isolation, and use of DNA techniques to refining mushroom identification and exploring evolutionary relationships.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Advanced Field Biology
description: Approval of Committee on Studies required.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introduction to Comparative Global and Ethnic Studies
description: The course explores historical and contemporary social forces that impact ethnic communities across the Americas. Students will examine social and economic dynamics of Indigenous, Latino, Asian-Pacific, Africana communities and women’s experiences in these societies.

title: Chicana-o Latina-o Musical Cultures and Expressions
description: The course explores diverse musical expressions of Chicana/o and Latina/o peoples in the present geographical boundaries of the United States. The course emphasizes the intercultural dynamics in the formation of Chicano and Latino music, which incorporates American, Latin American, African, Native American, and European roots.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Introduction to Chicana and Chicano Studies
description: Introductory survey of the Mexican American experience in the United States, with special reference to New Mexico. Exploration of historical, political, social, and cultural dimensions.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Introduction to Chemical and Biological Engineering
description: An introduction to the professions of chemical engineering and biological engineering; current research in these fields; career choices; guidance and advice on curricular matters and effective study techniques for chemical and biological engineering students.

title: Addressing Societal Challenges using the Tools of Chemical and Biological Engineering
description: An introduction to how chemical and biological engineers use mathematics and physics to solve sociotechnical problems; guidance on effective study techniques for chemical and biological engineering students.

title: Laboratory Electronics for Nuclear, Chemical and Biological Engineers
description: (Also offered as NE 213) Basic DC and AC circuits including capacitors and inductors and their applications in radiation measurement equipment and chemical process parameter measurements. Oscilloscopes, Op Amps, and Sensors and their use in the CBE and NE laboratories. {Spring}

title: Chemical Process Calculations
description:
Extensive problem work in material and energy balances for steady state processes. Students will utilize physical properties, chemistry and computer skills to obtain solutions. Detailed examination of case studies demonstrating the fundamentals of process analysis.  
Prerequisite: (CHEM 1225 or CHEM 1227) and CHEM 1225L and PHYS 1310.

title: Quantitative Processes in Bioengineering
description: This course introduces bioengineering problem solving through conservation laws of mass, energy, charge, and momentum in biological systems. Students develop quantitative analysis skills, explore physiological applications, address ethics, and complete a design challenge integrating core engineering principles with biomedical contexts.

title: Chemical and Biological Engineering Computing
description:
Introductory computer solutions to chemical engineering problems using MATLAB and ASPEN. Topics covered will include thermodynamic equations, transport problems, material-energy balances, staged operations, and reaction engineering. 
Prerequisite: 251. {Spring}

title: Computing for Bioengineering
description: This course introduces Python programming for bioengineering, emphasizing data analysis, visualization, and computational modeling. Students develop core coding skills, apply numerical methods to biomedical problems, and complete a capstone project integrating programming with real-world research applications in bioengineering.

title: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
Introduction to Transport Phenomena
description:
The mechanisms and the related mathematical analysis of momentum and heat transport in both the molecular and turbulent regimes. Similarities and differences between transport types and the prediction of transport properties. 
Prerequisite: 253 and MATH **316.
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Fall}

title: Unit Operations
description:
A study of the unit operations involved with momentum and heat transfer. Focus will be on the basics of equipment design and how to synthesize a process from the basic units. Includes extensive use of computer techniques and design exercises. 
Prerequisite: 311.
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Spring}

title: Numerical Methods for Chemical and Biological Engineering
description:
MATLAB application of numerical techniques to the solution of chemical engineering problems such as reaction engineering and transport phenomena. Included are linear/nonlinear equations; numerical integration/differentiation; regression and interpolation; ordinary differential equations; optimization.  
Prerequisite: 253 and MATH **316. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Fall}

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory I : Introduction to Experimentation
description:
Integrated lecture and laboratory. Introducing students to experimentation in Chemical Engineering. Topics include laboratory safety, experimental planning, data acquisition, sources of experimental error and uncertainty, introductory statistics, and writing reports and preparing technical presentations.
Prerequisite: 253 and 302. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory II
description:
Laboratory experiments in fluids and heat transfer. Students will apply concepts of error analysis and use computational fluid dynamics software for interpretation of experimental data. 
Prerequisite: 311. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Spring}

title: Bioengineering Laboratory I: Introduction to Experimentation
description: This laboratory introduces students to essential bioengineering techniques, including pipetting, aseptic cell culture, scaffold fabrication, tissue decellularization, and hydrogel preparation, as well as introducing them to scaffold/biomaterials characterization techniques. Students gain practical skills, apply experimental design and analysis, and present findings, preparing for advanced laboratory research and professional bioengineering practice.

title: Mass Transfer
description:
Continuation of 311. The mechanisms and the related mathematical analysis of mass transport in both molecular and turbulent regimes. Similarities and differences among mass, momentum and heat transport. Prediction of mass transport properties. Design of separation systems based on mass transfer. 
Prerequisite: 253 and 311. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Spring}

title: Bioengineering Laboratory II
description: This laboratory immerses students in advanced 3D bioengineering techniques, including spheroid and organoid culture, co-culture systems, and hydrogel embedding. Students gain hands-on experience, apply quantitative analyses, and explore translational applications in disease modeling, regenerative medicine, and drug discovery while addressing ethical considerations.

title: Bioengineering Instrumentation
description: This course surveys state-of-the-art instrumentation used in bioengineering, from biomolecular synthesis and separations to advanced imaging, spectroscopy, and computational tools. Students gain knowledge of operating principles, hands-on skills, and workflow design for analyzing, producing, and translating bioengineered systems in research and clinical contexts.

title: Introduction to Materials Engineering
description:
This course develops an understanding of materials from a molecular viewpoint. The structure, properties, and processing of metals, ceramics, polymers, and nanostructured materials are treated in an integrated fashion. Applications include nanotechnology, and biology.
Prerequisite: 302 and 311 and 317.
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Spring}

title: Heterogeneous Catalysis Seminar
description:
Discussion of current research in heterogeneous catalysis and materials characterization. Students learn to read the literature critically and to present reviews of ongoing research.
Restriction: admitted to School of Engineering.

title: Nanomaterials Seminar
description:
Investigate, evaluate, and discuss current frontier topics in sol-gel synthesis of nanostructured materials through a series of presentations.
Restriction: admitted to School of Engineering.

title: Bioengineering Seminar
description:
Emerging bioengineering concepts and applications with emphasis on materials and device technologies.
Restriction: admitted to School of Engineering.

title: Characterization Methods for Nanostructures
description:
(Also offered as CHEM 469/569; NSMS 512) 
Nanostructure characterization methods. Examine principles underlying techniques and limitations, and how to interpret data from each method: electron beam, scanning probe, x-ray, neutron scattering, optical and near field optical. Lab demonstrations and projects provide experience.

title: Applied Biology for Biomedical Engineers
description:
(Also offered as BME 517) 
Emphasis on engineering principles and analysis of: (i) the cell as a complete system, including cellular subsystems, structures and functions; and (ii) select higher order systems of human physiology.
Prerequisite: BIOL 2110C.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory III
description:
Laboratory experiments in mass transfer and unit operations. Students will plan experiments to study the operation of process equipment such as heat exchanger, distillation columns, etc. Fundamental experiments on mass transfer are also included. 
Prerequisite: 312 and 321. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Fall}

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory IV
description:
Laboratory experiments in kinetics and process control. Students will also do an in-depth project in their chosen chemical engineering concentration. 
Prerequisite: 454 and **461.
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Spring}

title: Bioengineering Lab III
description: This laboratory introduces modern biosynthesis and separations techniques through modules in plasmid construction, protein expression and purification, and functional assays. Students gain hands-on experience with DNA design, bacterial culture, chromatography, and protein analysis while developing data analysis, troubleshooting, and scientific communication skills.

title: Bioengineering Laboratory IV
description: This advanced laboratory focuses on the design, fabrication, and evaluation of natural and synthetic biomaterials. Students synthesize drug-delivery microspheres, test biocompatibility, characterize ceramics and composites, and apply 3D bioprinting to hydrogel scaffolds, integrating experimental, analytical, and communication skills essential for biomaterials research and applications.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Senior year. Presentation on safety, ethics,  opportunities and impact within the discipline. Effective technical communication and other basic skills and resources needed for a successful career in chemical and biological engineering.

title: Process Dynamics and Control
description:
Design and analysis of feedback control systems in chemical and biological systems. Topics include formulation of dynamic models, time and Laplace domain analysis of open- and closed-loop systems, design of single variable and multivariable controllers.
Prerequisite: 317. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Fall}

title: Chemical Reactor Engineering
description:
Elementary principles of chemical reactor design and operation utilizing the kinetics of homogeneous and heterogeneous-catalytic reactions. 
Prerequisite: 311 and 317. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Fall}

title: Biomaterials Engineering
description: (Also offered as BME 572) Introduction to biomaterials currently in use, including commercial and research applications. Includes an understanding of a material's properties, biological responses to the materials, clinical context of their use, manufacturing processes, and regulatory issues. Restriction: permission of the instructor. {Fall, odd years}

title: Electrochemical Engineering
description:
Introduction of the principles of elecrochemistry and their applications in materials characterization, corrosion, electro-plating and etching. The course builds on electrochemical kinetics and discusses the design of sensors, batteries and fuel cells. 
Prerequisite: 302. 
Restriction: admitted to School of Engineering.

title: Tissue Engineering
Introduction to Statistics and Design of Experiments
description:
This course will introduce computational tools and statistical methods important for chemical engineering practice, including fundamental concepts of statistics, numerical statistical analysis applications and methods for the design of experiments. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.

title: Undergraduate Research
description:
Advanced studies in various areas of chemical and biological engineering. Restriction: permission of instructor. 
{Summer, Fall, Spring}

title: Chemical Engineering Design
description:
Principles and practices of chemical engineering design, including process flow sheets, equipment design and specification, process modeling and simulation, economic analysis, and hazard analysis. In-depth design of at least one commercial-scale chemical process. 
Prerequisite: 253 and 302 and 312 and 321. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Fall}

title: Advanced Chemical Engineering Design
description:
Continued practice in creative chemical engineering design, including safety, health and environmental issues. Detailed project on a major open-ended process design or research problem. 
Prerequisite: 493L. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ch.E. program.
{Spring}

title: Chemical and Biological Engineering Honors Problems I
Chemical and Biological Engineering Honors Problems II
Selected Topics
description: A course which permits various faculty members to present detailed examinations of developing sciences and technologies in a classroom setting. Restriction: admitted to School of Engineering.  {Offered upon demand}

title: Chemical and Biological Engineering Seminar
description: Colloquia, special lectures and individual study in areas of current research. A maximum of 3 credits can be applied toward degree. {Fall, Spring}

title: Chemical and Biological Engineering Research Practices
description: Students will work on developing research proposals for their masters or doctoral degree. The course will involve oral presentations of proposals and journal article critiques. {Fall}

title: Heterogeneous Catalysis Seminar
description: Discussion of current research in heterogeneous catalysis and materials characterization. Students learn to read the literature critically and to present reviews of ongoing research.

title: Nanomaterials Seminar
description: Investigate, evaluate, and discuss current frontier topics in sol-gel synthesis of nanostructured materials through a series of presentations.

title: Bioengineering Seminar
description: Emerging bioengineering concepts and applications with emphasis on materials and device technologies.

title: Characterization Methods for Nanostructures
description:
(Also offered as CHEM 469/569; NSMS 512)
 Nanostructure characterization methods. Examine principles underlying techniques and limitations, and how to interpret data from each method: electron beam, scanning probe, x-ray, neutron scattering, optical and near field optical. Lab demonstrations and projects provide experience. {Fall}

title: Special Topics
description:
A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.
{Offered upon demand}

title: Applied Biology for Biomedical Engineers
description:
(Also offered as BME 517) Emphasis on engineering principles and analysis of: (i) the cell as a complete system, including cellular subsystems, structures and functions; and (ii) select higher order systems of human physiology.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Transport Phenomena I
description: Equations of change applied to momentum, energy and mass transfer. Analogies between these phenomena and their limitations. Transport dependent on two independent variables, unsteady state problems.

title: Methods of Analysis in Chemical, Biological, and Nuclear Engineering
description: (Also offered as NE 525) Mathematical methods of analysis for chemical, biological, and nuclear engineers. Topics will include vector calculus, curvilinear coordinate systems, linear algebra, and others. Emphasis will be on the use of MATLAB to solve engineering problems. {Fall}

title: Surface and Interfacial Phenomena
description: Introduces various intermolecular interactions in solutions and in colloidal systems; colloidal systems; surfaces; interparticle interactions; polymer-coated surfaces; polymers in solution, viscosity in thin liquid films; surfac-tant self-assembly; and surfactants in surfaces.

title: Advanced Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Advanced thermodynamics with reference to its application in chemical engineering.

title: Problems
description: Advanced study, design or research either on an individual or small group basis with an instructor. Recent topics have included convective diffusion, reactor safety, inertial confinement fusion and nuclear waste management.

title: Problems
description: Advanced study, design or research either on an individual or small group basis with an instructor. Recent topics have included convective diffusion, reactor safety, inertial confinement fusion and nuclear waste management.

title: Kinetics of Chemical Processes
description: Rate equations for simple and complex chemical processes, both homogeneous and heterogeneous. Experimental methods and interpretation of kinetic data for use in chemical reactor design and analysis. Applications to complex industrial problems. {Spring}

title: Biomaterials Engineering
description:
(Also offered as BME 572) Introduction to biomaterials currently in use, including commercial and research applications. Includes an understanding of a material's properties, biological responses to the materials, clinical context of their use, manufacturing processes, and regulatory issues.
Restriction: permission of the instructor. {Fall, odd years}

title: Selected Topics in Material Science
description:
A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.
May be counted an unlimited number of times toward degree, with departmental approval, since content varies. Credit is determined based on the content of the course. {Offered upon demand}

title: Selected Topics in Aerosol Science
description: Analysis of the motion of both charged and neutral aerosol particles; molecular and convective diffusion, particle size and classification, coagulation, precipitation and particle capture, current aerosol research and instrumentation. {Offered upon demand}

title: Electrochemical Engineering
description: Introduction of the principles of elecrochemistry and their applications in materials characterization, corrosion, electro-plating and etching. The course builds on electrochemical kinetics and discusses the design of sensors, batteries and fuel cells.

title: Introduction to Statistics and Design of Experiments
description:
This course will introduce computational tools and statistical methods important for chemical engineering practice, including fundamental concepts of statistics, numerical statistical analysis applications and methods for the design of experiments. 
{Fall}

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
See Graduate Programs section for total credit requirements. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Curanderismo: The Art of Traditional Mexican Healing
description: This course will explore how our ancestors used traditional healing methods to treat and help diminish various ailments, shaped our cultural diversity of yesterday and today, and what the future of this medicine might be.

title: Curanderismo Traditional Medicine without Borders
description: Examines the history of Curanderismo (the art of Mexican traditional Healing) in the Southwest and Mexico. Students will explore an integrative approach to medicine that incorporate Curanderismo with various traditional and holistic health techniques.

title: Global Curanderismo
description: This course will take an approach in exploring how our ancestors used traditional healing methods in their everyday lives to cure and help diminish various ailments. Additionally, this course will look on how our ancestors shaped our cultural diversity.

title: Immigration and Assimilation
description: This is a course on the historical, political and sociological dynamics that shaped the Chicana/o experience in America. A main focus will be on immigration history and the "assimilation" process, especially Mexican immigration.

title: Introduction to Mexican Folklorico Dance.
description: This course introduces the student to Folklorico dance from Mexico and how it is embedded in cultural significance. Students will learn the fundamentals of this practice which may include footwork, postures, and dances particular to cultural regions in Mexico.

title: Transnational Latina Feminisms
description: This course examines transnational feminist theories, methodologies, and praxis. Students explore how globalization affects Latin American women in the western hemisphere and the possibilities that decolonizing struggles hold for social justice and human rights.

title: Introduction to Chicana Studies
description: This course is an introduction to the interdisciplinary field of Chicana Studies. Includes historical and contemporary research on labor, political involvement, cultural studies and feminism.

title: Chicana Feminisms
description: Explores the history and development of Chicana Feminisms with special attention to how Chicana feminists voice their concerns and politics on a wide range of social dynamics that includes race, class, gender, sexuality, and language.

title: Chicana and Chicano Families
description: This course provides an overview of the formations, definitions and transformations of Chicana and Chicano families. This course focuses on critically analyzing changing gender roles, contrasting parent roles and obligations, transnational parenthood, adolescent experiences and social influences within the familial context.

title: Race, Culture, Gender, Class in New Mexico History
description: Hispano and Native perspectives of NM history begin with colonialism, military history, politics, economics, but must also consider culture, gender and class to understand the resilience of people as actors in their own history.

title: History of Chicana and Chicano Peoples
description: This course surveys Chicana/o History from the Pre-Columbian period to the present. Students will analyze community formation, experiences in the U.S., and civil rights advocacy.

title: Literatura y Cultura Afromexicana
description:
This course explores the literature and culture of Mexico in regards to Mexican intellectuals that have delineated the presence and invisibility of Afro-Mexicans and the contributions of Afro-Mexicans.
Prerequisite: Spanish 301 or Spanish placement exam demonstrating a 300-level equivalent competency.

title: Chicano Latino Civil Rights
description: The seminar examines Chicano Civil Rights by exploring forms of collective social action on behalf of immigration rights/reform, education rights/reform, labor rights, treaty rights, legal justice, environmental justice, veteran's rights, and political representation.

title: Chicana and Chicano Movement: El Movimiento Chicano
description: The course examines the Chicana/o Movement beyond the 1960s Civil Rights era, exploring the precursors to the political movement, as well as the legacies of the Chicana/o movement and its effect on society and academia.

title: Raza Genders and Sexualities
description: The course focuses on critical thinking about race, class, gender, and sexuality. Course discussions will center on questions of identity and representation and the social construction of gender and sexuality in transnational Latina/o communities.

title: Chicana and Chicano Cultural Studies
description: An exploration of a variety of contemporary forms of Chicana and Chicano cultural production and the ways in which these construct and transform individual experiences, everyday life, social relations and power.

title: New Mexico Villages and Cultural Landscapes
description: The course explores New Mexico's cultural heritage through an examination of cultural narratives and cultural landscapes and traditions, such as plazas, salas, resolanas, matanzas, and acequia culture from the past through the present day.

title: New Mexico's Literary Landscapes and Beyond
description: The course explores Chicano/a letters and the spoken word tradition in New Mexico and beyond. The course examines poetic traditions through a variety of forms including poetry, storytelling, singing/songwriting, and spoken word artistry.

title: Chicanx and Latinx Science Fiction
description: This course involves students in the study of Chicanx & Latinx science fiction, a field that draws on scholarship from both Latinx Studies and Science Fiction Studies.

title: Mysteries of New Mexico and Beyond An Introduction to Critical Mystery Studies
description: This course is an introduction to the field of “Critical Mystery Studies” (CMS) with an emphasis on New Mexican mysteries. We examine the meaning of these mysteries and the discourses surrounding them in their specific geographical and cultural contexts.

title: Community-Based Learning in Chicana and Chicano Studies
description: This course offers students the opportunity to engage in community-based learning at a selected Community-Based Organization site of their choice. The course broadens student knowledge and understanding of global and local economic and social realities.

title: Topics in Chicano Hispano Mexicano Studies
description: Special topics in Chicano Hispano Mexicano Studies are interdisciplinary in nature and draw from the Humanities, Social Sciences, and the Arts. May be repeated as subject matter varies.

title: Introduction to Mexican Folklorico Dance.
description: This course introduces the student to Folklorico dance from Mexico and how it is embedded in cultural significance. Students will learn the fundamentals of this practice which may include footwork, postures, and dances particular to cultural regions in Mexico.

title: Mexican Life and Culture
description: This interdisciplinary course explores Mexican culture as a social construct. Student examine Mexican history from the pre-colonial era to the present and analyze racial and social identities, Indigeneity, regionalism, economic development, and immigration as expressed religion, art, film, literature, music.

title: Chicanas and Chicanos in Education
description: This course provides an overview of Chicana/o educational experiences with the United States school system. Students will analyze historical and institutional developments that shape educational experiences of Chicanas/os through texts, theories, and methodologies.

title: Chicanos and Latinos in a Global Society
description: The course examines current theories and debates about globalization from a critical perspective. An emphasis will be placed on the study of Chicano and Latino communities in the U.S. as influenced by globalization.

title: Presencia Africana en Mexico
description: This course explores the African presence in Mexico in four periods: the Colonial Period, the Independence movement, the Modern Period, and representations of people of African descent in contemporary Mexico.

title: Community-Based Archaeology and Oral History of Indo-Hispano New Mexico
description: This is an intensive 2-week course organized around a unique set of collaborations between scholars, students, and the Indigenous and Hispano communities of the Río Embudo Watershed in northern New Mexico. Participants gain formal training in archaeological field methods as well as in the methods of oral history, while helping build new understandings of the complex Indo-Hispano history of the region.

title: Seminar: Querencia
description: We will examine the ideas of place and home and how Chicano/a and Native American writers have addressed it in their work. Northern New Mexico cultural envoy, Juan Estevan Arellano, used the concept of querencia to define the relationship between place and identity.

title: Acequia: Water, Land, Culture
description: With irrigation methodologies derived from Middle Eastern, Spanish, Mediterranean, and Indigenous peoples, acequias continue to function in the manner established in New Mexico. This class will examine the acequia cultural ecosystem and its unique traditions and practices.

title: New Approaches in Chicana and Chicano Studies
description: The course focuses on examining the various theories and methods utilized by Chicana/o scholars during the evolution of Chicana/o Studies. The class also presents theories and methods that inform writings in Chicana/o studies.

title: Writers in the Community
description: This course is designed to place UNM writing (and non-writing) students into diverse community settings to work alongside students of all ages, needs, interests. Writing workshops will be offered in schools, community centers, justice settings, homeless-shelters, healthcare facilities, non-profit organizations.

title: Advanced Seminar in Chicana and Chicano Studies
description:
Advanced seminar emphasizing synthesis of previous courses, research skills, theories and service learning in Chicana and Chicano Studies. Designed as a capstone seminar for the undergraduate program.
Restriction: junior or senior standing.

title: Special Topics
description: The content of this course varies by semester but will provide an in-depth analysis of special topics related to Chicana and Chicano Studies. For course content, consult the Schedule of Classes.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Cine Chicano y Mundial
description: This course explores film depictions of Chicana/o society in relation to diverse cultures of the world and their cosmovision as expressed in films. Course themes include gender, race, politics and immigration.

description: This course engages students in exploring how discourse, rhetoric, and imagery are used in addressing key humanities thematics and topics in the field.

title: Research Methods and Data Analysis
description: This course prepares students to survey a range of qualitative and quantitative approaches, the utility of different approaches depending on theoretical perspective, and the debates in and outside the field.

title: Seminar: Chicana and Chicano Perspectives on Border and Immigration Issues
description: This course offers Chicana and Chicano perspectives of their experience of the U.S.-Mexico borderlands, in which we will contextualize immigration patterns, attitudes, and policies while reviewing the broader political economy of the U.S. from the early colonial period through the twenty-first century.

title: Chicanos and Latinos in a Global Society
description: The course examines current theories and debates about globalization from a critical perspective. An emphasis will be placed on the study of Chicano and Latino communities in the U.S. as influenced by globalization.

description: Advanced seminar emphasizing synthesis of previous courses, research skills, theories and service learning in Chicana and Chicano Studies. Designed as a capstone seminar for the graduate program.

description: The content of this course varies by semester but will provide an in-depth analysis of special topics related to Chicana and Chicano Studies. For course content, consult the Schedule of Classes.

title: Individual Study
description: Provides graduate students with the opportunity to work one-on-one with an instructor to facilitate an intensive study of a subject or the completion of a project.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Teaching Practicum in Chicana and Chicano Studies
description: The teaching pedagogy course in Chicana and Chicano Studies offers graduate students in the department professional development support to strengthen the teaching of lower-division courses, including observation, mentoring, participation in classroom teaching, and planning, implementing, and assessing the teaching and learning in a lower-division Chicana and Chicano Studies course.

title: Practicum
description: Intended to support community-engaged learning and/or project-based learning in the departmental graduate program, and to enhance academic knowledge and provide students with opportunities to gain practical experience in a chosen community based or professional field.

title: Seminar: Critical Thought in Chicana and Chicano Studies
description: Students will explore how discourse and critical analysis have developed since the inception of the field of Chicana and Chicano Studies until today. Students will analyze and discuss Chicana/o criticism and theoretical texts through class discussion and weekly theoretical papers. Additionally, students will construct final research paper students in which they utilize terminology, discourse, and rhetoric by Chicana and Chicano scholars to generate further discussion on critical thought by Chicana and Chicanos.

title: Presencia Africana en Mexico
description:
This course explores the African presence in Mexico in four periods: the Colonial Period, the Independence movement, the Modern Period, and representations of people of African descent in contemporary Mexico.
Prerequisite: Spanish 401 or Spanish placement exam demonstrating a 400 nivel equivalence competency.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Mandarin Chinese I
description:
This is the first semester of a two-semester sequence in first year modern standard Chinese (“Mandarin”). This course is recommended for students who have had little or no experience in the Chinese language. A beginning Mandarin Chinese course is designed to introduce the Mandarin sound system (“pinyin”), basic vocabulary, Chinese characters (either in Simplified or Traditional characters), and basic grammatical concepts and structures. In order to help beginners develop their communicative competence in the four basic skills, the 5Cs (Communication, Cultures, Comparisons, Connections, and Communities) will be integrated consistently into the content and exercises in the course.

Credit for both this course and CHIN 1130 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Mandarin Chinese II
description:
This is the second semester of a two-semester sequence in first year modern standard Chinese (“Mandarin”). This course is designed for students who have taken first semester Mandarin Chinese, and focuses on enhancing pronunciation and expanding the vocabulary and grammar dealing with daily activities. In order to help beginners develop their communicative competence in the four basic skills, the 5Cs (Communication, Cultures, Comparisons, Connections, and Communities) will be integrated consistently into the content and exercises in the course.

Credit for both this course and CHIN 1140 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Mandarin Chinese I Intensive
description:
This is the first semester of a two-semester sequence in first year modern standard Chinese (“Mandarin”). This course is recommended for students who have had little or no experience in the Chinese language. The first year intensive Mandarin Chinese course is designed to introduce the Mandarin sound system (“pinyin”), basic vocabulary, and Mandarin Chinese characters (Simplified), and basic grammatical concepts and structures. In order to help beginners develop their communicative competence in the four basic skills, the 5Cs (Communication, Cultures, Comparisons, Connections, and Communities) will be integrated consistently into the content and exercises in the course. 

Credit for both this course and CHIN 1110 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Mandarin Chinese II Intensive
description:
This is the second semester of a two-semester sequence in first year modern standard Chinese (“Mandarin”). This course is designed for students who have taken first semester intensive Mandarin Chinese, and focuses on enhancing pronunciation and expanding the vocabulary and grammar dealing with daily activities. In order to help beginners develop their communicative competence in the four basic skills, the 5Cs (Communication, Cultures, Comparisons, Connections, and Communities) will be integrated consistently into the content and exercises in the course.

Credit for both this course and CHIN 1120 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Mandarin Chinese III
description: An integrated language course to develop oral and written proficiency to Intermediate-low level and initial cultural competency.

title: Mandarin Chinese IV
description: Follows prerequisite to further develop oral and written proficiency up to Intermediate-mid level and initial cultural competency.

title: Chinese Calligraphy
description:
This course will include lectures about the history of Chinese calligraphy, the tools used in calligraphy, the five major writing scripts/styles, and strategies to effectively memorize and write Chinese characters. A major part of class time will be dedicated to actual calligraphy writing practice. Student will be practicing and mastering the basic writing techniques of the Standard script/style.
Prerequisite: 1140.

title: Third Year Chinese I
description:
An integrated language course to develop oral and written proficiency up to Intermediate-high level and cultural competency. 
Prerequisite: 2120.

title: Third Year Chinese II
description:
Follows 301 to further develop oral and written proficiency up to advanced low-level and cultural competency.
Prerequisite: 301.

title: Intermediate Chinese Conversation
description: Designed for students who have completed three semesters of college-level study of Chinese or the equivalent. Students further develop their spoken language competency, enrich their vocabulary, and improve their cross-cultural skills.

title: Business and Media Chinese
description: Business and Media Chinese is an upper-division Chinese course. Learners will study vocabulary used in business settings, develop media literacy, improve speaking and writing for professional purposes, and develop cultural competency for professional and business purposes in Chinese-speaking regions.

title: Study Abroad
description:
When students take a semester studying abroad in Chinese-speaking regions (Mainland China, Hongkong, Taiwan, etc.) approved by GEO, they can transfer up to 6 credits back to UNM under this course number. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature and Culture in Translation
description: (Also offered as COMP, ENGL 343) This course surveys Chinese literature and culture from the origins of Chinese civilization to the present, with a focus on the continuities and discontinuities between traditional and modern China.

title: Topics in Chinese Film
description: Study of Chinese history, life, and culture through an examination of films produced in Hong Kong, Taiwan, and mainland China.

title: China Today: Advanced Readings in Chinese
description: This is an advanced Chinese language course. It introduces various aspects of contemporary Chinese society and culture to students through authentic Chinese reading materials. Prerequisite: 302 or 305.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Preparation for College Chemistry
description: A preparatory course for students who feel they are not prepared for CHEM 1215/1215L or CHEM 1120C. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Foundations of Chemistry
description: A unique preparatory course for CHEM 1215 and your subsequent chemistry courses. It has a dual purpose; firstly, to help you obtain a solid foundation in the chemical concepts that are essential to your future chemistry and science classes, and secondly to optimize your set of learning skills to help you learn more efficiently, demonstrate your knowledge and succeed in your future fast-paced high-level science courses.

title: Introduction to Chemistry Lecture and Laboratory for Non-Majors
description:
This course covers qualitative and quantitative areas of non-organic general chemistry for non-science majors and some health professions. Students will learn and apply principles pertaining, but not limited to, atomic and molecular structure, the periodic table, acids and bases, mass relationships, and solutions. The laboratory component introduces students to techniques for obtaining and analyzing experimental observations pertaining to chemistry using diverse methods and equipment. Three lectures, 3 hours demo lab/recitation. 

Credit for both this course and CHEM 1215 may not be applied toward a degree program.
Credit for both this course and CHEM 1217 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: General Chemistry I for STEM Majors
description:
This course is intended to serve as an introduction to general chemistry for students enrolled in science, engineering, and certain pre-professional programs. Students will be introduced to several fundamental concepts, including mole, concentration, heat, atomic and molecular structure, periodicity, bonding, physical states, stoichiometry, and reactions.

Credit for both this course and CHEM 1217 may not be applied toward a degree program.
Credit for both this course and CHEM 1120C may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: General Chemistry I for STEM Majors Laboratory
description: General Chemistry I Laboratory for Science Majors is the first semester laboratory course designed to complement the theory and concepts presented in General Chemistry I lecture. The laboratory component will introduce students to techniques for obtaining and analyzing experimental observations pertaining to chemistry using diverse methods and equipment.

title: General Chemistry II for STEM Majors
description: This course is intended to serve as a continuation of general chemistry principles for students enrolled in science, engineering, and certain pre-professional programs. The course includes, but is not limited to a theoretical and quantitative coverage of solutions and their properties, kinetics, chemical equilibrium, acids and bases, entropy and free energy, electrochemistry, and nuclear chemistry. Additional topics may include (as time permits) organic, polymer, atmospheric, and biochemistry.

title: General Chemistry II for STEM Majors Laboratory
description: The second of a two-semester sequence of laboratory courses designed to complement the theory and concepts presented in lecture. The laboratory component will introduce students to techniques for obtaining and analyzing experimental observations pertaining to chemistry using diverse methods and equipment.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Integrated Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry
description:
This course is a one semester introduction to Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry designed for students in health and
environmental occupations. The course surveys organic compounds in terms of structure, physical, and chemical properties,
followed by coverage of the chemistry of specific classes of organic compounds in the biological environment. Students will
apply course concepts to everyday organic and biological chemistry problems in preparation for careers in health and
environmental fields.
Credit for both this course and CHEM **301 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Quantitative Analysis Lecture and Laboratory
description:
Quantitative analysis is a subdiscipline within analytical chemistry which deals with the identification and assay of a material or its components. Students will learn how chemical characterization involves chemical reactivity, physical measurement, and data interpretation with an emphasis on solution equilibria and electrochemistry. The study of precise and reliable chemical characterization is fundamental to further study and practice in chemistry, biology, medicine, geology, chemical engineering, and many other related fields. The understanding of the methods and limitations of chemical characterization can aid in making informed judgments on a large variety of social and political issues. This course is designed to introduce you to techniques of quantitative analysis and complement the theory and concepts presented in lecture. Students will obtain reproducible quantitative laboratory data using classical (volumetric, gravimetric) and simple instrumental (potentiometric, spectrophotometric, chromatographic) methods, as well as analyze and interpret laboratory data using standard statistical and validation approaches. Three lectures, 4 hours lab. 
Prerequisite: (1225 or 1227) and 1225L.

title: Organic Chemistry
description:
Chemistry of the compounds of carbon.
Credit for both this course and CHEM 2120 may not be applied toward a degree program.
Prerequisite: 1225 or 1227.

title: Organic Chemistry
description: Continuation of **301.  Prerequisite: **301. {Summer, Fall, Spring}

title: Organic Chemistry Laboratory
description:
Teaches basic organic chemistry laboratory techniques, including separations, thin-layer chromatography, gas chromatography, and set up of apparatus for organic synthesis. Three hours lab.  
Prerequisite: (1225 or 1227) and 1225L. 
Pre- or corequisite: **301.

title: Organic Chemistry Laboratory
description:
Teaches synthesis of organic compounds using reactions learned in CHEM **302 and spectroscopic characterization of the products. Three hours lab. 
Prerequisite: 303L. Pre- or corequisite: **302. {Summer, Fall, Spring}

title: Physical Chemistry
description:
Introduction to quantum chemistry that starts from the postulates of quantum mechanics and simple models and covers structure of polyatomic molecules. 
Prerequisite: (1225 or 1227) and 1225L and MATH 1522 and (PHYS 1240 or PHYS 1320).  
Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2531.

title: Physical Chemistry
description:
An introduction to chemical thermodynamics. Topics will include basic thermodynamic principles, phase diagrams, and solution phase thermodynamics. 
Prerequisite: (1225 or 1227) and 1225L and MATH 1522 and (PHYS 1240 or PHYS 1320).  
Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2531.

title: Introductory Physical Chemistry
description:
Fundamentals of physical chemistry with primary emphasis upon biological and biochemical applications.
Cannot be used for credit toward B.S. Chemistry.
Credit not allowed for (**311 or **312) and **315.
Prerequisite: (1225 or 1227) and 1225L and (MATH 1440 or MATH 1522) and (PHYS 1240 or PHYS 1320).

title: Special Topics for Undergraduates
description: Possible topics are: chemical literature, environmental chemistry, photochemistry, stereochemistry, macromolecules, C-13-NMR, natural products. Can be taken twice for a maximum of 6 credit hours. {Offered upon demand}

title: Special Topics for Undergraduates
description:
Possible topics are: chemical literature, environmental chemistry, photochemistry, stereochemistry, macromolecules, C-13-NMR, natural products.

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Chemical Biology Research Methods Seminar Series
description: This seminar will cover modern concepts in chemical biology research. UNM and outside faculty will present research projects with particular emphasis on making the projects accessible to undergraduate students.

title: Readings in Selected Topics
description: Advanced topics not covered in general offerings. {Offered upon demand}

title: Readings in Selected Topics
description: Advanced topics not covered in general offerings. Can be taken twice for a maximum of 6 credit hours. {Offered upon demand}

title: Optical Properties of Materials
description: This course will cover basic principles of light-matter interaction, modeling optical processes in material, microscopy, and spectroscopy.

title: Molecular Simulation
description: This course focuses on the art and science of computer simulations of physical, (bio)chemical, and materials systems. The topics include ab initio quantum chemical methods, density functional theory, molecular mechanics, molecular dynamics, Monte Carlo, free-energy simulations, and machine learning.

title: Laboratory Methods in Physical Chemistry
description:
Introduction to modern physical chemistry laboratory techniques, including computational chemistry, quantum mechanical description of molecular rotations, molecular vibrations and electronic absorption, and thermodynamics. One lecture, 6 hours lab.
Prerequisite: **311 and **312 and 453L. {Fall}

title: Biological Chemistry/Chemical Biology I
description: Brings the fundamentals of general and organic chemistry to bear on the complex array of structures and chemical processes that occur in living organisms.

title: Biological Chemistry / Chemical Biology II
description: The course will focus on organic chemistry of enzyme reaction and protein transport mechanisms, enzyme and protein transport kinetics, substrate to product conversion and ligand transport thermodynamics, and compositional logic of metabolic pathways.

title: Biological Chemistry and Chemical Biology Laboratory Methods
description: This course is ideal for students majoring in chemistry or chemical biology and planning to continue in a graduate and/or professional program. The course will focus on laboratory methods used in chemical biology.

title: Introduction to Synthetic Biology
description: This course provides an introduction to the field of synthetic biology. Topics covered will include current methods of DNA construct assembly, protein, biological pathway, and whole cell design.

title: Organic Chemistry of Biological Pathways
description:
Covers basic principles of mechanisms, acidity, stereochemistry; structures; properties of biomolecules; reactions in lipid, carbohydrate, amino acid, nucleotide metabolic pathways.  Three hours lecture.
Prerequisite: **302. {Fall, Spring}

title: Genome Technologies and Bioinformatics
description: This course covers the history of whole genome sequencing and the technologies that have enabled whole genome sequencing to become commonplace. Additionally, the basic bioinformatic tools for analyzing raw sequencing data are discussed.

title: Molecular Biophysics
description: Physical and theoretical methods for investigating biological molecules and machines including: physical measurements on single biological molecules, classical and quantum simulations of biomolecular structure/function, high-resolution microscopy and x-ray diffraction, optical and NMR spectroscopies applied to biomolecules.

title: Advanced Inorganic Chemistry
description: Atomic theory and molecular structure, the fundamentals of symmetry, point groups, bonding concepts, acid-based chemistry, periodic trends, and reaction chemistry of both transition metals and non-metals.  Prerequisite:  **311 or **315.  {Fall}.

title: Advanced Synthetic Chemistry Laboratory
description: Provides students with basic laboratory techniques in synthetic, structural, mechanistic, spectroscopic, and computational chemistry.

title: Bioinorganic Chemistry
description: This course in bioinorganic chemistry discusses modern concepts in in the field.

title: Analytical Instrumentation: Theory and Application
description:
This course introduces students to a variety of instrumentation used in chemical analysis. Content includes theory, design, statistical data analysis and application of primary instrumentation used in analytical labs. Labs and research project will be performed. 
Prerequisite: 2310C and (MATH 1430 or MATH 1512).

title: Environmental Chemistry
description:
Introduction to the chemistry of natural and polluted environments, including both atmospheric and aquatic systems.
Prerequisite: 2310C and **302.

title: Characterization Methods for Nanostructures
description:
(Also offered as CBE, NSMS 512)
 Nanostructure characterization methods. Examine principles underlying techniques and limitations, and how to interpret data from each method: electron beam, scanning probe, x-ray, neutron scattering, optical and near field optical. Lab demonstrations and projects provide experience.

title: Advanced Topics in Chemistry
description: Current topics requiring a background in physical chemistry such as spectroscopy, reaction mechanisms, advanced synthesis, polymer chemistry and materials chemistry. {Offered upon demand}

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
{Offered upon demand}

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
{Offered upon demand}

title: Senior Honors Research
description: Senior paper based on independent research. {Offered upon demand}

title: Senior Honors Research
description: Senior paper based on independent research. {Offered upon demand}

title: Chemistry Seminar-Research
description:
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Scientific Teaching in Chemistry
description: A course which includes lecture, seminar, workshop and field experience to provide a scientific framework for teaching chemistry and practice in strategies of scientific teaching.

title: Molecular Structure Theory
description: General introduction to quantum mechanics with emphasis on chemical applications. Topics covered include basic postulates of quantum mechanics, standard analytically solvable quantum systems (free electrons, particle in a box, harmonic oscillator, rigid rotor, hydrogen atom), approximation methods (perturbation theory and the variational method). An introduction to molecular quantum mechanics, molecular spectroscopy and time-dependent perturbation theory.

title: Introduction to Graduate Studies and Career Preparation: Safety and Responsible Conduct of Research
description: This course provides first-year graduate students in chemistry with fundamental skills necessary for success in their research and professional development. The course covers essential safety training for working in a laboratory, including chemical handling, waste disposal, and emergency procedures. Additionally, students will engage in discussions on the Responsible Conduct of Research (RCR) and scientific ethics, addressing topics such as data integrity, authorship, conflicts of interest, and ethical decision-making in research. This course is required for all first-year chemistry graduate students.

title: Optical Properties of Materials
description: This course will cover basic principles of light-matter interaction, modeling optical processes in material, microscopy, and spectroscopy.

title: Chemical Dynamics
description: A rapid review of chemical thermodynamics and kinetics. Usually for graduate students in areas outside of physical chemistry.

title: Molecular Simulation
description: This course focuses on the art and science of computer simulations of physical, (bio)chemical, and materials systems. The topics include ab initio quantum chemical methods, density functional theory, molecular mechanics, molecular dynamics, Monte Carlo, free-energy simulations, and machine learning.

title: Mechanisms in Organic Chemistry
description: An introduction to the methods used for determining reaction mechanisms in organic chemistry and the application of those methods for determining the mechanisms of reactions based on ionic processes.

title: Synthesis in Organic Chemistry
description: Development of strategies for synthesizing organic compounds including stereochemical control; introduction to advanced reactions for carbon-carbon bond formation and functional group manipulation.

title: Topics in Organic Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Organic Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Biological Chemistry/Chemical Biology I
description: Brings the fundamentals of general and organic chemistry to bear on the complex array of structures and chemical processes that occur in living organisms.

title: Introduction to Synthetic Biology
description: This course provides an introduction to the field of synthetic biology. Topics covered will include current methods of DNA construct assembly, protein, biological pathway, and whole cell design.

title: 3D Structure Determination of Macromolecules by Crystallography and Cryo-EM
description:
Structural Biology is a multidisciplinary field that combines techniques from biology, chemistry, and physics to understand the molecular architecture of biological macromolecules. This course provides an in-depth exploration of structural biology, focusing on the principles, techniques, and applications of structural determination.
There will be a comprehensive exploration of structural biology, encompassing various levels of protein structure from primary to quaternary, as well as the intricate three-dimensional structures of proteins in complex with ligands.

title: Genome Technologies and Bioinformatics
description: This course covers the history of whole genome sequencing and the technologies that have enabled whole genome sequencing to become commonplace. Additionally, the basic bioinformatic tools for analyzing raw sequencing data are discussed.

title: Synthesis and Mechanism in Inorganic Chemistry
description: A general outline of synthesis methodologies and approaches for main group element and transition metal compounds is provided. In addition, the reactivity of these compounds is explored with particular emphasis on systematics in reaction mechanisms.

title: Topics in Inorganic Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Inorganic Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Analytical Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Analytical Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Physical Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Characterization Methods for Nanostructures
description:
(Also offered as CBE, NSMS 512)
Nanostructure characterization methods. Examine principles underlying techniques and limitations, and how to interpret data from each method: electron beam, scanning probe, x-ray, neutron scattering, optical and near field optical. Lab demonstrations and projects provide experience. {Fall}

title: Introduction to Quantum Technology for Chemists and Engineers
description: This course introduces chemistry and engineering students to Quantum Technology, with an emphasis on the role of photonics, chemistry and engineering. Students will gain familiarity with the relevant mathematics, wave electromagnetics, materials systems, and concepts including qubits, coherence, and entanglement.

title: Principles and Platforms of Quantum Technology
description: Quantum Technology harnesses the unique features of quantum mechanical systems, such as superposition and entanglement, for devices that may revolutionize computing, communication, and sensing. This course provides a deep dive into several platforms used by practicing Quantum Technology researchers.

title: Advanced Topics in Biological Chemistry
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Research Colloquium
description: Presentation and discussion of current research by faculty from other institutions. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Chemistry Divisional Seminar
description: Student presentations and discussion of current research by students and faculty in the same traditional division of chemistry. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Research and Readings
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive paper or research proposal that integrates knowledge attained through coursework, research, and experience.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introduction to Civic and Community Engagement
description: This introductory course covers concepts for understanding civic engagement, including models of civic life through America's history, critiques of philanthropy, volunteerism, community service, public service, political activism, and public service leadership.

title: Topics in Global Service-Learning Leadership
description:
This pre-fieldwork course provides students a forum for critical reflection on community-driven service and is intended for any student undertaking study abroad, fieldwork, and community engagement in CELR 430.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Lobo Gardens Community-Based Research
description: This experiential learning course explores the intersections of gardening, sustainability and social change while engaging with local farmers and gardeners, planting and growing food, and cultivating the community gardens on campus.

title: Independent Study in Research Service-Learning
description:
This course engages undergraduate students in a community-based project under faculty guidance in an approved community setting and working toward goals established in collaboration with community partners. 
Restriction: junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.

title: Community Engaged Learning and Research Capstone Seminar
description:
Students will culminate their Community Engaged Learning and Research experience working with a community partner to design, implement and present and project that demonstrates academic merit in addressing a community need.
Prerequisite: 350 and 351.
Restriction: permission of department.

title: CDL A: Theory Training
description: Upon completion students should acquire their Class A CDL Permit by successfully passing their General Knowledge exam, Air Brakes exam, Combinations exam, as administered by the State of New Mexico.

title: Road Driving
description: This section is focused on the students developing the necessary skill to safely operate a Class A commercial vehicle on public roadways.

title: Yard and Range Skills
