Course activities emphasize materials science, data science, and team science.
Restriction: instructor permission.

description: Variable course content depending on student interest. Topics may include physical sedimentology, sequence stratigraphy, basin analysis, cycle stratigraphy and chemostratigraphy.

description: Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

Hydrologic and geologic factors controlling groundwater flow, including flow to wells. The hydrologic cycle; interactions between surface and subsurface hydrologic systems; regional flow systems. Groundwater geochemistry and contaminant transport.

title: Geomorphology and Surficial Geology
description: Origin and development of landforms with emphasis on weathering, soils, hillslope processes, fluvial systems and surficial geology; occasional field trips. Prerequisite: (101 and 105L) or (ENVS 101 and 102L).

title: Advanced Mineralogy
description: Crystallographic principles; structure, chemistry, physical properties of rock forming minerals.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Dissertation

Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Macroeconomic Principles
description: Macroeconomics is the study of national and global economies. Topics include output, unemployment and inflation; and how they are affected by financial systems, fiscal and monetary policies. Prerequisite for most upper-division courses.

title: Microeconomic Principles
description: This course will provide a broad overview of microeconomics. Microeconomics is the study of issues specific to households, firms, or industries with an emphasis on the role of markets. Topics discussed will include household and firm behavior, demand and supply, government intervention, market structures, and the efficient allocation of resources. Prerequisite for most upper division courses.

title: Personal Investing
description: This is an economics course with no prerequisites. Consequently it should be considered introductory, however it offers a wide-ranging overview of personal finance and the role financial assets can play in achieving personal financial goals. This course won’t make you a millionaire, nor will it prepare you for a career as a hedge-fund manager. The goal is to increase your understanding of how financial markets work, and how to use financial assets as part of a life-long financial strategy. Your understanding of financial markets will be enhanced by learning the tools of economics, incorporating knowledge of human behavior, and becoming familiar with particular aspects of financial markets and assets.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Intermediate Microeconomics I
description:
Intermediate analysis of microeconomic theory and concepts. Topics include consumer behavior and demand, production and costs, price and output under both perfect competition and pure monopoly. 
Prerequisite: 2110 and 2120 and (307 or MATH 1430 or MATH 1512).

title: Intermediate Macroeconomics I
description:
Theories of national income determination in explaining business cycles; aggregate supply; and the role of expectations. Role of monetary and fiscal policies in stabilizing the economy.  
Prerequisite: 2110 and 2120 and (307 or MATH 1430 or MATH 1512).

title: Economics Tools
description:
Introduces math, data, and writing skills that are essential for understanding, interpreting and communicating economics concepts.  
Prerequisite: MATH 1220 or MATH 1240 or ACT Math =>26 or SAT Math Section =>620 or ACCUPLACER College-Level Math =>100.
Pre- or corequisite: 2110 or 2120.

title: Introductory Statistics and Econometrics
description:
Introductory statistics, probability, probability distributions and hypothesis testing. Basic econometric techniques emphasizing estimation of economic relationships and the use of econometric models in forecasting. 
Prerequisite: 2110 and 2120 and MATH 1350.

title: Money and Banking
description:
Principles of money, credit and banking; organization and operation of the banking system; and the relationship between money, banking and the level of economic activity.  
Prerequisite: 303.

title: Labor Economics
description:
Determinants of labor force, wage levels and structures, and employment; human capital theory and discrimination, economic consequences of trade union and government intervention.  

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Development Economics
description: Theories of development and growth. Problems facing developing countries and possible solutions. Historical case studies of some developing countries. Prerequisite: 300 and 303.

title: Economics of Poverty and Discrimination
description:
Explores trends in income distribution especially across and within groups and examines theories explaining behavior and outcomes. Public policy concerning poverty and discrimination is studied and discussed. 

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Health Economics
description:
Market concepts and health care issues. Economic assessment of the U.S. health care system. Explores physician supply and demand, hospitals, malpractice, pharmaceuticals, insurance and related topics. 
Prerequisite: 2110 and 2120.

title: Environmental Economics
description:
Introduction to economics of environmental management problems, conceptual tools and policy applications: resource scarcity and sustainability, efficiency and equity, property rights and externalities, benefit-cost analysis and discounting, provision of public goods and nonmarket valuation.
Prerequisite: 2110 and 2120.

title: Public Economics
description:
Explores government intervention in markets, particularly with respect to market failures, when the provision of goods and services are inefficiently or inequitably provided by markets alone. Covers policy effects, often focusing on healthcare, labor, poverty, environment, education, and more.
This is a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: History of Economic Thought
description:
Development of the principle economic doctrines and schools of economic thought from the Physiocrats to Keynes.
This is a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Problem-Based Learning Using Data Analytics
description:
Interdisciplinary STEM course examining real world problems like those faced by underserved communities (poverty, environment, education, and health) using empirical tools, public policy perspectives. Students use data analytical tools to explore implementable solutions. Topics vary.

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Seminar in Current Economic Issues
description:
Topics will vary. Offered on an occasional basis. For course content, consult the economics department. Course may be repeated without limit provided the topics differ. 
Prerequisite: 300 and 303.

title: Applied Economic Theory and Analysis
description: This course develops fundamental economic theory using differential calculus and linear algebra and emphasizes the methodology of constructing and applying appropriate models in quantitative analysis with an emphasis on static and dynamic optimization.

title: Mathematical Methods in Economics
description:
A survey course designed to develop those mathematical results and methods which find frequent use in economic analysis.  
Prerequisite: 300 and 303.

title: Economic Forecasting Methods: A Time Series Approach
description:
Computer modeling of economic time series using univariate Box-Jenkins models and multivariate vector autoregressive models. Intervention models to assess policy impacts such as gun control, environmental law, tax changes and social programs. 
Prerequisite: 309.

title: Intermediate Econometrics
description:
Intermediate econometric techniques with strong emphasis on computer modeling of applied economic problems. Covers autocorrelation, heteroscedasticity, multicollinearity, dummy variable and distributed lag model and the use of econometric models in forecasting. 
Prerequisite: 309.

title: Topics in Health Economics
description:
Specialized topics in health care economics including medical education, national health insurance, comparative systems, drug industry and other contemporary issues. Emphasis on empirical applications in the study of health care issues. For course content, consult the economics department. Course may be repeated without limit provided the topics vary. 
Prerequisite: 300 and 309 and **335.

title: International Trade
description:
Determinants of patterns of international trade and comparative advantage. Trade restrictions and gains from trade. International factor movements.  
Prerequisite: 300 and 309.

title: Topics in Labor Economics
description:
Wage theory, industrial relations, migration, discrimination, comparative labor problems, special groups in the work force and other contemporary topics. Emphasis on economic implications and the role of public policy in these labor topics.  
Prerequisite: 309 and *320.

title: Independent Study
description:
For senior students wishing to study topics not covered in an existing course or in more detail. Requirements will be agreed upon between student and instructor. 
Prerequisite: 300 and 303 and 309.

title: Seminar in International Studies
description:
Designed to provide students from several disciplines an opportunity to apply an international perspective to their undergraduate training. Each student will present a term project drawing upon his or her particular background and relating it to international matters.

Restriction: senior standing.

title: Topics in Applications of Economics
description: Offers an advanced exploration of economic issues and their practical applications using theory and/or empirical analysis. Topics vary each term, reflecting the dynamic nature of modern economics.

title: Reading for Honors
description:
A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique. Open to juniors or seniors with an overall grade point average of at least 3.2 and approval of the department. 

Prerequisite: 309. 
Restriction: junior or senior standing and permission of department.

title: Senior Honors Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of an undergraduate Senior-level thesis.
Prerequisite: 498.

title: Microeconomics I
description:
Topics include producer and consumer theory, duality and welfare measures, competitive markets and monopoly and decision making under uncertainty.
Restriction: admitted to M.A. Economics or Ph.D. Economics.

title: Applied Economic Theory and Analysis
description:
This course develops fundamental economic theory using differential calculus and linear algebra and emphasizes the methodology of constructing and applying appropriate models in quantitative analysis with an emphasis on static and dynamic optimization.
Restriction: admitted to M.A. Economics or Ph.D. Economics or permission or instructor.

title: Mathematical Tools and Economic Models
description:
Calculus and matrix theory as applied to macro and micro models. Unconstrained and constrained optimization; static and comparative static analysis; introduction to dynamic analysis.
Restriction: admitted to M.A. Economics or Ph.D. Economics.

title: Macroeconomics
description:
Micro foundations of macroeconomics. Introduction to dynamic macroeconomics; optimal economic policy; and theories of economic growth. 

Restriction: admitted to M.A. Economics or Ph.D. Economics.

title: Statistics and Introduction to Econometrics
description:
Discrete and continuous probability distributions; expectations; joint, conditional marginal distributions; hypothesis testing; least squares estimators; violation of the least squares principle. Econometric software with applications.
Restriction: admitted to M.A. Economics or Ph.D. Economics.

title: Econometrics I
description: Theory and applications: ordinary and generalized least squares, hypothesis testing, dummy variable and distributed lag models; simultaneous equation and two stage least square models; forecasting. Emphasis on computer modeling. Prerequisite: 508.

title: Econometrics II
description: Simultaneous equation methods, nonlinear least squares, maximum likelihood method, qualitative dependent variable models, asymptotic properties and test statistics. Emphasis on computer modeling. Prerequisite: 509.

title: Microeconomics II
description: Competitive equilibrium and welfare economics. Topics from imperfect competition, decision making under uncertainty, introduction to game theory and distribution theory. Prerequisite: 501 and 504.

title: Seminars in Industrial Organization
description: Industrial organization is the study of firms and markets. Course covers firms internal organization and the interactions of firms in markets that are competitive, oligopolistic or monopolistic. Prerequisite: 501 and 504.

title: Topics in Applied Economics
description:
Special topics in applied economics as they pertain to the major fields and support courses. Available for use by visiting faculty. 
Prerequisite: 501 and 504. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Natural Resource, Environmental, and Ecological Modeling I
description: Dynamic optimization and optimal control theory applications (deterministic and stochastic) and computation methods with an emphasis on renewable resources. Prerequisite: 501 and 504.

title: Topics in Environmental, Resource, and Ecological Economics
description: Special topics in environmental and natural resource economics. Credit can be earned more than once, as the topic and content will vary by instructor.  Prerequisite: 501.

title: Natural Resource, Environmental, and Ecological Modeling II
description: Dynamic optimization and optimal control theory applications with an emphasis on empirical. Models of natural resource utilization. Energy, minerals, fisheries, forest resources, ground- and surface water, and environmental and ecological stocks. Prerequisite: 501 and 504.

title: Environmental Economics
description: Causes and consequences of environmental externalities. Design and implementation of alternative policy instruments. Theory and methods to measure economic value of market and non-market environmental services. Prerequisite: 501 and 504.

title: Water Resources II: Models
description:
(Also offered as WR 572)
Practical aspects of the different technical models used by water resources professionals; hydrological, economic, ecological, etc. Students use models to solve problems. Emphasis on oral, written and graphic communication.
Prerequisite: (2120 or 300) and (CE 541 or CE 542 or EPS 562 or WR 576) and WR 571.

title: Water Resources I: Contemporary Issues
description:
(Also offered as WR 571)
Students examine contemporary issues in water resource systems, including water quality; ecosystem health; stakeholder concerns; economics; and water supply, policy, management and allocation. Emphasis on teamwork, cooperation, and oral, written and graphic communication. {Fall}

title: Independent Study
description: An independent study course on economic problems or issues. The study is carried out under the supervision of an economics faculty member. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Seminar in Applied Economics
description: This course is designed to enhance the academic and professional training of graduate students in economics. Through a combination of invited presentations, workshops, and internal presentations, the course offers exposure to cutting-edge research, skill development in research methodologies and programming, and opportunities to engage with both faculty and peers.

title: Public Economics
description:
Introduction to advanced study of public economics, including theoretical and empirical analysis of market failures and government interventions. Topics include externalities and public goods, social insurance programs, education policies, optimal taxation, income distribution, and government expenditures. 
Prerequisite: 501 and 508.

title: Topics in Health Economics
description:
Applications of economic theory and empirical analysis including information and agency problems, organization of markets for health care, and influence of government policies on medical innovation and population health. 
Prerequisite: 509 and 513.

title: Topics in Public and Labor Economics
description:
Advanced topics in public economics. Topics will vary; course can be repeated for credit if topics differ. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: International Development and Finance
description:
Role of foreign direct investment, foreign aid, remittances, and other financial flows for economic development and growth. Causes and consequences of currency and financial crises in developing countries. Balance of payments problems, international debt, and structural adjustment programs.
Prerequisite: 501 and 506.

title: Topics in International and Sustainable Development
description:
Topics in international/sustainable development . Emphasis on empirical modeling and analysis, using data for developing countries. including large-scale surveys. Exposure to econometric methods, simulations, GIS applications, and macro/micro-development modeling tools.
Prerequisite: 501 and 506 and 510.

title: Development Economics
description:
Economic growth and development, poverty and inequality, population growth. Credit markets and microfinance, risk and insurance. Role of government in development. Urbanization and rural-urban migration. 
Prerequisite: 501 and 506.

title: Sustainable Development
description:
Overview of sustainable development concepts, models, and policy issues, with an emphasis on sustainable uses of all types of capital - physical, human, social, and environmental - in an international context. 
Prerequisite: 501 and 506.

title: Workshop in Applied Economics
description: Research problems. Student presentations of methodology and results. Research projects may be student-directed or undertaken in conjunction with regular and/or visiting faculty. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Educational Leadership in a Democratic Society
description: Designed to help organizational leaders understand how schools have both limited and expanded educational opportunity and what leaders can do to organize educational institutions for democratic life.

title: Organizational Advancement: Strategic Leadership for School Improvement
description: This course prepares aspiring principals to lead schools through strategic resource management and organizational improvement. Participants examine school structures, personnel systems, budgeting, and technology integration through an equity lens, designing actionable plans to advance school performance.

title: Data-Informed Instructional Leadership
description: Development of instructional programs, human resources, and organizational improvements should be grounded in data, both qualitative and quantitative. Explores conceptual and practical approaches to analyzing data to improve schooling.

title: Instructional Leadership: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment
Visionary Leadership for Learning
description: This course explores in depth the idea that leadership is vision-based and that effective visions are developed jointly, communicated widely, support shared learning, and ultimately drive what gets done in the organization of community.

title: Developing and Retaining Exceptional Educators
description: Develops educational leaders' capacity to cultivate a diverse educator workforce honoring New Mexico's learners through recruiting and retaining culturally responsive educators, implementing inclusive evaluation systems, professional development, and authentic family partnerships grounded in historical/sociopolitical awareness and community cultural wealth.

title: Culture, Community, and Belonging: Creating Thriving Educational Communities
description: This course develops educational leaders' capacity to create thriving communities that honor New Mexico's diverse learners through culturally responsive practices. Participants focus on Safety, Community Engagement, and Well-being while building on community cultural wealth and fostering belonging.

title: Change and Instructional Management Internship
description: This course prepares educational leaders to lead change and instructional management through collaborative, equity-driven practices grounded in systems thinking. Emphasizes ethical practices and culturally responsive leadership. Requires 225 clinical experience hours.

title: Leadership and Organizational Change
description: This course, designed as an introduction to the concepts, theories and processes of organizational theory and organizational behavior, will provide prospective education leaders with a better understanding of the way organizations operate.

title: Professional Development and School Culture Internship
description: This course prepares educational leaders to lead professional development and school culture through collaborative, equity-driven practices grounded in systems thinking. Emphasizes ethical practices and culturally responsive leadership. Requires 225 clinical experience hours.

title: Communication for Education Leaders
description: This course explores both internal and external communication issues experienced in educational organizations. Emphasis will be placed upon what makes successful and productive communication from both theoretical and practical perspectives.

title: The School Principalship
description: This course focuses upon the school as a complex organization and the role of the principal within that organization. It examines the various roles played by the principal, with particular emphasis placed on school leadership.

title: School Finance and Resource Allocation
description: This course is designed to provide future school leaders with basic understanding of the workings of the educational funding system and to provide a framework for effective fiscal and staffing decisions at the school level.

title: The Adult Learner
description: Examines the teaching and learning transaction with adults. Specific attention is on adult life stage development, relevant learning theories and approaches, and learning style issues of cross-cultural populations.

title: Leadership for Conflict Resolution in Schools and Organizations
description: This course addresses current theories, strategies and practices in conflict resolution for educational and organizational leaders. Topics such as bullying, peer mediation programs, and restorative justice are covered.

title: Culturally Responsive Leadership for Equity and Social Justice
description: This course will focus on the struggle for educational equity and social justice, how this struggle has been interpreted and understood, and how leaders can help educational institutions and communities promote equity and justice.

title: Instructional Leadership, Supervision, and Evaluation
description: Focuses on supervision in terms of professional growth, staff development, and creating organizations in which learning, rather than power and control, is the center of attention. Supervision as evaluation is a relatively minor part of the course.

title: Legal Issues for School Leaders
description: This course explores how laws, including constitutional and statutory requirements, affect educational leadership.   Focusing on concepts behind legal cases, the course examines how leaders can improve educational provision to address underlying legal concerns.

title: Seminar in Educational Leadership
description: Topics vary from term to term, but are all critically important for educational administrators. They include but are not limited to: organizational development, leader behavior, teaching and learning, ethics, technology and educational policy. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Problems
description: Problems study is offered on demand only and with the permission of the instructor. Check with the chairperson of the Department for details.  Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Workshop in Educational Leadership
description: Faculty-supervised intensive and interactive program focusing on the development of specific skills.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Practicum
description: A required field experience for students in Educational Leadership programs who are not seeking state administrative licensure. Arranged with advisor and program faculty.

title: Advanced Field Experiences
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Administrative Internship
description: The administrative internship provides field-based experiences to develop the skills, knowledge, and practices identified in the New Mexico Administrator Competencies. Students must meet program requirements for entry into the internship.

title: Directed Readings in Educational Leadership
description:
A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Perspectives on Leadership
description: A doctoral seminar focusing on leading theories of educational, organizational, and community leadership, emphasizing transformational leadership and other forms of leadership that stress collaboration and shared decision making.

title: Introduction to Data Analysis for Organizational Leaders
description:
A doctoral seminar introducing quantitative inquiry with emphasis on methods that leaders can apply immediately to organizational issues. Content taught cooperatively to enhance opportunities for community building. 
Restriction: admitted to Ed.D. cohort.

title: Qualitative Research in Education
description: (Also offered as LLSS 605) A doctoral seminar focusing on qualitative research methods, including problem definition, data collection and analysis and increasing the trustworthiness of one s findings. A research study is required. Prerequisite: EDPY 511 or LLSS 523. Restriction: admitted to Ed.D. cohort.

title: Analyzing Qualitative Data
description: This doctoral seminar helps students increase their understanding and skills in analyzing qualitative data. It is assumed that prior to entering this seminar, the students have collected data on which to focus their analyses. Prerequisite: 605 or LLSS 605.

title: Quantitative Methods for Analyzing and Transforming Organizations
description:
A doctoral seminar focusing on how quantitative data can be used to understand organizational needs and to drive organizational decision making.
Restriction: admitted to Ed.D. program in Educational Leadership.

title: Organizational Change: Theory and Processes
description: Designed to help students better understand the change processes by studying various change models. Focuses on the  what  and  how  surrounding change as well as the decision making processes that impact change in institutions.

title: Community Learning as Leadership
description: A doctoral seminar focusing on what leaders can do to model learning, to jointly create the conditions to support individual and community learning, and to use what is learned to drive transformation.

title: Leadership and Group Dynamics
description: Explores the workings of groups in various learning environments and what makes a well-functioning educational group, committee, or team. Focus on importance of group dynamics as an imperative part of adult learning and training.

title: Democracy, Ethics, and Social Justice in Transformational Leadership
description: Using the work of leading theorists and activists on democracy, diversity, and dialogue, this seminar examines pressing educational issues. Focus on educational goals and purposes, including analysis and articulation of our own visions for education.

title: Seminar for Practicing School Administrators
description: Exploration of important issues facing practicing school leaders. Includes organizational analysis, facilities management, budgeting, educational assessment, evaluation of staff and strengthening ties to families and the local community.

title: Education, Politics, and Policy
description: A doctoral seminar focusing on shaping educational policy, how leaders can influence the policy making process and how policies are applied to meet educational and community needs.

title: Legal and Fiscal Underpinnings of Educational Equity
description: A doctoral seminar examining the intersections of law, finance, and social equity. Explores landmark cases, historical shifts in fiscal provision of education, and trends in the pursuit of educational equity.

title: Leader as Researcher
description: A doctoral seminar synthesizing how leaders use inquiry to influence decisions. Papers framing a research problem and justifying the methods to be used in researching a problem are required. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Workshop in Educational Leadership
description: Co-constructed by students and faculty, workshops are responsive to the most current and pressing educational issues facing educational leaders. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 credits for students enrolled in a doctoral program. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Educational Leadership
description: Topics vary. Restriction: doctoral students only.

title: Doctoral Internship
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
Doctoral students only.  
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Directed Readings in Educational Leadership
description:
A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.
Doctoral students only.  Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introduction to Education
description: Introduction to the historical, philosophical, sociological foundations of education, current trends, and issues in education; especially as it relates to a multicultural environment. Students will use those foundations to develop effective strategies related to problems, issues and responsibilities in the field of education. A field component at an educational site is required.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Foundations of Education
description: This course is designed to assist future teachers in gaining an understanding of the purposes and framework of schools. Topics covered in this course include, but are not limited to, ethics, legal issues, and leadership relative to education. The dynamic role of teachers is also explored.

title: Literacy/Language Instruction for ESL Learners
description: This course provides an understanding of second language acquisition and develops a strong basis for instruction of literacy/language to English as Second Language learners in K-12 classrooms.

title: ESL Across Content Areas
description: This course provides an understanding of the goals, strategies, and teaching techniques for effectively teaching content to ESL students in K-12 classrooms.

title: Children's Literature
description: This course focuses on the building familiarity with high quality, culturally responsive, and authentic children’s literature with the purpose of supporting literacy instruction in K-8 classrooms. Students will be introduced to a range of literature and strategies for incorporating this literature into their instruction.

title: Educating Linguistically and Culturally Diverse Students
description: Course familiarizes students with history, theory, practice, culture and politics of second language pedagogy and culturally relevant teaching. Examines theoretical and practical issues related to diversity of culture, race, gender, language, socioeconomic, and ability level in the classroom. Students will be introduced to effective teaching methods for linguistically and culturally diverse learners, including critical teaching behaviors and essential best practices for diverse students.

title: The Effective Classroom
description: This course is designed to prepare future teachers with the necessary classroom management skills needed for student success by providing instruction on classroom processes, techniques, and procedures. Systematic motivational strategies for a diverse student body are covered. The impact on student learning due to emotional, social, physical, and cognitive development from birth through adolescence is explored. Also covered are educational strategies to assist students with exceptional needs.

title: Curriculum and Assessment
description: Explores planning effective instruction, as well as designing and analyzing meaningful assessment based on students’ needs and on district state standards. Specific strategies focus on differentiating instruction, designing a conceptually-based curriculum, and analyzing standards for alignment with curriculum content and outcomes. Students learn how to use assessment data to direct their teaching practices and curriculum development. This course includes a structured 10-hour field component.

title: Effective Teaching
description: This course explores effective instructional processes, techniques, and procedures to help teachers lead their classrooms toward high levels of student success. Students learn practical keys and strategies for increased student motivation and learning in a diverse classroom. The processes of social, emotional, physical, and cognitive development from birth through adolescence and their implications for student learning are reviewed. Students examine a variety of classroom management models for understanding classroom behaviors and educational strategies that are effective in helping all students succeed. This course includes a structured 10-hour field component.

title: Technology Integration in the Classroom
description: Students apply knowledge of learning theory to explore how to incorporate educational technology as a classroom tool in the K-12 learning environment. Students will use classroom technology to enhance curriculum development and application to the classroom. Examines the impact of technology on the changing role of the teacher.

title: Elementary Curriculum and Teaching Methods
description: This course will emphasize effective teaching methods and lesson design for the elementary level (Kindergarten – 8th grade) that promotes hands-on learning and encompasses state standards. Strategies for leading a class in small and large group settings as well as maintaining a classroom environment that is conducive to learning will be emphasized. Practicum assignments allow students the opportunities to evaluate the effectiveness of their curriculum activities and teaching methods.

title: Teaching Reading for the Elementary Classroom
description: An exploration of developmentally, culturally and linguistically appropriate, formal and informal research- based assessments and teaching strategies in the teaching of reading in grades K-8. This course includes a structured 10-hour field component.

title: Fundamentals of Reading Instruction
description: Prepares students to apply research-based techniques in the development and implementation of a literacy program including phonics, phonemic awareness, fluency, vocabulary and comprehension. Students are introduced to a literature-based curriculum and explore developmentally appropriate reading techniques as applied to the reading process. Students explore and understand theory and research on the effective teaching of reading and writing, the components of language and the cognitive characteristics of readers. This course includes a structured 10-hour field component.

title: Secondary Teaching Methods
description: This course emphasizes methods and materials for teaching at the secondary level. It examines issues specific to the secondary teacher including adolescent motivation, communication, and appropriate classroom management. It provides analysis of secondary content standards in relation to instructional approaches, integration of technology, and alternative assessments. Students develop lesson plans using a variety of media and internet technology.

title: Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Internship
description: Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.

title: Teaching Social Studies K-8
description: Development of conceptual framework for study of community-based curriculum with emphasis on the diverse cultures of the southwest and value clarification. Supervised work with children allows for in-depth analysis of both content and process. Three lectures, 1 hour lab.

title: Teaching of Reading to Culturally and Linguistically Diverse Students I
description: Study of reading process for emergent and intermediate readers focusing on: cueing systems, assessment, family and community contexts, language, culture and instruction in individual and small group settings. Lab includes supervised tutoring and discussion group.

title: Teaching of Reading to Culturally and Linguistically Diverse Students II
description: Establishing a theoretical framework for exploring various approaches to reading/language development, instruction and evaluation in multicultural classroom settings. Three lectures, 1 hour lab.

title: Teaching Language Arts K-8
description: Study of oral and written forms of language. Background theory in language development and use in teacher-child interactions is presented and followed by carefully designed experiences with children. Three lectures, 1 hour lab.

title: Teaching Science K-8
description: Methods, processes, content and management of children's science observation, exploration, discovery and invention; attitudes of inquiry and wonderment. Science integrated with math and other areas of life. Three lectures, 1 hour lab.

title: Teaching Mathematics K-8
description:
Strategies and materials appropriate for traditional and innovative instructional programs in elementary school mathematics. Supervised work with children allows for in-depth analysis of both content and process. Three lectures, 1 hour lab.
Pre- or corequisite: MATH 1118 and (MATH 1220 or MATH 2118).

title: Teaching Experience I
description: Pre-Service Teaching Experience I  includes a one semester (Fall) apprenticeship under the guidance of an experienced co-teacher, also known as a Cooperating Teacher (CT). The CT will provide mentoring to the Teacher Candidate (TC )at their assigned school for a minimum of 14 hours each week.   The work of the TC will be overseen by the Cooperating Teacher in partnership with the EDUC 362 course instructor and the University Supervisor (US).

title: Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Student Teaching in the Elementary School
description:
Additional requirements are listed in previous section entitled “Student Teaching.” Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Pre- or corequisite: 321L or 331L or 333L or 353L or 361L.
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: Creating Effective K-8 Learning Environments I
description:
This course is the discussion and analysis seminar for Elementary Education majors during their first semester of Field Experience.
Corequisite: 411L. Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: Creating Effective K-8 Learning Environments II
description:
This course is the discussion and analysis seminar for Elementary Education majors during their second semester of Field Experience.
Prerequisite: 401L and 411L. Corequisite: 412L. Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: Using Assessment: Creating Effective K-8 Learning Environments III
description:
This course is the discussion and analysis seminar for Elementary Education majors during their third and final semester of Field Experience.
Prerequisite: 402L and 412L. Corequisite: 413L. Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: K-8 Field Experience I
description:
This course is designed for planning and teaching in K-8 Schools for Elementary Education majors in their first semester of Field Experience. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Prerequisite: 1120 and EDPY 310 and EDPY 472 and LLSS 443 and MSET 365. 
Corequisite: 401L. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: K-8 Field Experience II
description:
This course is designed for planning and teaching in K-8 Schools for Elementary Education majors in their second semester of Field Experience. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Prerequisite: 401L and 411L. Corequisite: 402L. Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: K-8 Field Experience III-Student Teaching
description:
This course is designed for planning and teaching in K-8 Schools for Elementary Education majors in their third and final semester of Field Experience. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Prerequisite: 402L and 412L. Corequisite: 403L. Restriction: admitted to B.S.Ed. in Elementary Education.

title: Teaching Social Studies K-8
description: Overview and development of the social studies curriculum within the contexts of the elementary school program and multicultural community settings.

title: Teaching Reading and Writing in the Content Field
description:
Course explores issues of literacy development (i.e. reading, writing, listening and speaking) across core content areas of school curriculum. Required in secondary teacher education for all content specialization areas. 
Restriction: permission of department.

title: Issues in Secondary Education
description:
An exploration of issues that face secondary school teachers, including classroom management, school and community, learning needs of adolescent learners, and planning for diverse groups. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Teaching Science K-8
description:
Teaching Science K-8: Methods, processes, content, and management of children’s science observation, exploration, discovery, and invention; attitudes of inquiry and wonderment.  Science integrated with math and other areas of life.

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Teaching Mathematics K-8
description: Teaching Mathematics K-8: K-8 strategies and materials appropriate for traditional and innovative instructional programs in elementary and middle school mathematics. Supervised work with children allows for in-depth analysis of both content and process.

title: Student Teaching
description: Observation and teaching in secondary schools for one or more semesters. Weekly seminar meetings required with University supervisors.  Prerequisite:  362.

title: Student Teaching Seminar
description: The seminar course is designed for teacher candidates to provide opportunities to reflect on and analyze issues and developments in the student teaching experience.   Teacher Candidates who are participating in field experiences as a requirement for secondary licensure require a space to share, reflect, problem-solve, and find support for their experiences in schools and for their future career paths. This course is designed to support teacher candidates while working with cooperating teachers during their field experience semesters.

title: Workshop
description:
(Also offered as MSET 492)
Faculty-supervised intensive and interactive program focusing on the development of specific skills.

title: Topics in Education
description: Undergraduate and or graduate credit for students in Teacher Education working with faculty in specific topics identified by the course title.

title: Reading and Research in Honors
description:
Advanced studies and research under the supervision of a faculty mentor.
Restriction: permission of major advisor.

title: Research Applications to Education
description: An exploration of the forms of research in teaching and learning. Students have opportunties to identify types of research and determine the significance of the conclusions of research.

title: Advanced Instructional Strategies
description: Exploration of accomplished teaching through study, practice and inquiry. Subject matter pedagogy and the diversity of pathways for learning, assessment and special needs in instruction are addressed.

title: Experiential, Project-Based and Service Learning
description: Explores experiential, project-based, and service learning for effective inquiry teaching.  Students design lessons that engage learners in real-world activities.  Students learn about research that supports the use of these methods.

title: The Process of Reflection and Inquiry
description: Engages experienced teachers in the study and analysis of their own teaching and learning events through reflection and inquiry. Case studies, journals and narratives of teachers are used as tools for developing understandings.

title: Integrating Curriculum and Inquiry in the Classroom
description: Inquiry and practice in integrating curriculum across disciplines of knowledge, children's diverse understandings, habits of mind and community needs and projects. Explores organization, models, goals setting, planning and evaluation.

title: Effective Teaching and Student Learning
description: Concepts of effective teaching and student learning are defined and compared to current trends in measuring teacher effectiveness and student achievement. Throughout the course, individuals examine their own teaching practices and their students' learning.

title: Teaching of Reading to Culturally and Linguistically Diverse Students II
description: The course focuses on methods for teaching reading in the elementary school and establishing a theoretical framework for exploring various approaches to reading/language development instruction and evaluation in multicultural classroom settings. Prerequisite: 330L.

title: Principles of Curriculum Development
description: Focuses on issues of curriculum (K-12) from formal aspects of goals setting and planning to implicit issues of politics, culture and ideology.

title: Issues in Secondary Education
description:
An exploration of issues that face secondary school teachers, including classroom management, school and community, learning needs of adolescent learners, and planning for diverse groups. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Social Justice in Education
description: Exploration of complex social justice issues and pedagogical responses. Focuses on knowledge and skills teachers need to integrate principles of social justice fully into classroom curriculum and instruction.

title: Engaging Youth Literacy through Latin American Testimonio
description: Presupposing interconnectedness between literacy and social justice, this course engages high school youth to design curriculum around diverse literacies through Testimonios unfolding within Latin American and Chicana/o literature, music, poetry, and visual and performative art.

title: Mentoring Educators for Professional Growth
description: This course is designed for active mentor teachers. Participants are reflective practitioners sharing experiences, issues, and suggestions with other mentor teachers.

title: Graduate Seminar in Secondary Teaching
description: The seminar course is designed for teacher candidates to provide opportunities for reflection on and analysis of issues and developments in the student teaching experience.

title: The Art of Masterful Teaching
description:
Exploration of multiple perspectives on characteristics of master teachers and the qualities of highly effective teaching. 
Restriction: permission of the department.

title: Initial Field Experience K-8 Seminar
description: This course is the discussion and analysis seminar for students in the Graduate Certificate in Elementary Education with K-8 Licensure during their first semester of Field Experience.

title: K-8 Field Experience Seminar II
description: This course is the discussion and analysis seminar for Graduate Certificate in Elementary Education with K-8 Licensure students during their second and final semester of Field Experience.

title: Seminar
description: For students in the Department of Teacher Education, this course synthesizes course work which has made up a master's degree program. Enhance and develop competence in professional communication, written and oral.

title: Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Workshop
description: Faculty-supervised intensive and interactive program focusing on the development of specific skills.

title: Topics in Education
description: Undergraduate and or graduate credit for students in Teacher Education working with faculty in specific topics identified by the course title.

title: Initial Field Experience
description:
This course is designed for planning and teaching in elementary schools for students during their first semester of Field Experience. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Advanced Field Experiences
description: Planned and supervised advanced professional laboratory or field experiences in agency or institutional settings.

title: Internship
description:
(Also offered as MSET 596)
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.

title: Directed Readings in Secondary and Adult Teacher Education
description: A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.

title: Directed Reading in Elementary Education
description: A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.

title: Master’s Thesis
description:
(Also offered as MSET 599) 
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Teacher Education Pedagogy
description: This doctoral course examines theory, research, policy frameworks and best practices for instruction appropriate for each stage of teacher development within the field of teacher education.

title: Contexts of Teaching and Learning in Teacher Education
description: This course is designed to examine teaching, learning, teacher education and teacher education reform using lenses from history, sociology, and philosophy; raising and addressing questions regarding teacher education.

title: Curriculum Theory Seminar
description:
Doctoral level seminar examining curriculum theory.  
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Teacher Education and Social Justice
description: Doctoral investigation of social justice specifically related to three aspects of teacher education: teaching for social justice; higher education, hegemony, models of education; and teacher education for social justice.

title: Teacher Leadership in Teacher Education
description:
Focuses on instructional leadership as related to professional growth, staff development, and creation of learning organizations. Development of teacher leaders is the main focus. 
Does not satisfy requirements for Educational Leadership licensure.

title: Instruction and Supervision in Teacher Education
description: The study and design of instruction in teacher education programs and of clinical preparation and supervision of instruction. Addresses national and state standards in teacher education and clinically based programs.

title: Dissertation Seminar
description:
(Also offered as MSET 690)
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

title: Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: admitted to Ed.D or Ph.D. Teaching, Learning, and Teacher Education.

title: Workshop
description:
Co-constructed by students and faculty, workshops are responsive to the most current and pressing issues in teaching, learning, and teacher education.
Restriction: permission of department.

title: Topics in Teacher Education
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Internship
description: Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.

title: Directed Readings in Elementary-Secondary Teacher Education
description: A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Human Growth and Development
description: Principles of human growth and development across the life span and implications for education.

title: Learning and the Classroom
description: The basic principles of learning, particularly cognition, motivation and assessment, and their application to classroom situations.

title: Facilitating Learning in College Courses
description: This course provides a theoretical and practical foundation for college-level teaching for undergraduates serving in the role of Peer Learning Facilitators and tutors. Topics include: instructional strategies; motivation; professional development; ethics; feedback and questioning techniques.

title: Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Classroom Assessment
description: Provides educators with skills in assessment and knowledge of issues in measurement and assessment. Skills necessary to understand and communicate large-scale test information are also developed.

title: Survey of Research Methods in Education
description: Overview of quantitative and qualitative research methods for research consumers. Emphasis is on locating published research and reading research reports with critical understanding of researchers  methods of data collection and analysis.

title: Survey of Statistics in Education
description: Non-technical overview of statistical methods in educational research; computation is not covered. Emphasis on developing critical understanding of statistical methods and results when reading and interpreting research, not on producing research or calculating statistics. Pre- or corequisite: 500.

title: Principles of Human Development
description: Principles of human growth and development, which include cognitive, psychosocial and physical development across the life span, with a particular focus on educational implications.

title: Statistical Software Applications for Education Research
description:
Lab-based instruction of R including R functions, packages, and resources available for data management and analysis, basic
operations (e.g. data importation, management, and examination), and analyses. 
Corequisite: One credit hour with EDPY 603.

title: Conducting Quantitative Educational Research
description: Provides students with skills for designing quantitative educational research, including identifying a problem, reviewing literature, formulating hypotheses, considering ethical issues, selecting participants, selecting or constructing measures, making valid inferences, writing reports.

title: Principles of Classroom Learning
description: Research and theory in learning, particularly cognition, motivation and assessment, with emphasis on educational implications.

title: Introductory Educational Statistics
description:
Foundations of statistical methods for research producers. Covers sampling methods, descriptive statistics, standard scores, distributions, estimation, statistical significance testing, t-tests, correlation, chi-square and effect size using statistical software and computation.

Pre- or corequisite: 505.

title: Survey and Questionnaire Design and Analysis
description: Covers survey research from item writing and survey development to sampling, administration, analysis and reporting. Emphasizes applications and interpretations in educational and social science research and use and interpretation of statistical software for survey research. Prerequisite: 511.

title: Motivation Theory and Practice
description: The course promotes understanding of current theories and research in motivation with an emphasis on applications in educational settings. Strategies for establishing motivation-rich environments will be developed.

title: Seminar in Thought and Language
description: (Also offered as LING, PSY 565) The role of language in human cognition is approached from a sociocultural framework. Topics: semiotic systems, languages of the mind, categorization, problem solving, and cognitive pluralism.

title: Classroom Assessment
description: Provides educators with skills in assessment and knowledge of issues in measurement and assessment. Skills necessary to understand and communicate large-scale test information are also developed.

title: Introduction to Educational and Psychological Measurement
description: A survey of classical and modern approaches to measurement and assessment as applied to education and/or psychology. Includes measurement and scaling, reliability and validity, traditional and alternative assessment methods. Prerequisite: 511.

title: Identity Development
description: This course examines identity through a developmental and educational psychology lens, focusing on early adolescence to emerging adulthood, with a particular emphasis on educational contexts. Key topics include gender, sexuality, race, class, and their intersections. Consideration is given throughout for how power, privilege, and oppression shaping identity and society.

title: Psychological Development of Women
description: This course provides an introduction to the psychological development of women and girls using contemporary and emerging theoretical frameworks. The course will also explore qualitative research methods and consider the implications of cultural and empirical constructions of gender for social and educational policy and practice.

title: Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Directed Readings
description: A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.

title: Master s Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Applied Statistical Design and Analysis
description:
Includes factorial analysis of variance (ANOVA), planned comparisons, post hoc tests, repeated measures designs, and regression analysis. Emphasis on solving applied problems using statistical analysis with computer software. 
Prerequisite: 511.
Corequisite: 504.

title: Multiple Regression Techniques as Applied to Education
description: Includes bivariate regression, multiple regression with continuous and categorical independent variables and interactions, orthogonal and nonorthogonal designs and selected post hoc analyses. Computer analysis, conceptual understanding and applications to educational research are stressed. Prerequisite: 603.

title: Applied Multivariate Statistics
description: The course will cover the following topics: principal component analysis, factor analysis, cluster analysis, discrimination analysis, multivariate ANOVA, and item response theory.

title: Structural Equation Modeling
description: Theory, application, interpretation of Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) techniques. Includes covariance structures, path diagrams, path analysis, model identification, estimation and testing; confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation modeling and linear structural relations using latent variables. Prerequisite: 604 or 606.

title: Multilevel Modeling
description: The fundamentals of multilevel regression models, applications of such models to both cross-sectional and longitudinal data, and a survey of several advanced multilevel regression models. Prerequisite: 604.

title: Seminar in Classroom Learning
description: An examination of selected research and theory on learning and cognition in specific domains with emphasis on application to classrooms or other learning situations.

title: Seminar in Human Growth and Development
description: Examination of selected topics in research and theory relevant to human growth and development, including implications for instruction and child rearing. May be repeated once for credit when topics differ.

title: Advanced Motivation
description: This course is designed for researchers in educational psychology and related disciplines. It aims to guide students in identifying emerging theories, methodologies, and debates that can inform and inspire their future research endeavors.

title: College Teaching Seminar
description: This course provides an empirically-based theoretical and practical foundation for college-level teaching. Topics include: instructional strategies; teaching technologies; assessment; professional development; ethics; teaching as part of overall professional identity.

title: Advanced Seminar in Educational Psychology
description: Seminar introduces students to current research topics and professional issues in Educational Psychology.

title: Dissertation Seminar
description:
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Advanced Seminar in Quantitative Educational Research
description: Seminar introduces advanced students to current research designs and controversies, statistical analysis techniques and computer applications.  Prerequisite: 603.

title: Advanced Qualitative Research Methods in Human Development
description: In this seminar, students will have the opportunity to deepen their understanding of qualitative research methods relevant to human development, educational psychology, and adjacent disciplines. The key focus of the course will be on learning how to conduct semi-structured interviews and analyze interview data using reflexive thematic analysis.

title: Internship
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Directed Readings
description: A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Electrical Math I
description: Applies basic arithmetic functions, electrical formulas, calculations of material and circuit load requirements, rules for series, parallel and combination circuits and mechanical work and power.

title: National Electric Code
description: Provides students with a basic understanding of the National Electrical Codes and how they apply to residential, commercial, and renewable energy systems such as photovoltaic electrical generating systems. How the NEC Codes apply to an Industrial Setting.

title: Electrical Theory I
description: Covers the basic concepts of DC and AC theory with emphasis on electron theory, units of electrical measurement, NEC terminology, and selection of branch circuit conductors. Upon successful completion of this course the student will define the following concepts and demonstrate her/his ability to apply them to the electrical trade by means of written examinations and assignments, with a minimum accuracy of 71%

title: Electrical System Fundamentals
description: AC and DC Electrical Systems: a/c circuits – a/c capacitors – d/c and a/c inductors – transformers – ac relays and contactors– electrical distribution – troubleshooting methods – hands on a/c d/c trainer – Enclosures and Conduit Systems : installing metal struts – installing enclosures and boxes – installing flex metal and liquidtight nonmetallic conduit – conduit bending –installing electrical metallic tubing – cutting reaming – threading and installing ridge metal conduit – feed conductor into conduit and using a pulling point – hands on industrial trainer.

title: Applied Industrial Electricity I
description: Electrical safety, AC and DC circuits, use and care of common measuring instrumentation, schematic and wiring diagrams, electromagnetism, National Electric Code branch circuits.

title: Electrical Theory II
description: Covers the application of the National Electrical Code, local codes and regulations for installation of branch circuits, services, feeders, temporary services and associated materials and equipment for residential and light commercial applications.

title: Blueprint Reading I
description: Provides instruction in reading and interpreting blueprints and specifications. Emphasizes terminology, symbols, notations, scaling, dimensioning and basic blueprint drawing techniques.

title: Introduction to Wiring Lab
description: Covers safety, tools, materials, single pole switches, receptacles, overcurrent protection, three- and four-way switches, pilot switches, door chimes, dryer and range receptacles and swamp coolers. Analyze Blueprint applications as it applies to electrical installations. NEC requirements for light commercial applications.

title: Topics in Electricity
description: This course explores the history of electricity from early discoveries to modern power systems. Students will examine key scientific breakthroughs, inventions, and technological advancements that shaped the electrical industry. Topics include the work of pioneers such as Benjamin Franklin, Alessandro Volta, Michael Faraday, Nikola Tesla, and Thomas Edison, as well as the evolution of power generation, transmission, and distribution systems. Emphasis is placed on how historical developments continue to influence modern electrical theory, practice, and safety. By studying the historical context, students will gain a deeper appreciation for the industry and its role in shaping society.

title: Photovoltaic Fundamentals/Applications
description: Photovoltaic (PV) is electrical energy from the sun. This course is an introduction to the fundamentals associated with Photovoltaic systems. Topics cover PV market analysis, the solar resource, electrical PV principles, solar module fundamentals, system components, types of systems, site assessment, system applications, energy production analysis, and best practice code compliant practices including mounting, wiring, and interconnecting systems to the utility. Anyone interested in knowing more about the solar industry or anyone working in fields associated with solar energy will benefit from this course.

title: DC Circuit Analysis
description:
Basic elements of DC electrical and electronic circuits, circuit analysis, measurement, and circuit design. Study of circuit network theorems and their applications to design techniques. Study of conductors and insulators. 
Prerequisite: MATH 1215 or (MATH 1215X and MATH 1215Y and MATH 1215Z).

title: Mechanical Systems
description: This course covers vacuum, cryogenic, and hydraulic technologies and systems. Provide basic understanding of the design, assembly, and operations of mechanical systems typically found in industrial applications.

title: Industrial Shop Practice
description: Principles of and practice with hand and machine tools used by electromechanical technicians. Includes operation of lathe and milling machines, drilling, welding, sawing, grinding, soldering, brazing, measurements, sheet metal work, benchwork, or other appropriate operations.

title: Robotics
description: This course covers designing, building, and programming NXT style robots. It includes autonomous robotics, light, sound, touch and ultrasonic sensors, computer programming, problem solving, and remote control robotics.

title: Advanced Robotics
description:
This course covers more advanced robotics concepts, including designing, building, testing, and refining a prototype using engineering design process. Topics include remote control robotics, robotic electronics, remote video navigation, autonomous robotics, more advanced programming techniques. 
Prerequisite: 162.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Titles will vary.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Titles will vary.

title: Advanced Robotics II
description:
This course covers advanced topics in robotics, including: advanced robotic platform design and construction, remote control electronics, servo controlled mechanisms, and remote wireless video applications. 
Prerequisite: 163.

title: Emergency Medical Responder
description:
An entry level course which prepares students to respond to and provide care for ill or injured patients. It includes an
overview of the human body, specified basic life support, airway management, trauma, medical and environmental
emergencies, medical/legal, emergency operations and other related topics.

title: Emergency Medical Responder Laboratory
description: An entry level course which focuses on EMR and NM First Responder skills development through simulations and scenarios with an emphasis on assessment, hands-on skills and team work in the patient care environment.

title: Emergency Medical Technician Lecture
description: An entry level course which prepares students to respond to and provide care for ill or injured patients. It includes an overview of the human body, basic life support, airway management, trauma, medical and environmental emergencies, medical/legal, emergency operations and other related topics.

title: Emergency Medical Technician Laboratory
description: An entry level course which focuses on EMT and NM EMT-Basic skills development through simulations and scenarios with an emphasis on assessment, hands-on skills and team work in the patient care environment.

title: Emergency Medical Technician Clinical
description: A course for EMT students to complete patient contact and clinical care requirements. Students rotate through various healthcare settings, refining clinical competencies required as an entry-level EMT.

title: Introduction to EMS Systems
description: Covers the history of emergency medical services and the development of EMS systems and current trends and issues in EMS. Ideal for students considering a career in EMS.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Titles will vary.

title: Advanced EMT Lecture
description: A course which prepares students to respond to and provide specified advanced emergency care for ill or injured patients. It includes an overview of the human body, advanced life support, airway management, trauma, medical and environmental emergencies, medical legal issues, emergency operations, intravenous therapy, vascular access, advanced pharmacology and other related topics.

title: Advanced EMT Laboratory
description: An advanced laboratory course which focuses on AEMT and NM EMT-Intermediate skills development through simulations and scenarios with an emphasis on assessment, hands-on skills and team work in the patient care environment.

title: Advanced EMT Clinical
description: A course for AEMT students to complete patient contact and clinical care requirements. Students rotate through various healthcare settings, refining clinical competencies required as an entry-level AEMT.

title: Cooperative Education Work Phase
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
$10.00 annual fee.  Offered on a CR/NC basis only.  Open to undergraduate students in the School of Engineering only.

title: Evaluation of Cooperative Education Work Phase 1
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive, written reflection on the student's professional or practical experience.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Evaluation of Cooperative Education Work Phase 2
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive, written reflection on the student's professional or practical experience.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Evaluation of Cooperative Education Work Phase 3
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive, written reflection on the student's professional or practical experience.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Evaluation of Cooperative Education Work Phase 4
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive, written reflection on the student's professional or practical experience.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Cooperative Education Work Phase
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
$10.00 annual fee.  Offered on a CR/NC basis only.  Open to graduate students in the School of Engineering only.

title: Introduction to Engineering Computing
description: Introduction to the use of computing to solve engineering problems. Students learn computer programming fundamentals and  learn to use a numerical computing environment (e.g.  MATLAB).  Applications to engineering problems are explored.

title: Engineering Honors Seminar
description: The first course in the Engineering Honors sequence.  The course provides an introduction to the engineering profession and to the curricula offered in the School of Engineering.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics in interdisciplinary engineering or computer science at an introductory level.

title: Technology in Society
description: This is an introduction to the ways in which technology shapes the world-and is itself shaped by society, culture, politics, economics and history. Topics include industrialization, technological changes, cultural impact, environmental policies and social and ethical responsibilities.

title: Engineering, Business, and Society
description: Using a team-teaching format to foster students' understanding of the interaction of engineering practice with business and society. Students will learn about innovation, entrepreneurship, global engineering standards, professional ethics, business and technical writing.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics in interdisciplinary engineering or computer science at a senior level.

title: EMT-Basic
description: This course meets or exceeds the National EMS Education Standards for the EMT level, New Mexico EMT-Basic Scope of Practice and National Registry requirements for Emergency Medical Technician.

title: Introduction to EMS Systems
description: Covers the history of emergency medical services and the development of EMS systems and current trends and issues in EMS. Ideal for students considering a career in EMS.

title: EMT-Basic Lab
description: This course meets or exceeds the National EMS Education Standards for the EMT level, New Mexico EMT-Basic Scope of Practice and National Registry requirements for Emergency Medical Technician.

title: EMT-Intermediate Lab
description: Meets New Mexico requirements for EMT-Intermediate skills training.

title: EMT-Intermediate
description: Meets New Mexico requirements for EMT-Intermediate lecture content.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Titles will vary.

title: Principles of EMT Lab Instruction
description:
