title: T: Medieval French Studies
description: Study of topics in medieval French literature and culture.

title: Reading French for Graduate Students I
description: This is the first of a two-course series for graduate students who need to acquire a reading knowledge of French.

Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.

title: Physical Geology
description:
This course is an introduction to our dynamic Earth, introducing students to the materials that make up Earth (rocks and minerals) and the processes that create and modify the features of our planet. The course will help students learn how mountains are formed, how volcanoes erupt, where earthquakes occur, and how water, wind, and ice can shape the landscape. Students will also develop a basic understanding of the ways humans have altered the planet including our impact on natural resources and global climate change. 
Students are encouraged but not required to enroll concurrently in 1110L.

title: Physical Geology Lab
description: Students will learn to identify rocks and minerals in hand samples, work with topographic maps, geologic maps, and geologic cross-sections, and apply stratigraphic principles to explore geologic time.

title: Geological Disasters
description: This course will incorporate an overview of the geological processes that result in natural disasters and the input humans have on the amplification or mitigation of these natural disasters. We will examine past catastrophes and discuss the probability of such disasters occurring again. Hazards investigated will include, but not be limited to earthquakes, volcanoes, tsunami, hurricanes, floods, landslides, and astronomical events such as meteor and comet collisions with Earth. We will investigate the data obtained from recent disasters and explore the costs in human and economic terms.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Historical Geology Lecture and Laboratory
description: Origin and history of the earth including age of the planet and dating of rocks, changing configurations of oceans and continents as a result of plate tectonics, records of climate change, history of formation and erosion of mountain chains, origin and evolution of life and causes of extinction.  Required field trip and lab exercises permit understanding of how Earth history is interpreted from the geologic rock record.

title: Topics
description:
Eight- to 16-week courses on selected topics relating directly to the human experience, e.g., Volcanoes, Extinctions, Weather, Earthquakes, New Mexico's Water, Soils, Nuclear Hazards, Geomagnetism, Albuquerque Field Geology and the Geology of Everyday Life. 
{Fall, Spring}

title: Elements of Global and National Security
description: Analyzes issues such as: economics, demography, ecology, energy, health, and geopolitics, with a view towards understanding their implications for global and national security.

title: National Security and Intelligence Analysis
description: Introduces students to the Intelligence process, principles, and product, then explores the structured analytic techniques that help analysts detect & mitigate the influence of bias and obstacles to critical thinking.

title: Topics in Global and National Security
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. If topics vary, the course may be repeated up to two times.

title: Introduction to Global and National Security Policy
description: The course provides a broad overview of national security policymaking context and the challenges of global security policy, after an overview of basic concepts.

title: Ethics of AI in Autonomous Systems
description: This class covers ethical considerations in the design, development and implementation of autonomous and intelligent systems.  Students will identify ethical issues in case studies, explore strategies to incorporate ethics in their work, and generate solutions that include ethics as part of the design parameters.

title: Design of Human-Centered AI in Autonomous Systems
description: This course focuses on implementation, analysis, and evaluation of AI-enabled autonomous systems within a socio-technical systems context.   Using an ethics-as-design framework, students will develop a technical design that embeds ethical considerations, then prototype, test, and critically assess their design for technical efficacy and implications for users and social systems.

title: Cybersecurity and National Security
description: This course covers the importance of cybersecurity to national policy today. The history of cybersecurity as a strategic weapon, how long it's been recognized and why, and campaigns where cybersecurity has been used as a weapon.

title: Introduction to Directed Energy
description: Directed energy (DE) is a technology that delivers energy to a target at the speed-of-light with a deep magazine. This course fills the knowledge gap in the government, providing an introduction to DE technology and policy.

title: National Security and Intelligence Analysis
description: Introduces students to the Intelligence process, principles, and product, then explores the structured analytic techniques that help analysts detect & mitigate the influence of bias and obstacles to critical thinking.

title: Human Decision Making
description: The course Human Decision Making will present the idea that humans are not rational but are still predictable in the ways they deviate from rationality.

title: The Ecology and Evolution of Human Decision Making
description: The course will cover ecological/evolutionary models of heuristics and biases, including error management theory and the theory of fast and frugal heuristics, and will present recent work on the evolution of collective decision-making.

title: Environmental Security: Energy
description: This course explores the major systems that supply energy to modern societies and discusses potential threats to energy availability, reliability, and affordability.

title: Environmental Security: Food and Water
description: Examines food and water supply from the perspective of environmental security. Environmental security is related to national security, but it encompasses more concerns than the viability of the nation-state.

title: Globalization and National Security
description: This course reviews and assesses the main features, concepts and issues related to the global security environment. In the process, this course surveys global security policies and strategies.

title: Technology, Innovation and National Security
description: This class addresses the unique processes and skills required for the realization of new technology products from concept to commercialization and the ability to predict blind spots that could have national security consequences.

title: Introduction to Resilience
description: This course aims to offer a broad introduction to assist students to understand and apply concepts of resilience on generic systems to build and maintain sustainable communities.

title: Decision Making Under Various Forms of Uncertainty
description:
Decision making involves a mix of information that can be vague, ambiguous and highly subjective. The information can also be non-numeric. This course addresses methods to make decisions in this environment.
Pre-requisite: A modest familiarity of probability models.

title: Global Nuclear Security
description: This course focuses on the main aspects of geopolitical nuclear security.

title: Nuclear Safeguards and Nonproliferation
description: Nuclear Safeguards and Nonproliferation is a graduate level course focusing on methods and technologies associated with nuclear materials safeguards and protection.

title: Fundamentals of National Security Law
description: This course surveys the national security law construct of the United States. Students will analyze and evaluate the Constitution and a range of national security-related statutes, case law, treaties, and commentaries.

title: Human Rights Law and National Security
description: Students will exam how national security law is developing, and how the United States Government educates and informs national security professionals to work in a world of threats and civil liberties.

title: Global Health Security: Threats, Policies, and Politics
description: This course will provide an overview of the actors, drivers, and policies that shape the increasingly complex global health landscape.

title: Global Health Security: Climate, Disease, and Public Health
description: This course provides an introduction to global health issues and how they pertain to U.S. National Security interests for the novice.

title: American National Security Policy and Process
description: The purpose of this course is to help you build a theoretical understanding of American foreign and national security policy with a discussion of America’s grand strategy informed by the major theoretical traditions in the international relations field.

title: Issues in American National Security
description: This course is designed to help you develop skills and knowledge in the field of national security policy. You will receive an introduction to the principal threats and challenges to United States foreign policy and national security.

title: U.S.-Latin America Strategic Partnership: A National Security Perspective
description: This course reviews and assesses the main features, concepts and issues related to the global security environment, paying heed to the U.S.-Latin America dimension. This course surveys U.S-Latin America security policies and strategies.

title: U.S. Latin America National Security
description: This course reviews and assesses the main features, concepts and issues related to Latin America's contemporary social, political, economic, and environmental dimensions.

title: Topics in Global and National Security
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: GNSPI Capstone: Communication for Policy Professionals
description: This course should be taken in the final semester in the degree program. It enables students to polish their best work into publishable-quality writing and effective public speaking to help effect policy change in their chosen field of study. It encourages reflection on their entire degree program by creating and presenting a portfolio of their work.

title: Greek I
description: This course serves as an introduction to the fundamentals of the historic Greek language and culture. The aims of this course are to acquire basic skills in reading, writing, and comprehension in order to read authentic Greek texts with the help of a dictionary. Students will also study the history of the Greek language, texts, and culture.

title: Greek II
description: This course continues the study of the historic Greek language and culture. The aims of this course are to continue to build basic skills in reading, writing, and comprehension in order to read authentic Greek texts with the help of a dictionary. Students will also continue to study the historical factors behind the developments of the Hellenic world and its texts.

title: Greek III
description: Continuation of 1120. The aims of this course are to develop skills in reading, writing, and comprehension in Greek. These include the grammar and vocabulary needed in order to read and understand authentic Greek texts with the help of a dictionary.

title: Greek IV
description: Continuation of 2110. The aims of this course are to develop skills in reading, writing, and comprehension in Greek. These include the grammar, vocabulary, and exegetical tools needed in order to read and understand Greek texts of moderate difficulty and complexity.

title: Advanced Greek I
description: Readings in Homer, Sophocles, Euripides, Plato and the New Testament, depending on the level and interests of the class.

title: Advanced Greek II
description: Readings in Homer, Sophocles, Euripides, Plato and the New Testament, depending on the level and interests of the class.

title: Topics in Greek Language and Literature
description:
Graduate readings in ancient Greek authors. 
Prerequisite: 301 or 302.

title: Survey of Ancient Greek Literature
description: This course provides students experience reading a wide range of texts in ancient Greek literature from Homer to the Hellenistic period, across all genres, periods, and styles.

title: Graduate Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: German I
description: Intended for students with no previous exposure to German, this course develops basic listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills aiming toward the ACTFL novice-mid level. This is an introductory course designed to teach the student to communicate in German in everyday situations and to develop an understanding of German cultures through the identification of cultural products and practices, of cultural perspectives, and the ability to function at a survival level in an authentic cultural content. This course will also develop the student’s sense of personal and social responsibility through the identification of social issues.

title: German II
description: Continuation of 1110. Students will develop a broader foundation in skills gained during the first semester, including understanding, speaking, reading and writing German aiming toward the ACTFL novice-high level. This course is designed to increase student fluency in German as applied to everyday situations. Students will also learn to recognize and understand various German products, practices, and perspectives, identifying common cultural patterns, describing basic cultural viewpoints, and further developing their sense of personal and social responsibility through the investigation of cultural issues.

title: German III
description: In this third semester course, students will continue to develop a broader foundation in skills gained during the first two semesters, including understanding, speaking, reading and writing German aiming toward the ACTFL intermediate-low level. This course is designed to teach the student to communicate in a more sustained way in areas of personal interest and in everyday situations. Students will engage in and analyze various German products, practices, and perspectives, as well as continue to develop their sense of personal and social responsibility through comparison and contrast of cultural perspectives.

title: German IV
description: In this fourth semester course, students will continue to broaden and refine skills gained during previous semesters, including understanding, speaking, reading and writing German aiming at the ACTFL intermediate-mid level. This course is designed to teach the student to communicate in a more sustained way in situations that go beyond the everyday. Students will evaluate various German products, practices, and create ways to demonstrate their sense of personal and social responsibility through participation in cultural interaction.

title: Sickness, Insanity and Transgression in German Literature and Film
description: In this course students will explore the concepts of physical and psychological health and happiness by analyzing the ways in which sickness, insanity and social transgression appear in a range of cultural records from the late 19th and early 20th centuries in Germany. We will review these concepts by looking at a range of cultural records, including literary texts, medical, psychological and contemporary scientific writings and films.

title: Advanced German I
description: Contextual grammar review based on cultural materials from a variety of media and short literary texts.

title: Advanced German II
description: Contextual grammar review based on cultural materials from a variety of media and short literary texts.

title: Advanced GRMN Conversation
description: Practice in speaking and listening within the context of selected topics.

title: Topics: Germany Today
description: Study of present-day life and culture in Germany as represented in popular media, including TV and film. Aimed at students who wish to improve their language skills.

title: German Drama and Theater
description: Introduction and overview of the history and trends of drama and theater in German-speaking cultures.

title: Introduction to German Literature
description: Study of literary texts of varying length from a variety of genres and periods.

title: Introduction to German Culture
description: Introduction to cultural and intercultural aspects of life in German-speaking countries. Readings include historical as well as contemporary material.

title: Topics in German Literature and Culture in Translation
description: (Also offered as COMP, ENGL 336) Study of individual authors, genres, and/or periods of German literature and culture in translation. May only be taken twice for the German major and once for the German minor.

title: Intermediate Language Instruction
description: Review of grammar topics, conversation, and composition. Prepares students for the Zertifikat Deutsch exam administered by the Goethe-Institut.

title: Lecture Series in German Studies
description: Team-taught interdisciplinary lecture series with an overarching theme followed by discussion sections. May be repeated three times for undergraduate credit. May not be repeated for graduate credit.

title: Seminar in German Studies
description: Introductory undergraduate seminar on specific topics in German Literature, culture and language. Multiple sections may be offered in a given year. Titles of individual sections may vary as content varies. Repetition unlimited if content/topic changes.

title: Workshop in German Studies
description: Introductory workshops on various topics relating to contemporary German Culture. Emphasis on applied language skills. Multiple sections may be offered in a given year. Titles of individual sections will vary as content varies.

title: Contemporary German Cultures
description: Study of contemporary social, political, and cultural trends in German-speaking countries based on a variety of current sources.

title: Advanced German Composition
description: Intensive practice of writing skills in a variety of genres.

title: German-English Translation
description: Study of theories and methods of translating, and practical work in translation from German into English and English into German.

title: Special Topics in German Studies
description: Topics will deal with specific aspects of German literature and culture and language. Repetition unlimited if content changes.

title: Advanced German Composition
description: Intensive practice of writing skills in a variety of genres. Prepares students for the Zentrale Oberstufenprufung administered by the Goethe-Institut. May be repeated twice for undergraduate credit, and once for graduate credit.

title: Topics: Advanced Seminar in German Studies
description: Advanced study of periods and genres in German literature and thought from 1700 to present. Repetition unlimited if content changes.

title: Lecture Series in German Studies
description: Team-taught interdisciplinary lecture series with an overarching theme followed by discussion sections. May be repeated three times for undergraduate credit. May not be repeated for graduate credit.

title: Advanced Seminar in German Studies
description: Advanced undergraduate seminar on specific topics in German literature, culture and language. Multiple sections may be offered in a given year. Titles of individual sections will vary as content varies.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Reading and Research for Honors
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive paper or research proposal that integrates knowledge attained through coursework, research, and experience.
Open to juniors and seniors approved by the department.

title: Honors Essay
description:
A faculty-taught course emphasizing writing, critique and revision within the context of a particular genre or academic discipline.
Open only to seniors enrolled for departmental honors. Restriction: permission of supervising instructor.

title: German Reading for Graduate Students
description: Accelerated course for graduate reading requirements in other departments. Emphasizes readings in sciences and humanities. Will not satisfy the Arts and Sciences language requirement. Undergraduates must have permission of instructor.

title: Topics in 18th-Century German Literature and Culture
description: Topics in German literature and culture from the Enlightenment to Early Romanticism and Weimar Classicism.

title: Special Topics in German Studies
description: Topics will deal with specific aspects of German literature, culture and language. Repetition unlimited if content changes.

title: Graduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
May be repeated per Office of Graduate Studies policy three times. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in 19th-Century German Literature and Culture
description: Topics in German literature and culture from Romanticism to the Fin-de-Sicle.

title: Topics in 20th-Century German Literature and Culture
description: Topics in German literature and culture from the Fin-de-Sicle to contemporary developments.

title: Topics in Gender, Media, and Literatures in German Contexts
description: Emphasis on the study of gender, or film, or other media from 1800 to the present.

title: Lecture Series in German Studies
description: Team-taught interdisciplinary lecture series with an overarching theme followed by discussion sections. May be repeated three times for undergraduate credit. May not be repeated for graduate credit.

title: Graduate Seminar in German Studies
description: Graduate seminar on specific topics in German Literature, culture, and language. Multiple sections may be offered in a given year. Titles of individual sections will vary as content varies. Repetition unlimited if content/topic changes.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Maps and GIScience
description: Explore the principles of Geographic Information Science (GIScience) and its applications in solving human, natural, and socio-environmental challenges. Learn to evaluate geospatial data and technologies; analyze their significance, implications, and applications; and communicate insights effectively. Topics include map use, spatial data analysis, Geographic Information Systems (GIS), remote sensing, and Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS).

title: Maps and GIScience Laboratory
description: Gain hands-on field and laboratory experience with geospatial tools, including Geographic Information Systems (GIS), remote sensing, and Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS). Manage, collect, analyze,  interpret, and visualize spatial data to solve real-world problem, and develop your own GIScience research report.

title: Introduction to Environmental Studies
description: Survey of environmental issues related to the degradation of land, air and water resources.

title: Home Planet: Land, Water, Life
description: This course introduces the physical elements of world geography through the study of climate and weather, vegetation, soils, plate tectonics, and the various types of landforms as well as the environmental cycles and the distributions of these components and their significance to humans.

title: Home Planet Laboratory
description: Exercises designed to complement 1160. Applied problems in the spatial processes of the physical environment. Map construction and reading, weather and climatic analysis, classification of vegetative and soil associations, landform distribution analysis. Two hours lab.

title: People and Place
description: By focusing on issues of globalization, this course provides an overview of core concepts from human geography, including systematic analyses of economic, political, and cultural geography.

title: World Regional Geography
description: The regional geography of the world. Both physical and human aspects are studied along with current economic and political problems.

title: World of Beer
description:
This course will explore the complex contemporary and historical geography of beer. It examines the functions of beer and brewing from unique and sometimes overlapping spatial
perspectives. At the same time, this course provides an introduction to the discipline of Geography and its many different methods, theories, and technologies. After taking this course, students should have a good understanding of both beer AND geography, preparing them well to take other geography courses or to explore courses about alcohol/food in other disciplines.
One of the most unique features of the course is that it relies on a wide variety of guest lectures from the Department of GES and from the New Mexico brewing community. These speakers will highlight various perspectives that help us understand beer production as an expression of the human-environment relationship.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Information Design in Science and Society
description: Students learn to communicate complex, scientific data by designing infographics, and to analyze infographics as storytelling devices. This course builds information literacy and communication skills in an increasingly data-driven society.

title: Energy, Environment and Society
description: A look at the social, ethical, and environmental impacts of energy use both now and through history. A survey of renewable energy and conservation and their impact on environmental and social systems.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Field Methods in Geography
description:
Field-based learning experiences focused on the collection and analysis of natural phenomena. Methods for recording and analyzing climate, vegetation, biodiversity, and disturbance patterns are explored through field and laboratory experiences in the Albuquerque region. 
Prerequisite: 1160 and 1160L.

title: Global Climate Change
description:
(Also offered as EPS 352)
Comparison of natural and anthropogenic causes of large-scale climate change. Factors influencing development of mitigation of adaptation policies.
Prerequisite: MATH 1220 or MATH 1230 or MATH 1240 or MATH 1250 or MATH 1512.

title: Nature and Society
description: This course explores the human dimensions of geographical challenges through the traditions, actions and social organization of contemporary western and global/international human systems.

title: Land Use Management
description: Exercise of legal and political power over land and other resources. Resolution of conflicts between competing land users.

title: The Global Countryside
description: Course examines rural spaces in global context, rural development and agrarian transitions, rural-urban connections, and changing patterns of rural land use. Integrates critical development theory, decolonial theory, feminist theory, and other perspectives.

title: Law, Place and Space
description: This class examines the relationships between law and geography, interrogating how law shapes the human experience of place, and the ways that a variety of spatial categories inform the law.

title: Latin American Culture and Society
description: This course serves as an introduction to the cultures and societies of Latin America from an interdisciplinary perspective. The course surveys the region using materials drawn from both the humanities and social sciences.

title: Introduction to Geographic Information Systems
description: This course is an introduction to Geographic Information Systems (GIS), a tool for managing, integrating, analyzing, and visualizing geospatial data and information. GIS can be used to explore patterns and relationships in geographic data, seek explanations, and develop solutions to pressing problems. The foundations and theories of GIS will be emphasized.

title: Introduction to Geographic Information Systems Laboratory
description: This laboratory course introduces applied methods in Geographic Information Systems (GIS), a tool for managing, integrating, analyzing, and visualizing geospatial data and information. GIS is used to explore geographic data and develop solutions to pressing problems.

title: Geography Careers
description:
Applications of environmental analysis and geographic information technologies to a selected geographic problem. Field trips required. Recommended during the last semester for majors.
Restriction: senior standing.

title: Cartography
description: Learn to make great maps, to think visually, and to communicate spatial data in graphic formats.  Course emphasizes hands-on learning for an advanced introduction to the art and science of mapmaking.

title: Environmental Systems Modeling
description: Course assesses social-environmental challenges from the perspective of system dynamics. Students learn the basics of system structure and behavior, and apply these concepts in a hands-on, computer-based modeling project. Prerequisites: 380L and MATH 1430 or MATH 1512.

title: Critical Cartography
description: Explores the critical nature of maps and mapping, focusing on maps as tools of communication, action, and power. Evaluates histories of cartography and experiments with modern applications of mapping technology in community context.

title: Introductory Programming for GIS
description:
This course is intended to provide GIS software users with an introduction to Python, the de facto programming language of the GIS community.
Prerequisite: **381L.

title: Race and Geography
description: Course examines how relations of power--including race, gender, and sexuality--are mapped onto the body, how bodies can become sites for geographical inquiry, and how the body can become a place for resisting systems of oppression.

title: Environmental Management
description: Examination of critical issues of environmental degradation in global and local system related to: air and water pollution, soil erosion, deforestation, strip mining, over dependence on fossil fuels and improper management of toxic and other wastes. Appraisal of the conservation methods and policies applied to these issues and the outlook for the future.

title: Water Governance
description: An examination of the problems and trends in the use of water resources in the United States, with emphasis on the physical and social aspects related to its management.

title: Interdisciplinary Museum Science
description:
(Also offered as ANTH 444/544, BIOL 444/544, EPS 444/544, GEOG 544, MSST 444/544.) Interdisciplinary overview of how science museum resources are used to study change in human communities, natural, and physical systems over space and time. Course activities emphasize materials science, data science, and team science.
Restriction: instructor permission.

title: Sustainable Development
description: The role of global and regional governmental and non-governmental organizations in environmental politics and the process of their formation and change in response to environmental problems.

title: Hazards and Disasters
description: Natural and anthropogenic hazards and disasters are explored via field trips and student-led research and discussion. Topics include climate change, drought, floods, earthquakes, tsunamis, wildfire, hurricanes, disease, and invasive species.

title: Environmental Security: Energy
description: Course explores the systems that supply energy to modern societies, and identifies threats to energy availability, reliability, and affordability.  Students develop skills in the strategic, policy and planning, and sustainability dimensions of energy security.

title: Environmental Security: Food and Water
description: Course explores the systems that supply food and water in modern societies, analyzes threats to these systems, and assesses strategies and policies to improve food and water security at scales ranging from households to global.

title: Public Lands
description: Explore America's public lands system through case studies, expert interactions, and field trips. Learn how national parks, forests, and wilderness areas are managed while examining contemporary challenges including biodiversity protection, recreational access, and resource conflicts.

title: Food, Environment, and Society
description: Students gain an advanced introduction to the interrelated social and environmental factors shaping food systems locally and globally by considering forms of production, distribution, and consumption.

title: Environmental Justice
description: This course is designed to provide students with an interdisciplinary approach to understand environmental issues through an environmental justice lens. This course of study invites students to critically analyze examples of environmental justice cases and to develop an understanding of the complex relationships among actors and the social, political, and economic processes that lead to environmental injustices. This course begins with an examination of the foundational principles and history of the environmental justice movement and includes the concepts of justice, gender, race, and class.

title: The City
description: This class examines the vectors of difference - cultural, economic, political, legal and environmental - that animate urban form and life. Class involves fieldwork.

title: Environments and Peoples in Latin America
description: An introduction to the societies of Latin America from a geographical and interdisciplinary perspective, this course focuses on how environments, people, and culture are active forces that continuously shape each other.

title: Geography of New Mexico and the Southwest
description: This course introduces students to human and cultural geographies in the Southwest United States, focusing on New Mexico.

title: Field Experience Abroad
description: This field course focuses on food and craft production as related to geography, sustainability and development. Topics include indigenous culture, food systems and globalization. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Remote Sensing Fundamentals
description: This course introduces theoretical, technical, and applied aspects of remote sensing to monitor and manage earth resources. Students will obtain, process, and visualize imagery for real-world environmental applications. This is intended to be a solid foundation for advanced remote sensing and GIS coursework you may choose to engage in later in your academic or professional career.

title: Remote Sensing Fundamentals Lab
description: This lab course introduces technical and applied aspects of remote sensing to monitor and manage earth resources.

title: Applications of Remote Sensing
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the principles and concepts of remote sensing and explore its applications specific to the environment and natural resource management.

title: Applications of Remote Sensing Lab
description: This laboratory course is designed to introduce students to the principles and concepts of remote sensing and explore its applications specific to the environment and natural resource management.

title: Interactive Web Mapping
description:
Students will learn the fundamentals of web design and development with HTML, CSS, and Javascript, and bring their professional maps to life on the web as interactive experiences.
Prerequisite: **381L or instructor permission.

title: Applied GIS and Spatial Analysis
description: Introduces GIS and spatial analytical methods and selected applications of these methods in anthropology, business, crime, ecology, engineering, health, planning, water resources and others. Covers analytical techniques specific to selected applications.

title: Geocomputation and Spatial Modeling
description:
Introduces theory, concepts, and methods of spatial modeling and geocomputation techniques involving computerized vector and raster-based modeling. Includes a lab component that covers the use of GIS and other software to carry out modeling projects.
Prerequisite: **381L.

title: GIS Concepts and Techniques
description:
Eelected advanced concepts and techniques in Geographic Information Systems. Includes a lab component that provides students with the opportunity to apply concepts and techniques in a hands-on manner. Fee required.
Prerequisite: **381L.

title: Problems
description: Supervised individual study and field work. Must be taken for 6 credit hours in the departmental honors program.

title: Internship in Applied Geography
description: Written field analysis of a project coordinated between student, faculty and public or private manager. Credits to be determined by supervising faculty.

title: Topics in Geography
description: Course explores a topic not covered in the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students. Topics will be identified in the schedule of classes. Credit can be applied by majors to department degree requirements; consult with academic advisor.

title: Topics in Geography
description: Specific topics in geography which relate contemporary issues to the discipline. Topics will be noted in the appropriate schedule of classes. Credit can be applied by majors to the appropriate department group requirements for the degree.

title: Geographic History and Methods
description: Examines, evaluates, and criticizes the methods geographers have used to analyze the relationship between humankind and the environment.

title: Approaches to Geographic Research
description: Introduces the basic elements of research design and proposal writing, focusing on examples from current geographic research. Prerequisite: 501.

title: Human Dimensions of Climate Change
description: This course explores the interdisciplinary nature of natural resource challenges.  Topics will vary each semester.  Field trips will be included to investigate issues relevant to the class.

title: Seminar in Geographies of Power
description: This seminar explores how power and space together shape contemporary sociocultural, political, and ecological worlds. Focal topics and theoretical approaches will vary each semester.

title: Seminar: Globalization and Development
description: This course investigates development and globalization as inter-related processes that have transformed many societies and created a global economy and society. It grapples with ideas of development from different perspectives, including geography, anthropology, and economics.

title: Political Ecology
description: This seminar surveys the field of political ecology. Students critically explore theories and cases that illuminate relationships between political economies and environmental change.

title: Environmental Systems Modeling
description: Course assesses social-environmental challenges from the perspective of system dynamics. Students learn the basics of system structure and behavior, and apply these concepts in a hands-on, computer-based modeling project.

title: Advanced GIScience Seminar
description:
Examination of current trends in Geographic Information Science, including technical, social, institutional and legal issues.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Critical Cartography
description: Explores the critical nature of maps and mapping, focusing on maps as tools of communication, action, and power. Evaluates histories of cartography and experiments with modern applications of mapping technology in community context.

title: Introductory Programming for GIS
description:
This course is intended to provide GIS software users with an introduction to Python, the de facto programming language of the GIS community.
Prerequisite: **381L or 581L.

title: Race and Geography
description: Course examines how relations of power--including race, gender, and sexuality--are mapped onto the body, how bodies can become sites for geographical inquiry, and how the body can become a place for resisting systems of oppression.

title: Environmental Management
description: Examination of critical issues of environmental degradation in global and local system related to: air and water pollution, soil erosion, deforestation, strip mining, over dependence on fossil fuels and improper management of toxic and other wastes. Appraisal of the conservation methods and policies applied to these issues and the outlook for the future.

title: Water Governance
description: In this class, we view political considerations as inherent in water management and unavoidable. This focus on politics before technical water resource manipulation is what we call water governance, compared to traditional "water resource management".

title: Interdisciplinary Museum Science
description:
(Also offered as ANTH 444/544, BIOL 444/544, EPS 444/544, GEOG 444, MSST 444/544.) Interdisciplinary overview of how science museum resources are used to study change in human communities, natural, and physical systems over space and time. Course activities emphasize materials science, data science, and team science.
Restriction: instructor permission.

title: Governing the Global Environment
description: The role of global and regional governmental and non-governmental organizations in environmental politics and the process of their formation and change in response to environmental problems.

description: Course explores the systems that supply energy to modern societies, and identifies threats to energy availability, reliability, and affordability.  Students develop skills in the strategic, policy and planning, and sustainability dimensions of energy security.

title: Environmental Security: Food and Water
description: Course explores the systems that supply food and water in modern societies, analyzes threats to these systems, and assesses strategies and policies to improve food and water security at scales ranging from households to global.

title: Food and Natural Resources
description: Students gain an advanced introduction to the interrelated social and environmental factors shaping food systems locally and globally by considering forms of production, distribution, and consumption.

This course of study invites students to critically analyze examples of environmental justice cases and to develop an understanding of the complex relationships among actors and the social, political, and economic processes that lead to environmental injustices. This course begins with an examination of the foundational principles and history of the environmental justice movement and includes the concepts of justice, gender, race, and class.

description: An introduction to the societies of Latin America from a geographical and interdisciplinary perspective, this course focuses on how environments, people, and culture are active forces that continuously shape each other.

title: Geography of New Mexico and the Southwest
description: This course introduces students to human and cultural geographies in the Southwest United States, focusing on New Mexico.

title: Spatial Statistics
description: Introduces fundamental statistical and quantitative modeling techniques widely used in geography. Emphasizes geographic examples and spatial problems. Includes a lab component that covers the use of statistical software in geographic analysis. Fee required.

title: Introduction to GIS for Graduate Students
description: Introduces the concepts underlying Geographic Information Systems and its utilization for the input, storage, manipulation, query, display, and analysis of geographical data. Includes a lab component that covers the range of analytical techniques available in current software. Fee required.

Students will obtain, process, and visualize imagery for real-world environmental applications. This is intended to be a solid foundation for advanced remote sensing and GIS coursework you may choose to engage in later in your academic or professional career.

title: Remote Sensing Fundamentals Lab
description: This lab course introduces technical and applied aspects of remote sensing to monitor and manage earth resources.

title: Applications of Remote Sensing
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the principles and concepts of remote sensing and explore its applications specific to the environment and natural resource management.

title: Applications of Remote Sensing Lab
description: This laboratory course is designed to introduce students to the principles and concepts of remote sensing and explore its applications specific to the environment and natural resource management.

title: Interactive Web Mapping
description: Students will learn the fundamentals of web design and development with HTML, CSS, and Javascript, and bring their professional maps to life on the web as interactive experiences. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Applied GIS and Spatial Analysis
description: Introduces GIS and spatial analytical methods and selected applications of these methods in anthropology, business, crime, ecology, engineering, health, planning, water resources and others. Covers analytical techniques specific to selected applications.

title: Geocomputation and Spatial Modeling
description:
Introduces theory, concepts, and methods of spatial modeling and geocomputation techniques involving computerized vector and raster-based modeling. Includes a lab component that covers the use of GIS and other software to carry out modeling projects.
Prerequisite: **381L or 581L.

title: GIS Concepts and Techniques
description:
Eelected advanced concepts and techniques in Geographic Information Systems. Includes a lab component that provides students with the opportunity to apply concepts and techniques in a hands-on manner. Fee required.
Prerequisite: **381L or 581L.

title: Qualitative Methods
description: This course is designed to expose students to the underlying theories, purpose, scope, and procedures of qualitative research, especially as applied to human geography.

title: Problems
description: Supervised individual study and field work.

title: Internship in Applied Geography
description: Written field analysis of a project coordinated between student, faculty and public or private manager. Credits to be determined by supervising faculty.

title: Master's Project
description:
Development of an advanced project in geographical research under the supervision of a graduate committee. A grade of CR is earned if the project is approved by faculty committee. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Prerequisite: 501 and 502. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Geography
description: Specific topics in geography which relate contemporary issues to the discipline. Topics will be noted in the appropriate schedule of classes. Credit can be applied to the appropriate department group requirements for the degree.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introduction to Geographic Theory and Application
description: Overview of geographic history and philosophy, with emphasis on relationship between theory and practice. Includes virtual and in-person meetings with students from both UNM and NMSU.

title: Integrative Research Design
description:
Introduction to research design in geography, with emphasis on the integration of qualitative and quantitative methods. Includes virtual and in-person meetings with students from both UNM and NMSU.
Prerequisite: 601.

title: Professional Geographic Practice
description:
This professional development course focuses on communication, ethics, grantwriting, and applied geographical work in the student's area of interest.
Prerequisite: 602.

title: Independent Study
description:
Student works under faculty supervision to conduct independent readings or research in the faculty member's area of specialization.
Restriction: must be admitted to a PhD program.

title: Supervised Research
description:
Student works under faculty supervision to prepare for doctoral candidacy requirements such as Ph.D. comprehensive exam, research proposal, and funding applications.
Restriction: admitted to PhD Geography.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Prerequisite: 601.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Health Information Technology I
description: An introduction to health technology, with topics to include: the history and regulation of medical record documentation, public health initiatives, the structure and language of healthcare delivery, reimbursement and billing practices, electronic health records and coding, patient privacy and security, and certification processes in health technology.

title: Basic ICD-CPT Coding
description: Introduction to coding is a focus upon foundational coding knowledge, the application of current coding guidelines, health record documentation analysis, and regulatory compliance. Students will master the intricacies of ICD-10-CM, ICD-10-PCS, CPT, and HCPCS coding systems, emphasizing accuracy and adherence to official guidelines. The course delves into the critical analysis of health record documentation, ensuring it supports diagnoses, reflects patient progress, and meets legal and regulatory requirements. Students will also explore the roles of various healthcare providers and disciplines in maintaining comprehensive and accurate documentation Dental coding and 24/7 nursing facility coding methodologies are introduced. A significant portion of the curriculum is dedicated to understanding and applying domains, specifically Data Content, Structure & Standards; Informatics, Analytics and Data Use; and Compliance. This includes mastering diagnostic and procedural groupings (DRG, MS-DRG, APC, RUGS), evaluating coding accuracy, analyzing health record content, and ensuring compliance with HIPAA, The Joint Commission, and other relevant regulations. Students will also learn about physician query processes, analytics and decision support, and career opportunities in Health Information Management.

title: Intermediate Medical Coding
description: Advance your coding skills with in-depth practice in ICD-10-CM, ICD-10-PCS, HCPCS, and CPT. This course tackles complex inpatient cases, explores specialized coding areas including psychiatric and dental coding, and emphasizes mastery of coding resources and modifiers. Gain a comprehensive understanding of payment methodologies and prepare for professional certification exams (CPC, CCS/CCS-P) through rigorous case studies and practical application of coding guidelines.

title: Advanced Medical Coding and Clinical Documentation Integrity
description: This advanced course is designed for experienced coders seeking to enhance their expertise in complex medical coding scenarios. Participants will delve into the intricacies of assigning accurate and compliant codes across various healthcare settings, including inpatient, outpatient, and specialized facilities such as dental coding and skilled nursing facilities coding.

title: Medical-Legal and Quality Management
description: This course provides a comprehensive study of legal and ethical principles governing health information management, with a focus on ensuring the quality of health information and its ethical use. Students will examine the medical record as a legal document, explore standards and regulations governing medical records in various settings, and analyze methods used by healthcare facilities to assure the provision of high-quality medical care. The course will cover legal processes, external forces, and legislation impacting health information, including issues such as professional liability, informed consent, privacy and security laws, the legal health record, and workplace legalities. A variety of ethical issues in health care will also be explored.

title: Healthcare Reimbursement Methodologies
description: This course covers policies, regulations, and standards as they pertain to revenue cycle management. Reimbursement methodologies and regulatory requirements pertaining to the prospective payment system and nomenclatures, health care insurers and contracts, case-mix, and conducting patient billing activities for a variety of healthcare organizations are included. Also included, are basic instructions for filing various types of insurances.

title: Health Information Technology I
description:
An overview of health records services in the United States and the role of the health record practitioner. It introduces the student to the principles and practices of the health records profession. 
Restriction: admitted to A.S. Health Information Technology.

title: Principles of Disease
description:
Introduction to human pathophysiology for non-nurs-ing health professionals. Content includes: normal pathophysiology over the lifespan, abnormal pathophysiology, cellular function, immunity, gastrointestinal, nervous, skeletal, and integumentary systems. 
Prerequisite: HCHS 111 and HCHS 113.

title: Advanced OP Coding
description:
Covers the development and format of CPT and ICD. Included are instructions for basic coding guidelines, descriptions and definitions of symbols, correct usage of modifiers, overviews of the appendices in the CPT manual and guidelines for using the index. Use of encoder software will be included.
Prerequisite: 211.

title: Advanced IP Coding
description: Practice assigning ICD-10-CM and ICD-10-PCS codes using inpatient diagnosis, procedures and reports, using the 3M encoder.

title: Health Information Technology II
description:
This course covers supervisory principles and electronic medical records, including collection, arrangement, presentation and verification of healthcare data. Also included are reimbursement methodologies, confidentiality rules and regulations, and uses of coded data. 
Prerequisite: 121.

title: Computer Applications and Statistics in Healthcare
description: This course presents the development of health information systems, computer applications in the health-care industry, and methods used to control accuracy and security of information. Included are the concepts and procedures used in preparation of statistical reports including vital statistics, census systems and commonly computed rates and percentages.

title: Professional Practice Experience
description:
In this course the student applies skills, concepts and theory from previous classroom experiences in various healthcare environments. Students are under the direction of faulty and health information preceptors. 
Pre- or corequisite: satisfactory completion of all HCHT program core courses.

title: Medical Terminology
description: An introduction to terminology used in health careers. It will provide a basic knowledge of prefixes, suffixes, and root words used in describing anatomical parts of the human body as well as general terms relating to disease processes.

title: Basic Body Structure and Function I
description: This is an introductory course in Anatomy and Physiology for students from diverse backgrounds and varying levels of educational preparation. No prior knowledge of biology or chemistry is assumed.

title: Human Growth and Development
description: This course presents theories of biological, cognitive and psychosocial development across the life span. This course will stress the application of these concepts to the health care field.

title: Pathophysiology for Health Sciences
description: Introduction to the nature of disease and its effect on body systems. Disease processes affecting the human body via an integrated approach to specific disease entities will be presented including a review of normal functions of the appropriate body systems. Diseases will be studied in relation to their etiology, pathology, physical signs and symptoms, diagnostic procedures, complications, treatment modalities and prognosis.

title: Phlebotomy
description:
Introduction to the practice of phlebotomy, blood specimen collection, and processing. Content areas consist of: general safety and universal precautions, professionalism and ethics, equipment and supplies, anticoagulants, site selection, vein puncture and capillary puncture techniques, and specimen processing. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Pharmacology For Health Occupations
description: Presents an introduction of responsibilities and principles of drug administration, classifications of drugs, usage, contraindications, dosage, and computations.

title: Introduction To Substance Abuse
description: Introduction to physiological, psychological and socio-cultural dimensions of substance abuse assesment, intervention, and treatment. Overview of emerging indicators of effective substance abuse treatment and prevention with diverse populations. {Fall}

title: Independent Study
description:
Designed for study of specific topics, issues and problems in health sciences.
Restriction: permission of Department Chair.

title: Topics
description: Study of specific topics related to the health sciences.

title: Topics II
description:
Study of specific topics related to the health sciences. 
Restriction: permission of Department chair.

title: American Heart Association Heartsaver® First Aid & CPR/AED
description: A video-based, instructor-led course that teaches students critical skills needed to respond to and manage an emergency until emergency medical services arrive. Skills covered in this course include first aid; choking relief in adults, children, and infants; and what to do for sudden cardiac arrest in adults, children, and infants. Upon successful completion of the course, students will become eligible for AHA Heartsaver® First Aid & CPR/AED certification.

title: First Aid and CPR
description: Preparation in practice to meet needs and situations when basic first aid is needed. Students eligible for standard first aid certification and CPR Certificate.

title: American Heart Association CPR
description: The gross anatomy and physiology of the heart, electrical pathway, and respiratory system are discussed in preparation for CPR. Includes primary assessment and evaluation of the C-A-B sequence. Successful completion of this course will result in American Heart Association CPR certification.

title: Personal Health Management
description: An introduction to the major areas of information that help humans achieve, maintain, and promote positive health. Topics covered include nutrition, mental and physical health, drugs, human sexuality, prevention and control of diseases and injury, nutrition, and societal and environmental impacts on health.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Consumer Health Education
description: Acquire the knowledge and skills related to consumer health. Learn how to choose wisely from the menu of health products and services that affect you and your family’s health.

title: Foundations of Health Promotion
description: For those considering becoming health majors or minors in school health or community health. Exploration of the basic philosophy and fundamental practices currently utilized in health education.

title: HIV and Disease Prevention
description: This course will cover the HIV/AIDS epidemic with awareness including basic information, prevention, history, compassion, legal issues, testing, and societal implications.

title: Sexual Health Promotion and Education
description: This course provides an introduction and basic knowledge about human sexuality including anatomical, physiological, psycho-social, and ethical components. Reproduction, contraception, sexually transmitted disease, sexual health and sexual dysfunctions are among areas examined.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Conflict Mediation
description: The course will cover methods of resolving conflict situations and methods of preventing conflict. Students will learn to design educational strategies that can be implemented as part of a classroom curriculum.

title: Injury Prevention
description: The course content will include specific strategies for preventing unintentional injuries in young children. Students will examine specific principles for the development of new strategies that will address unintentional injuries.

title: Violence Prevention
description: The course will examine strategies that have been successful in preventing violence. Students will examine the literature to understand the principles to use in the development of strategies for the prevention of violence.

title: Emotional Health and Interpersonal Relationships
description: Course will examine a psychological framework that is the cause of a dysfunctional lifestyle and create a psychological framework that can result in the improvement in the quality of living.

title: Introduction to Health Behavior Theory
description: The course will provide an understanding of theoretical principles of various health behavior theories that explain the adoption of health-compromising and health-promoting behaviors.

title: Introduction to Public and Community Health
description: Introduction to the field of public and community health, origins and design of the U.S. public health system and its role in population based health.

title: Principles of Epidemiology
description: Prepares students to design and implement evidence-based interventions for disease prevention and health promotion in public health settings.

title: Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of health education faculty member.

title: Strategies for Prevention of Substance Use Disorder
description: Examines basic principles for the development of effective strategies for substance use prevention programs for youth. Evidence of principles used in successful programs from research literature will be provided.

title: Health Promotion Program Planning
description: This course provides a comprehensive study of the practical and theoretical skills needed to plan, implement and evaluate health promotion programs in a variety of settings. Emphasis will be placed on the major components of a sound planning model: needs/asset assessment; priority setting; evidence-based strategies; program goals and objectives; program implementation; and program evaluation. We will also study ethical issues, health equity and diversity as they relate to community health program planning and evaluation.

title: Methods in Community Health Education
description: This course examines prevention strategies effective for disease prevention and health promotion at the organizational and community level. Students will develop specific skill-sets tailored at various intervention levels. Case studies from multiple settings such as school, clinical, worksite, and the broader community will be examined.

title: Health Issues in Death and Dying
description: An introduction to content in the area of death and dying: the dying process, grief, types and alternatives to funerals, out-of-body experiences, types of death and community resources available for support.

title: Stress Management
description: Deals with multiple causes of stress and its resolutions. Emphasizes chief stressors of adults, self-responsibility for change, holistic approach, emotional/mental methods of stress reduction.

title: Social Determinants and Multicultural Health
description: Course provides an overview of the health beliefs of people in New Mexico with anl emphasis towards Latino/Hispanics, Native Americans, African Americans, Asians, and Anglos.  Examines health behavior as influenced by health beliefs and practices.

title: Thriving with Homeopathy
description: This course facilitates the student and present and future healthcare practitioner of integrated medicine as well as the student of the nursing programs with an in-depth understanding of homeopathy, its philosophy, and practical application. The student will also be introduced to the interrelationship between the Mind, Body, and Soul with their intricate communication channels and unlimited potentialities. Homeopathy is a complete therapeutic system of medicine that aims to promote general health and thus reinforces the body’s natural healing capacity. This most creative intelligence, inherent in us all, functions to heal its host to its highest potential.

title: Being and Living Quantum Healing
description: This course facilitates the student and healthcare practitioner of integrated medicine with an in-depth understanding and application of the interrelationship between the Mind, Body, and Soul with their intricate communication channels and unlimited potentialities. The curriculum is designed with beauty and mindfulness, and not only serves the student with a wealth of information but also as a “feast of the senses”, welcoming the participant to experience and feel the presented study material as a means to personal transformation.

title: Integrated Health Coaching
description:
This course is an evidence-based approach to integrative health as well as the core competencies,
skills, and tools grounded in well-respected theories regarding the psychology of change and
well-being, students gain knowledge and practice to facilitate and empower the client to
achieve self-determined skills related to health and wellness.

title: Integrated Mental Health
description: Integrative mental health care is a rapidly emerging paradigm that combines traditional medicine, psychotherapy, and a range of evidence-based CAM (complementary and alternative) therapies. This course will explore advances in the understanding of the causes of mental illness which have important implications for therapeutic uses in the treatment of depression, anxiety, dementia, Alzheimer’s disease, ADHD, and other mental health problems.

title: Physical Activity and Aging
description: Concerned with the process of aging as it affects physical activity and the potential of physical activity in adjustment to the process of aging.

title: Workshop
description: Faculty-supervised intensive and interactive program focusing on the development of specific skills.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Field Experience
description: This field experience is designed as a culminating experience to provide students an opportunity to apply health education and health promotion knowledge and skills in a professionally supervised setting.

title: Health Behavior Theory
description: This course explores multiple theories and models and their application in the development of health promotion programs to support change within individuals, families and communities.

title: Research Design in HPER
description: (Also offered as PEP 507) Emphasizes an understanding of different research designs, their level of sophistication and their application from both a theoretical and practical point of view.

title: Management of Health Promotion Programs
description: Provides student with program management and administrative skills such as leadership style, communication techniques, problem solving, motivational strategies, budgeting, goal setting, grant writing essentials, and evaluation pertaining to the field of school and community health.

title: Advanced Community Health Education Research
description: An overview of community health, its role in public health and current trends in community-based research.  Examines different methodologies for conducting academic and community-based research partnerships.

title: Community Health Education Program Planning, Development and Evaluation
description: This course is intended to provide students with an advanced proficiency in health education program planning, development, implementation, and evaluation. Focus will be placed on the further understanding, application, and potential research related to the competencies of a health education specialist.

title: Epidemiological Principles for Health Educators
description: Designed to introduce students to statistics of diseases. Course surveys various research designs used in discovering and tracking diseases as they affect a human population.

title: Measurement and Evaluation in Health Promotion
description: Designed to provide graduate students in Health Promotion and related fields: competencies in major measurement/evaluation systems in HP and HE.

title: Stress Management
description: Explores multiple causes of stress and its resolutions. Emphasizes chief stressors of adults, self-responsibility for change, holistic approach, emotional/mental methods of stress reduction.

title: Advanced Multicultural Health Education Research and Applications
description: Course focuses on conducting a critical analysis of health literature using various databases and social cultural frameworks for analyzing health issues as influenced by cultural health beliefs in conducting research with diverse and multicultural communities.

title: Physical Activity and Aging
description: Concerned with the process of aging as it affects physical activity and the potential of physical activity in adjustment to the process of aging.

title: Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of Health Education faculty member.

title: Workshop
description: Faculty-supervised intensive and interactive program focusing on the development of specific skills.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Advanced Field Experiences
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
Restriction: admitted to M.S. Health Education and permission of fieldwork supervisor.

title: Directed Readings in Health Education
description:
A faculty-directed exploration of a specific topic through reading, discussion, and critique.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Internship
description:
Practical experience in a public, private or non-profit organization, supervised by a professional with an academic component overseen by a UNM instructor.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introduction to Fundamentals of Refrigeration
description:
Demonstrate the ability to perform HVAC/R Technician duties in a safe manner. Accurately perform HVAC/R related calculations and interpret results for the purpose of diagnosis, repair, or installation of HVAC/R equipment and systems. Professionally communicate in oral and written forms. Demonstrate the use of current industry techniques including tools, testing equipment, manufacturers’ apps. Determine the appropriate ethical action that should occur in a given
circumstance. Work effectively in a team-based environment. Possess a mastery of the refrigeration cycle and its components

title: Refrigeration Management
description: This course will stress the accepted practices and proper procedures to handle refrigerate materials. The course will educate the student in the proper procedures for safely conducting leak detection, evacuation, recovery and charging systems. Students will prepare for and take the Universal CFC Refrigerant Handlers Certification exam, which is required by the EPA. This certification is required to handle and service all types of refrigeration equipment containing and using refrigerants. Students must pass the exam with a 71% and be able to successfully perform evacuation, recovery, and charging of systems for Type 1, Type 2 and Type 3 procedures.

title: Motors and Controls
description: Covers primary and control circuits in various applications, troubleshooting and components. Emphasizes attention to motors and starting devices.

title: Introduction to Sheet Metal Fabrication
description: Introduction to sheet metal fabrication to include hands-on practical laboratory applications, cutting and forming procedures, identifying types and gauges. Design and layout techniques.

title: Gas Heating Furnaces
description: The study and application of gas furnaces including installation, operation, service, maintenance and controls. The students will learn about natural gas, and electric heating systems used for residential and/or light commercial heating systems including furnace and boiler package systems and alternative heating sources. Highlights electrical and electronic trouble shooting, service, maintenance, repair and replacement of residential and light commercial heating systems. The course will include service, maintenance and troubleshooting.

title: Pre-Health Pathways Topics Course
description: This course explores selected topics in Pre-Health Pathways in Preparation for Professional Health Careers with a focus on preparing students for applying for and engaging in professional health programs and practice. Course content will vary by semester and may address themes such as: exploring health careers and programs, exposure to basic clinical skills, culture in healthcare, cultural humility, growth mindset, healthcare in New Mexico communities including health equity, application preparation, leadership including volunteering hours, and thinking skills. Students engage in readings, discussions, applied activities, and projects.

title: Meanings and Culture in Health and Illness
description: In this course, students will develop a more complex and nuanced understanding of meanings, cultures, and social factors influencing health and illness. The course will explore the role culture and meanings play in the conceptualization, experience, and treatment of health and illness, as well as, healthcare interactions and outcomes. Cultural humility and respect are foundational concepts in this course. Students should expect to receive a health-focused foundational understanding of the complexity of meanings, culture, and society with an eye toward application both personally and professionally in their healthcare career. Additionally, students will expand their understanding, respect, skill, and humility in approaching individuals with different cultural frameworks from their own.

title: Integration of Psychology and Medicine
description: Explore the importance of integrating psychology into healthcare and gain skill development in patient communication especially about difficult or uncomfortable topics.  This two-tiered course will explore the importance of integrating psychology into healthcare delivery, as well as, provide skill development opportunities in patient communication.  Many professional health students struggle with patient interactions about difficult or uncomfortable topics.  After a brief review of foundational psychology theories, students will explore how behavioral health impacts medical conditions and outcomes.

title: Critical Thinking, Learning, and Problem Solving for Health Professions
description: Specific and explicit instruction on critical thinking and problem solving in healthcare through group practice, discussion, and practical application in critical and integrative thinking using a variety of scenarios, case studies, and examples.

title: Communication in Action for Health Professions
description: The importance of communication skill cannot be overstated especially in healthcare.  This rigorous course is heavily weighted on skill development and engagement with a focus on the development of engaged, respectful, and meaningful communication.  Students will deepen their understanding of the components of meaningful communication including engaged listening, narrative, metaphors, and facework.   The praxis of communication elements will focus on narrative medicine, motivational interviewing, writing, and interview skills.  Students should expect to apply content learned from HSCI400,  HSCI401, and HSCI402.

title: Pre-Health Observational Learning
description: In-person observational learning provides opportunities for students to engage their whole being within a context in a way that can facilitate lifelong deep learning.  This course is designed to help students make deep connections between what they have learned in classrooms about social determinants of health, culture, and health professions with real world contexts in both cognitive and affective ways.  The two components of this course: shadowing and community engagement aim to provide students with meaningful experiences in their pre-health preparation.

The course will explore the role culture and meanings play in the conceptualization, experience, and treatment of health and illness, as well as, healthcare interactions and outcomes.
