title: Primary Care of Pediatrics II
description:
This course focuses on management of common primary health care problems of children birth through adolescence, and provides a framework for integrating concepts from the clinical sciences and current evidence base into diagnosis and management. 
Prerequisite: 542. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Women's Health
description:
Theories and concepts applied in the promotion of the health of adolescent and adult women. Clinical component is specialty-specific.  
Prerequisite: 526 and 540.  
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Intrapartum Care
description:
Management of labor and birth, triage of complications and cultural dimensions foundational to the nurse-midwifery model of intrapartum care are studied.  
Prerequisite: 544 and 548.

title: Newborn Care
description:
Study of the normal neonate within the cultural structure of the family. Common physiological, pathological problems and their management by nurse-midwife emphasized. Clinical component is specialty-specific. For students pursuing an advanced clinical nursing concentration.
Prerequisite: 526 and 540.

title: Evidence-Based Care in Nurse Midwifery
description:
This course focuses on skill-building in the assessment of the quality and relevance of clinical research in obstetrics and midwifery. Evaluation of the current science base and identification of biases and weaknesses therein are required to articulate and support options in women's health care. Current research topics are explored from historical and scientific perspectives. 
Prerequisite: 544 and 548. Corequisite: 550 and 551.

title: Nurse-Midwifery Professional Practice
description: This advanced class in nurse-midwifery standards of professional practice analyzes variations based upon populations, geography, practice teams and delivery systems. Historical and ethical frames are used in the analysis of clinical, organization and international issues.

title: The Evidence Base for APRN Primary Care Practice
description: The focus of this course is on analysis of clinical research in primary care practice, including identification of biases and limitations, allowing students to articulate best practices and support clinical decision making in primary care. Prerequisite: 535 and 542.

title: Management of the Acutely Ill Adult-Gerontology Patient
description:
This course focuses on the acutely ill adult/gerontology patient. Emphasis is placed on acute and episodic health problems common to the adult/gerontology patient.
Prerequisite: 564. 
Pre- or corequisite: 505 and 566. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. Nursing.

title: AG-ACNP Practicum I
description:
Introductory skills and concepts of acutely ill adult patients. Evaluation and management of common disease processes of adults in the acute care setting will be included.  
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Managment of the Complex and Chronically Ill Adult-Gerotonology Patient
description:
This course focuses on the complex and chronically ill adult/gerontology patient. Emphasis is placed on common complex and chronic health problems. 
Prerequisite: 555 and 566. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: AG-ACNP Practicum II
description:
This clinical course focuses on the diagnosis, management, and continued care of the complex, acute, and critically ill adult/gerontology patient.  
Prerequisite: 561 and 562. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Health Promotion, Protection, and Disease Prevention for Acute, Critical or Chronically Ill Adults
description:
This course focuses on health promotion, protection and disease prevention in the acutely ill, critical or chronically ill adult. Health promotion strategies, risk stratification, and disease prevention and protection concepts are included. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Management of the Critically Ill Adult-Gerontology Patient
description:
This course focuses on the critical ill adult/gerontology patient. Emphasis is placed on management of critical illnesses.
Pre- or corequisite: 563. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. Nursing.

title: Advanced Diagnostic and Therapeutic Skills
description:
This course focuses on skills such as diagnostic and technical interventions used to diagnose, sustain, or stabilize the acutely ill, complex/chronically ill and/or critically ill adult/gerontology patient. 
Prerequisite: 501 and 503 and 526 and 540 and 543. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: ACNP Clinical Topics I:
description:
Covers content related to areas of advanced practice that require specialized training and skills. Student chooses from operating room RN first assistant, flight nursing, disaster management, or specialized acute care topics approved by the instructor. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: ACNP Clinical Topics II:
description:
Builds on ACNP Clinical Topics I. The student continues in the chosen topic of operating room RN first assistant, flight nursing, disaster management, or in the specialized acute care topic approved by the instructor. 
Prerequisite: 568. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Psychotherapy, Behavior Change, and Health Promotion-Disease Prevention Across the Lifespan
description:
Theoretical, foundational knowledge of individual, group, and family therapy, health promotion, disease prevention across the lifespan with focus on motivational interviewing, behavior change, use of cognitive, behavioral, dialectical, solution focused, play, and reminiscence therapy.
Prerequisite: 584. 
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Integrated Behavioral Health Care and Common Psychiatric Presentations
description:
Overview of behavioral health assessment in common psychiatric disorders and medical conditions with psychiatric presentations in patients across the lifespan with a focus on integrated care settings, interdisciplinary communication, care coordination within a trauma-informed setting.
Prerequisite: 526 and 540. Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Advanced Assessment, Neurobiology, and Psychopharmacology Across the Lifespan
description:
Integration of current neurobiological and psychopharmacological theory and research to assessment of symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment of psychiatric disorders across the lifespan.
Prerequisite: 583 and 584. 
Corequisite: 588.
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Diagnosis and Management of Adults for the PMHNP
description:
Evidence-based assessment, diagnosis, health promotion, management, and evaluation of adults with mental illnesses and addictions by the Psychiatric Mental Health Nurse Practitioner. Emphasis on neurobiology, complex psychopharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments, and culturally-sensitive interventions.
Prerequisite: 585. 
Corequisite: 589. 
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Diagnosis and Management of Children and Older Adults for the PMHNP
description:
Psychiatric-mental health assessment, diagnosis, health promotion, management, and evaluation of children, adolescents, and older adults for the PMHNP. Emphasis on complex individual, family, and group therapies, neurobiology, psychopharmacological treatment, and developmentally and culturally-sensitive interventions.
Prerequisite: 586. 
Corequisite: 595. 
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Advanced Practicum I
description:
The first in a 3 course clinical sequence for PMHNP students. Minimum of 150 hours supervised clinical practice working with a preceptor to evaluate patients, develop management plans, and facilitate individual and group therapies.

Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Advanced Practicum II
description:
The second in a 3 course clinical sequence for PMHNP students. Minimum 150 hours supervised clinical practice working with preceptor to develop higher level competencies in evaluation and management of patients across the lifespan.
Prerequisite: 588. 
Corequisite: 586. 
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Clinical Specialty Practicum
description:
This course provides application of advanced knowledge of health assessment, pathophysiology, and pharmacology to a clinical specialty site while supervised by a clinician. Principles of quality improvement and caring for vulnerable populations will be explored.
Prerequisite: 527.

title: Topics
description: Specialized courses about a particular topic in nursing. A variety of topic courses are offered according to demand. Different sections indicate different topic content.

title: Advanced Practice Seminar
description: The focus of the course is entry into practice for the advanced practice nurse. Prerequisite: students must have completed the majority of their clinical courses specific to their concentration. Coordinators for the advance practice nursing concentrations must approve students  admission into this course.

title: Advanced Fieldwork for the Healthcare Educator
description:
A minimum of 4 field work credits is required. Taken after core and specialty required courses in the concentration have been completed. Students enroll with faculty in specialty area. Faculty member oversees experience and monitors students progress. 3 lab hours per week, per credit. 
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Applied Examination
description:
Comprehensive examination taken during the final term of the course of study for completion of Plan II requirements. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Nursing Thesis I
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only. Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Philosophical Foundations of Nursing Science
description:
This course prepares PhD students in nursing and health sciences to analyze and evaluate scientific practice through a variety of philosophical perspectives. Epistemological, logical, cultural, and historical foundations of modern sciences and scientific methods are emphasized.
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or permission of instructor.

title: Theoretical Foundations of Research
description: This course focuses on the role of theory in the design and interpretation of research. Emphasis is placed upon mid-range, multidisciplinary theories relevant to research and the application of theory to the student's research. Prerequisite: 600. Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or permission of instructor.

title: Quantitative Methods in Health Research
description:
This course focuses on the critical analysis and application of quantitative research designs and methods used to study clinical problems and health policy issues. Emphasis is on survey methods, non-experimental, quasi-experimental and experimental designs. 
Prerequisite: 620.

title: Qualitative Methods in Health Research
description:
Exploration of major methodological traditions and techniques of qualitative research and their applications to knowledge development in nursing and health research. 
Restriction: admitted to M.S.N. or Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or NURCP Nursing Certificate.

title: Rural and Cultural Health
description:
Rural and cultural health analyzes unique characteristics, current issues, cultural competence and future trends for professional nursing in rural environments. Learning strategies incorporate critical reflection, dialogue, analysis and synthesis of ideas, problem-solving, rural experiences and the creative expression of thought. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Health Outcomes Research
description:
This course focuses on the advanced analysis and application of quantitative research designs and methods to study patient-centered outcomes and the comparative effectiveness of therapies, health interventions, and systems of healthcare delivery.
Prerequisite: 606 and 621. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or permission of instructor.

title: Mixed Methods Research
description: This course provides doctoral students with specific knowledge needed to design and implement a mixed methods research proposal, and assist students to develop critical evaluation skills to critique peer-reviewed mixed methods research reports.

title: Critical Ethnography and CBPR in Health Care Research
description: Examines conceptual/philosophical traditions, foundations, characteristics, procedures, applications and contexts for health care research involving critical ethnographic and CBPR methods. Focuses on strategies for designing and implementing studies in multicultural settings and diverse populations. Prerequisite: 607.

title: Health Care Statistics I
description:
Provides the student with knowledge, skills, and practice in performing, analyzing, and interpreting quantitative data. Course content will include descriptive data analysis as well as parametric and nonparametric statistical tests of association and differences. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Health Care Statistics II
description: Provides knowledge, skills and practice analyzing and interpreting quantitative data using multivariate statistical techniques, including: multiple linear and logistic regression, factorial analysis of variance and covariance, and exploratory factor analysis. Prerequisite: 620.

title: Health Policy, Politics, and Research
description:
This course focuses on health policy, politics, and research. It examines policy stakeholders' use of different types of evidence to influence policy outcomes. Emphasis on U.S. national policies and the affects on health politics and policies.
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice or permission of instructor.

title: Health Care Economics
description:
This course examines concepts, theory, and methods of analysis used in health economics. Emphasis on health policy decision making. Examines the impact of health economic policies on access, cost, quality, and delivery of health care. Prerequisite: 621.
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or permission of instructor.

title: Health Policy Field Placement
description:
Building upon objectives from NURS 648, Health Policy, students in the health policy concentration apply knowledge from prior courses in health policy, research methods, and other areas through field placements in public or private sector settings. 
Prerequisite: 648. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or permission of instructor.

title: Disaster Science and Health Equity
description: This course assesses the principles of disaster science and health equity in the context of recent disasters and public health emergencies. As an initial course in the program of study, students will learn how the science of nursing, medicine, public health, environmental science, and social science interact during the disaster cycle.

title: Issues in Disaster Health Care
description: This course explores issues in disaster health care and policy and links them to specific disasters in the last 100 years. Major disasters will be explored in detail including the impacts on human health especially for at-risk populations, policy at the time of the disaster, and subsequent changes, and notable impacts of health and the healthcare system and nursing. The evolution of both core competencies and crisis standards of care will be explored along with ethical considerations that have arisen. Finally, the course will explore worker health and safety during a disaster and ways to improve both while ensuring the organization’s capacity for disaster.

title: Impacts of Climate Change on Disaster Risk and Health
description: This course explores concepts in climate science, and policy and links them to an overview of public health and epidemiology. Specific ways that climate change impacts human health especially for at-risk populations will be covered including heat-related illnesses, health impacts of extreme weather, respiratory and vector-borne diseases, water- and foodborne illnesses, malnutrition, mental health conditions, and the health impacts of population displacement. The intersections between climate change and healthcare and public health with healthcare providers perceptions about how climate change impacts patient health, the responsibility to advocate for climate action, and necessary education and research.

title: Environmental Health Science
description: A comprehensive study of the major topics of environmental health, including a background of environmental epidemiology, environmental toxicology, and environmental policy and regulation; Environmental diseases; and applications and domains of environmental health.

title: Disaster Theory and Research Methods
description: This course develops competency in principles of research and theory in emergency and disaster management. The course builds on graduate level research and statistic courses.

title: Contemporary Threats of Terrorism and War
description: This course familiarizes participants with major contemporary threats from terrorism, violent extremism, and war and develop an understanding and familiarity with the range of capabilities available to nation states to defeat/minimize threats and strategies for responding to a terrorist event.

title: Disaster Health Education and Communication
description: The purpose of this course is to familiarize participants with the major concepts of disaster health education and risk communication before, during and after a disaster and to learn how to create appropriate/targeted disaster health education and risk communications.

title: Population Focused Health and Human Services Impact from Disasters
description: The purpose of this course is to familiarize participants with issues related to population health and human services impacts from disasters.

title: Refugee Health
description: This course familiarizes participants with issues related to refugees and the complex issues (health, legal, social determinants of health, repatriation, local integration, resettlement, etc.) affecting them and the role of nurses and health professions in the provision of care.

title: Complex Humanitarian Disasters
description: The purpose of this course is to familiarize participants with issues related to complex humanitarian disasters, the need for interdisciplinary and agency (usually global) coordination and response and minimum protections and standards as describe in the Sphere Handbook.

title: Ph.D. Seminar
description:
Specialized courses about a particular topic applicable to advanced graduate students. Different sections indicate different topic content.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Independent Study
description:
Intensive, directed study at the doctoral level on a specific topic or issue pertaining to nursing. The focus for this independent study, objectives, and strategies to achieve the objectives are agreed upon by instructor and student. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Topics
description:
Specific topics relevant to Ph.D. studies in nursing, as identified by the course title. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing.

title: Research Practicum
description:
Focuses on development of skills and techniques for the conduct of research through active participation in faculty research. Emphasis on practical application of research skills and growth in knowledge related to the conduct of research. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing or D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Nursing.

title: Introduction to Professional Inquiry
description: This course will focus on the professional competencies of the DNP for advanced nursing practice emphasizing theory, health equity, professionalism, reflective practice, and professional concepts.

title: Evidence-Informed Practice I
description: This is the first course about evidence-informed practice (EIP) projects. The types of evidence needed to undertake local nursing practice changes will be introduced. Learners will generate and answer practice questions by searching, retrieving, appraising, and summarizing existing evidence.

title: Applied Epidemiology in Advanced Nursing Practice
description:
Using group discussion, literature reviews, and primary and secondary data services, students will gain knowledge regarding the historical dimensions leading to health disparities, social injustice, and concepts of marginalization, poverty, race, class, and gender.
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Applied Clinical Research in Advanced Nursing Practice
description:
This course will focus on the systematic examination and application of health-related research in relation to advanced nursing practice, including clinical practice, nursing and health care administration, community and public health, or health policy.
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: The Business and Policy of Practice and Their Influence on the U.S. Health Care System
description: The complexities of the U. S. health care system are examined with a focus on the major core changes and challenges affecting health care economics, public health care, policy, health reform, care provisions and workforce issues that affect the health of the population, as well as the cost of care and access to care.

title: Organizational Systems and Quality Management for Advanced Nursing Practice
description:
Advanced practice nursing student will focus on the process of quality management, improvement, and outcomes through management and coordination of care in ambulatory and in-patient organizational systems. Individual settings and system-wide performance improvement plans will be developed. 
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Formulating Strategies for Systemic Health Inquiry
description: This course will address population health trends and risk factors, population data, social and economic determinants underlying health disparities, community assessment and analysis, as well as statistics used with epidemiological analysis and associated databases.

title: Evidence-Informed Practice II
description: This second evidence-informed practice (EIP) course introduces learners to concepts needed to plan an EIP project, including writing goals, proposing practice changes, selecting EIP models and frameworks, and collecting data about outcome, process, and structural measures.

title: Integrating Innovative Strategies for Systemic Health Change
description: This course will build on personal and population health strategies to expand inquiry about innovation, policies, and ethical considerations that influence health. Using innovation, information and communication technologies, and informatics processes; construct strategies to manage and improve health outcomes across systems. This includes diverse and under-resourced communities. Following completion of this course, students will use knowledge gained from previous learning set the foundation for students to critically analyze the health of selected populations in a community.

title: Evidence-Informed Practice III
description: This third evidence-informed practice (EIP) projects course includes content relevant for implementing, evaluating, disseminating, and sustaining practice changes. Concepts include EIP project approval and oversight, managing practice change initiatives, data analysis, and presenting findings.

title: Advanced Pathophysiology
description: The course focuses on the application of analytical reasoning and problem solving based on pathophysiology and clinical presentations of a broad variety of diseases of children and adults across the lifespan for advanced nursing practitioner.

title: Pharmacological Principles of Clinical Therapeutics
description: The focus of this course is on principles of pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics which support safe, evidence-based clinical decision making. Knowledge of clinical therapeutics, including drug categories essential for pharmacologic management of primary and acute care problems, is also emphasized. Content includes pharmacologic considerations related to age, genetics, health status, pregnancy / lactation, and patient context.

title: Advanced Health Assessment
description: This course focuses on theories and concepts applied by nurse practitioners in assessment across the lifespan.

title: Primary Care Concepts
description: This course explores concepts such as: principles of justice – diversity, equity and inclusion,  integration of anti-racism, determinants of health into strategies for building therapeutic relationships, and interdisciplinary collaboration in health supervision/  promotion, and disease prevention.

title: Diagnostic Reasoning
description: This course supplements content from the advanced health assessment course. Students utilize data obtained in the history and physical exam for development of differential diagnoses, management plans and documentation common to primary care.

title: Mental Health in Primary Care
description: This course focuses on integration of current neurobiological, pathophysiological, and psychopharmacological evidence for symptom assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of common mental health disorders across the lifespan and in diverse populations.

title: Etiology and Management of Complex Health and Illness in Rural Health
description:
This course examines complex health and illness in individuals in a rural health setting with an emphasis on multi-system processes, environmental, nutritional, economic, cultural, psychosocial stressors, and genetic influences.
Prerequisite: 715. Corequisite: 727 and 797.

title: Professional Concepts and Issues for Advanced Nursing Practice
description:
This course presents the student with the opportunity to explore professional concepts and issues in advanced professional nursing practice and health care and incorporate them into their practice.
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Leading Organizational Change
description:
Integrated learning experiences explore leadership challenges in changing workplaces through on-site implementation of capstone projects. Executive leadership is discussed in context of the role of the nurse leader in promoting and managing institutional change.
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Health Care Innovations and Informatics
description:
This course focuses on nursing informatics. Topics include: (1) introduction to the nursing informatics; (2) health care informatics applications; (3) evidence-based decision support; (4) information systems design; and (5) new opportunities and emerging trends.
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Primary Care of Adults I
description: This is the first in a three-course sequence focusing on primary care competencies in the adult/geriatric population. Students learn patient-centered assessment, diagnostic reasoning, and beginning management of common conditions presenting to primary care settings.

title: Primary Care of Adults II
description: This is the second in a three-course sequence focusing on primary care competencies in the adult/geriatric population. This course builds skills in assessment, diagnosis, and management of increasingly complex acute and chronic health care issues.

title: Primary Care of Adults III
description: This is the third in a three-course sequence focusing on primary care competencies in the adult/geriatric population. Using a problem-based approach, students further skills in evidence-informed management of complex medical conditions and special populations.

title: Sexual and Reproductive Health Across the Lifespan
description: Prepare students in the foundation of client-centered assessment, diagnostic reasoning, and management of common conditions seen in adolescents and adults presenting to primary care settings for sexual, perinatal, and reproductive health concerns.

title: Clinical Skills and Procedures in Primary Care
description: This course provides students with development of technical skills needed when providing procedures commonly offered in primary and urgent care settings for patients across the lifespan.

title: Introduction to Pediatric Physical and Developmental Assessment
description: This course focuses on theories and concepts applied by nurse practitioners in the area of pediatric assessment.

title: Advanced Pediatric Physical and Developmental Assessment
description: This course focuses on the application of advanced pediatric assessment theories and concepts by nurse practitioners.

title: Case Studies in Pediatric Diagnostic Reasoning
description: Course provides students with focused pediatric diagnostic reasoning skills including application of knowledge during simulated patient encounters.

title: Primary Care of Pediatrics I
description: Course focuses on health promotion across the pediatric lifespan including education, prevention, and culturally and developmentally appropriate care in the context of the family.

title: Primary Care of Pediatrics II
description: This course focuses on the evaluation and management of common health care problems across the pediatric lifespan with an emphasis on differential diagnosis and collaboration in primary care.

title: Adolescent and Behavioral Health
description: Course covers primary care for adolescents including health promotion, illness prevention for common health problems including behavioral health problems.

title: Pediatric Special Populations
description: Course focuses on evidence-informed management of complex acquired or inherited pediatric conditions and adolescent primary care.

title: Specialty Pediatric Clinical Practicum
description: Course builds upon the student’s proficiency in comprehensive health evaluation and management in pediatric healthcare.

title: Fundamentals of Reproductive Health and Primary Care Across the Lifespan
description: This course focuses on the physical, mental, genetic, environmental, sexual, and social wellbeing of women from adolescence through the lifespan.  The student will develop fundamental skills of reproductive health and whole person wellness using national recommendations and evidence-based practices.

title: Midwifery in Action; Historical and Scientific Perspectives
description: This course focuses on grounding nurse-midwifery students in the history of midwifery in the United States and in international maternal-child health, as well as the evidence-base for midwifery care in diverse settings.

title: Complex Reproductive Healthcare
description: This course focuses on the complex reproductive and sexual health of women from adolescence through the lifespan.  Public health recommendations and evidence-based practices will be utilized to formulate safe and effective treatment plans in partnership with clients.

title: Midwifery Care for Pregnancy and Postpartum
description: This course focuses on theories and concepts applied by nurse-midwives in the promotion of health for childbearing people in the antepartum and postpartum periods of the reproductive cycle including cultural and rural context, health maintenance, preventive care, and policy considerations.

title: Foundations of Childbirth
description: This course focuses on concepts and evidence which frame techniques and skills required for safe labor and birth and management of the immediate postpartum period including skill development in techniques for labor, birth, third, fourth stage and the postpartum period.

title: Midwifery Care for Childbirth
description: This course will focus on clinical midwifery care of laboring persons and the fetus including facilitation of labor and birth and the management of birth and postpartum care, as well as acute emergencies and obstetrical triage.

title: Fundamentals of Newborn Care
description: This course focuses on the delivery of well childcare and the management of common primary health care problems of the normal infant from birth through one month of age. The antepartum and intrapartum prediction of neonatal health problems by the nurse midwife are also emphasized.

title: Midwifery Care of the Newborn
description: This clinical course focuses on the delivery of well childcare and the management of common primary health care problems of the normal infant from birth through one month of age including antepartum and intrapartum prediction of neonatal health problems.

title: Advanced Diagnostic Test Interpretation
description: This course focuses on interpretation of advanced diagnostic tests necessary for the advanced practice nurse to diagnose health issues and direct care management. Topics will include 12 Lead EKGs, radiographic imaging, ultrasound, and CT scans.

title: Managing the Acute & Complex/Chronically Ill Adult/Gerontology Patient I
description: This course focuses on the complex/chronically ill adult gerontology patient presenting with acute illness.  Emphasis will be placed on the cardiovascular, pulmonary, neurological, psychiatric, and renal, endocrine systems.

title: Managing the Acute and Complex/Chronically Ill Adult/Gerontology Patient II
description: This course is part 2 of 2 courses focusing on the complex/chronically ill adult gerontology patient presenting with acute illness.  Emphasis will be placed on the gastrointestinal, hematological, integumentary and musculoskeletal systems.

title: Managing Emergency & Critical Illness in the Adult/Gerontology Patient
description: This course focuses on the adult gerontology patient presenting with emergency and/or critical illness.  Emphasis will be placed on advanced cardiovascular hemodynamics, ventilatory management, multisystem trauma, shock states, and critical care issues.

title: AGACNP Practicum I
description: This clinical course focuses on the care of the acutely ill adult/gerontology patient.  The AG-ACNP student will have experiences in the acute care setting and develop evidence-informed, person-centered plans of care.

title: AGACNP Practicum II
description: This clinical course focuses on the care of the acutely ill adult/gerontology patient.  The AG-ACNP student will have experiences in various acute care settings & subspecialties and develop plans of care using a collaborative approach.

title: Principles in Advanced Nursing Leadership
description: This course focuses on leadership characteristics and skills required to initiate and sustain a culture of organizational excellence. A broad range of established leadership theories, frameworks, strategies, and ethics in leadership practice are explored.

title: Organizational Excellence Seminar
description: Topics by current administrative and/or organizational leaders addressing key management strategies and metrics of organizational excellence for patient experience, provider engagement and satisfaction, staff engagement and satisfaction, finance measures, and community accountability.

title: Transforming Organizations through Healthcare Reform
description: This course delves into various inter-relationships, effects, strategies, and methods of healthcare change leader practice through the lens of organizations as complex adaptive systems.

title: Finance for Emerging Nurse Leaders
description: This course focuses on managing financial resources within a continually evolving healthcare environment. A working knowledge of finance concepts and processes provide a framework for basic competency in a healthcare setting.

title: Nursing Admininstrative Leadership Specialty Practicum
description: A precepted practice experience incorporating foundational leadership knowledge for the novice to mid-career nurse administrative and/or organizational leader.

title: Human Resources Management
description: A journey into human resource management for the nurse leader from addressing the nursing shortage, ethical practice, recruitment, legal implications, retention, onboarding, leader and staff professional development, through succession planning.

title: Finance for Advanced Nurse Leaders
description: For the advanced nurse leader, this course provides depth of knowledge in organizational performance improvement, cost effective, cost benefit, and ethical healthcare financing, and financial drivers in healthcare systems.

title: Quality Management for Nurse Leaders
description: This course provides an evidence-based systems approach to quality management in healthcare from theoretical, philosophical, and practice-based perspectives.

title: Public Community-Based Program Management and Evaluation
description: Students will explore evidence-based evaluation frameworks commonly used by government and philanthropic entities for grant proposal development and program evaluation.

title: Foundations in PMHNP Clinical Assessment and Practice
description: Course presents an overview of behavioral health assessment in common psychiatric disorders. The focus is on Trauma-Informed Care, provider wellbeing, reflective practice, crisis intervention, establishing therapeutic relationships, and care coordination across healthcare systems.

title: Psychiatric Diagnostic Reasoning
description: Course provides students with a biopsychosocial framework for PMHNPs. Knowledge of diagnosis of psychiatric disorders is emphasized. Students apply knowledge and skills in the differential diagnosis of major psychiatric disorders based on selected taxonomies.

title: Psychotherapy and Behavior Change
description: Course presents theoretical and foundational knowledge related to individual, group, and family therapy for PMHNPs. The focus is on forming a therapeutic relationship and applying motivational interviewing and cognitive behavioral therapy techniques.

title: Neurobiology and Psychopharmacology in Psychiatric Disorders
description: Course focuses on the integration of current neurobiological, pathophysiological, and psychopharmacological theory and research to the assessment of symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment of psychiatric disorders across the lifespan with psychotropic medication.

title: Advanced Practice Seminar on Leadership Development for the PMHNP
description: Course explores the role of the PMHNP as an independently licensed clinician, leader, and member of a comprehensive health care system. Emphasis is on consumer-focused mental health care that is equitable and ethically-driven.

title: Complex Management of Adults for the PMHNP
description: Course focuses on evidence-informed psychiatric mental health assessment, diagnosis, and symptom management of adults. There is an emphasis on complex psychopharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments as well as healthcare policy and equitable access to care.

title: Complex Management of Special Populations for the PMHNP
description: Course focuses on evidence-informed psychiatric mental health assessment, diagnosis, and symptom management of special populations. There is an emphasis on developmentally and culturally-sensitive psychopharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments interventions for these diverse populations.

title: Advanced Practicum I
description: First in a three-course clinical sequence for PMHNP students. Students complete a minimum of 150 hours supervised clinical psychiatric practice evaluating patients and developing management plans. Students will reflect on practice in small group seminars.

title: Advanced Practicum II
description: Second in a three-course clinical sequence for PMHNP students. Students complete a minimum of 300 hours supervised clinical psychiatric practice evaluating patients and implementing management plans. Students will reflect on practice in small group seminars.

title: Independent Study
description:
Specific intensive directed study at the doctoral level relevant to D.N.P. studies in Nursing.
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: Topics
description:
Specific topics relevant to D.N.P. studies in Nursing, as identified by the course title. 
Restriction: admitted to D.N.P. Nursing Practice.

title: DNP Practicum
description: This course provides students with the opportunity to design implement and evaluate a variety of learning experiences appropriate to the course environment and outcomes expected of the designated learners within the full spectrum of advanced nursing practice.

title: Advanced Nursing Fieldwork
description: This is the final practicum for the program, focusing on achieving terminal program competencies in the advanced practice role. didactic components of this course facilitate the development of students into independent, docotorally- prepared clinicians and leaders who integrate program content into practice across systems of care.

title: Systems Thinking for Healthcare Transformation I
description: This course focuses on design of the scholarly project. Application and integration of knowledge gained through foundational coursework continues with the implementation of a scholarly project in an identified area of practice to optimize healthcare system outcomes.

title: Systems Thinking for Healthcare Transformation II
description: This course focuses on implementation of the scholarly project. Application and integration of knowledge gained through foundational coursework continues with the implementation of a scholarly project to optimize healthcare system outcomes or care delivery for populations and communities.

title: Systems Thinking for Healthcare Transformation III
description: This course focuses on evaluation of the scholarly project. Application and integration of knowledge gained through foundational coursework continues with the implementation of a scholarly project to optimize healthcare system outcomes or care delivery for populations and communities.

title: Systems Thinking for Healthcare Transformation IV
description: This course focuses on dissemination of the scholarly project. Application and integration of knowledge gained through foundational coursework continues with the dissemination of a scholarly project to optimize healthcare system outcomes or care delivery for populations and communities.

title: Phlebotomy Technician
description: An examination of concepts of phlebotomy and venipuncture procedures along with basic anatomy and physiology, medical terminology, medical legal and ethical issues, patient care, best practices, and quality assurance.

title: Certified Phlebotomy Technician Clinical
description: Clinical apprenticeship program for PBT 101L.  Successful completion required for credit in PBT 101L.  Corequisite:  PBT 101L.

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: In this course, students will be introduced to some of the key questions of philosophy through the study of classical and contemporary thinkers. Some of the questions students might consider are: Do we have free will? What is knowledge? What is the mind? What are our moral obligations to others? Students will engage with and learn to critically assess various philosophical approaches to such questions.

title: Logic, Reasoning, and Critical Thinking
description: The purpose of this course is to teach students how to analyze, critique, and construct arguments. The course includes an introductory survey of important logical concepts and tools needed for argument analysis. These concepts and tools will be use to examine select philosophical and scholarly texts.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Professional Ethics
description: This course focuses on some of the ethical issues that arise in the context of professional life. Beginning with an overview of several major ethical theories, the course will consider how these theories, which traditionally concern personal morality, apply to life in a professional setting. The course will focus on issues that might include lying and truth-telling, whistleblowing, confidentiality, the obligations of businesses toward the public, and the ethical concerns of privacy in journalism. Using a combination of readings, case studies, and discussion, students will explore these issues by critically evaluating ethical principles and also applying them to real-world settings.

title: Early Modern Philosophy
description: This course is an introductory survey of early modern Western philosophy. Through an in-depth reading of primary source material, this course will examine the traditions of Rationalism and Empiricism that emerged during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Concepts to be discussed might include theories of knowledge and metaphysics, early modern scientific thought, and theories of the self.

title: Greek Philosophy
description: This course is an introductory survey of early and classical Greek philosophy. The course will include discussion of such philosophers as the Pre-Socratics, the Sophists, Socrates, Plato and Aristotle. Topics to be discussed may include the beginnings of scientific thought, theories of the self, the concept of being, virtue ethics, happiness, and theories of justice.

title: Greek Thought
description:
An introductory survey of early and classical Greek philosophy, literature, and history. Figures: Presocratics, Socrates, Plato and Aristotle; Homer and Sophocles; Herodotus and Thucydides.
Meets New Mexico General Education Curriculum Area 5: Humanities.

title: Introduction to Existentialism
description: The aim of this course is to introduce students to the tradition of existential philosophy through a careful reading of philosophical texts by authors, such as Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Sartre, de Beauvoir, and Heidegger.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Buddhist Philosophy
description: This course traces the evolution of such topics as karma and rebirth and the nature of the liberated mind as discussed in the Buddhist traditions of India, Tibet, East Asia, and the modern West.

title: Indian Philosophy
description: Upanishads, Bhagavad-gita, Jainism, Buddhism, the six Hindu systems and recent developments.

title: Chinese Philosophy
description: The development of Chinese thought from pre-Confucian times through the T'ang dynasty.

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: An investigation of some important philosophic debates.

title: Existential Thought
description: An examination of themes and concepts characteristic of existential thought through a careful and comparative study of philosophical writings from figures such as Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Sartre, de Beauvoir, and Heidegger. Literary works from authors such as Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, and Camus may also be covered.

title: Contemporary Continental Philosophy
description:
A survey of main themes in Dilthey, Husserl, Scheler, Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, Sartre, Hermeneutics, Structuralism, Deconstruction and the Frankfurt School.  
Prerequisite: one course in Philosophy.

title: Philosophy of Science
description: This course is a survey of the main epistemological, ontological and conceptual issues that arise from or concern the methodology and content of the empirical sciences.

title: Theory of Knowledge
description:
An examination of the nature and possibility of knowledge. Topics include skepticism, the analysis of knowledge, and the nature and structure of epistemic justification.
Prerequisite: 2210.

title: Metaphysics
description:
Problems and theories of metaphysics. Topics may include: investigation into the structure of things and their properties, identity and individuation, causation, necessity and possibility, universals, mind and body, space and time, God, truth and naturalism. 
Prerequisite: one course in Philosophy.

title: Symbolic Logic
description: (Also offered as MATH **356) This is a first course in logical theory. Its primary goal is to study the notion of logical entailment and related concepts, such as consistency and contingency. Formal systems are developed to analyze these notions rigorously.

title: Ethical Theory
description:
Inquiry concerning goodness, rightness, obligation, justice and freedom. 
Prerequisite: one course in Philosophy.

title: Modern Christian Thought
description:
Background of the intellectual issues facing Roman Catholic and Protestant traditions today. 
Prerequisite: one course in Philosophy or Religious Studies.

title: Environmental Ethics
description: Close reading of contemporary writings by naturalists, lawyers, theologians and philosophers on the philosophical aspects of environmental problems.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Philosophic analysis of some major concepts and problems in religion.

title: Classical Social and Political Philosophy
description:
From Plato to Hobbes. 
Prerequisite: 1115 or 2220 or 2225.

title: Modern Social and Political Philosophy
description:
From Hobbes to present.  
Prerequisite: one course in Philosophy.

title: Philosophy of Law and Morals
description:
Examination of philosophical issues pertaining to law, including the nature of law, responsibility, rights, justice, the justification of punishment, and the justification of state interference with individual liberty. 
Pre-requisite: any course in Philosophy.

title: Latin American Thought
description: Positivism through contemporary thought.

title: Kant
description:
A study of Kant’s philosophical thought that typically focuses on a close reading of significant portions of the Critique of Pure Reason. Topics may include: a priori knowledge, causality, and idealism.
Prerequisite: 2210.

title: Hegel
description:
A study of Hegel’s philosophical thought focused on a close reading of texts such as Phenomenology of Spirit and Encyclopedia Logic. Topics may include: Hegel’s conceptions of metaphysics, ethics, social theory, and the method and aims of philosophy.

PHIL 411 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 511 such that a student cannot take PHIL 411 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 511 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: History and Philosophy of Mathematics
description: A historical survey of principal issues and controversies on the nature of mathematics. Emphasis varies from year to year.

title: Early Heidegger
description:
Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) is widely considered one of the most original and important philosophers of the 20th century.  This seminar will focus on his most famous and influential work, Being and Time (1927).

PHIL 421 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 521 such that a student cannot take PHIL 421 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 521 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: Wittgenstein
description:
A survey of Wittgenstein's thought and work typically including close reading of his Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus as well as portions of his later writings (1929 and after).  Topics may include Wittgenstein's conception of philosophical methodology, philosophy of language, and philosophy of mind.

PHIL 422 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 522 such that a student cannot take PHIL 422 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 522 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: Seminar in Asian Philosophers
description: Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

title: Topics in Philosophical Figures and Movements
description:
A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Topic varies. 

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Seminar in Individual Philosophers
description:
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study. Figure varies. 
Prerequisite: one course in Philosophy.

title: Asian Studies Thesis
description: Supervised research in one or more disciplines leading to an undergraduate thesis for the major in Asian Studies.

title: Seminar in Metaphysics and Epistemology
description: This course offers graduate and advanced undergraduate students exposure to contemporary literature and current professional discussion on issues in metaphysics and/or epistemology.  Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Philosophy of Mind
description:
A study of certain issues connected with the nature and status of minds. Topics include the mind-body problem, intentionality, consciousness, and mental causation.

PHIL 455 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 555 such that a student cannot take PHIL 455 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 555 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: Seminar in History of Philosophy
description:
A close and critical examination of issues in the history of philosophy. Emphasis may be placed on a particular philosophical figure or on the development of a particular trend in the history of philosophy.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Seminar in Moral and Political Philosophy
description:
A study of advanced topics in ethics. Possible topics include: priactical reason; the connection between ethics and agency; metaehtics; the nature of normativity.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Seminar in Philosophy of Religion
description:
Advanced topics in philosophy of religion.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Seminar in Philosophy of Art and Aesthetics
description:
An in-depth examination of the genesis of modern aesthetics in the 18th and early 19th centuries, with a special focus on the aesthetic theory of Immanuel Kant.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Philosophy of Art and Aesthetics
description:
Philosophical investigation of concept and theories of art and literature. Possible topics include the nature, definition and criteria of art; its functions; form and content; aesthetic experience; evaluation; artist's/author's status; meaning; reception; hermeneutics and representation.

PHIL 467 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 567 such that a student cannot take PHIL 467 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 567 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: Seminar in Psychoanalytic Theory and Continental Philosophy
description:
This seminar offers students an in-depth introduction to psychoanalysis considered in relation to philosophy. It focuses on Freudian and/or Lacanian versions of analytic thought and their consequences for various philosophical discussions.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Seminar in Continental Philosophy
description:
This seminar offers graduate and advanced undergraduate students an in-depth engagement with a specific philosopher or philosophical orientation situated in the context of twentieth-century Europe. It focuses on French and/or German philosophies in particular.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Philosophy and Literature
description:
Selected philosophical movements and their relationships to literary masterpieces. 

PHIL 480 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 580 such that a student cannot take PHIL 480 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 580 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: Seminar on Major Continental Philosopher
description:
A close reading of a leading figure in contemporary continental philosophy, typically focusing on that thinker's most influential work, such as Sartre's Being and Nothingness, Levinas's Totality and Infinity, Gadamer's Truth and Method, etc.
Prerequisite: 12 credit hours Philosophy course work.

title: Honors Seminar
description: For departmental honors in philosophy. {Offered upon demand}

title: Reading and Research
description: A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive paper or research proposal that integrates knowledge attained through coursework, research, and experience.

title: Senior Thesis
description: For departmental honors. {Offered upon demand}

description: A study of Kant’s philosophical thought that typically focuses on a close reading of significant portions of the Critique of Pure Reason. Topics may include: a priori knowledge, causality, and idealism.

title: Hegel
description:
A study of Hegel’s philosophical thought focused on a close reading of texts such as Phenomenology of Spirit and Encyclopedia Logic. Topics may include: Hegel’s conceptions of metaphysics, ethics, social theory, and the method and aims of philosophy.

PHIL 411 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 511 such that a student cannot take PHIL 411 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 511 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

title: Nietzsche
description:
A study of Nietzsche's philosophical thought. Topics may include: Nietzsche's ethical critiques; the will to power thesis; agency and free will; truth; meaning; eternal recurrence and the affirmation of life.

PHIL 414 will be considered equivalent to PHIL 514 such that a student cannot take PHIL 414 as an undergraduate and later take PHIL 514 for graduate credit. Students in our Shared-Credit Program will be advised of the equivalency.

description: A historical survey of principal issues and controversies on the nature of mathematics. Emphasis varies from year to year.

Topics may include Wittgenstein's conception of philosophical methodology, philosophy of language, and philosophy of mind.

title: Later Heidegger Post-Heideggerian Philosophy
description: This course will examine the "later" (post-1937) Heidegger and/or some major critical appropriations of Heidegger's later thinking by Badiou, Baudrillard, Blanchot, Cavell, Derrida, Dreyfus, Foucault, Irigaray, Lacan, Levinas, Marcuse, Rorty, Vattimo, Zizek, or others.

description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
