title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Ph.D. Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Seminar in Sanskrit Philosophical Texts
description: This course is designed to give students at the intermediate to advanced level practice in reading philosophical literature in Sanskrit. The texts chosen will be those that are most relevant to the students' research interests.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introduction to Pharmacy Practice and Pharmaceutical Sciences
description: The course provides an introduction to the scientific principles that form the basis for pharmacy practice and an overview of contemporary pharmacy practice, including specialized patient care settings.

title: Pharmaceutical Biochemistry
description: The course covers basic principles of protein, carbohydrate and lipid function, metabolism, and an introduction to molecular mechanisms of drug action.

title: Physical Pharmacy and Biopharmaceutics
description: This course provides an introduction to physical pharmacy and biopharmaceutical principles that impact drug development and the performance of pharmaceutical dosage forms.

title: Pharmaceutical Calculations
description: Students will be introduced to the basics of pharmaceutical calculations. The course begins with and overview of the metric system and common unit conversion. The course will then cover SI sytstem, ratio and dimensional analysis, density, specific gravity and volume, active drug calculation, reduction and enlargement of formulae, percent and ratio strengths, dose considerations, and finally concentration and dilution of active preparations.

title: Fundamentals of Pathophysiology and Immunology
description:
The course provides in introduction to the fundamentals of human pathophysiology and diagnostic tests used to evaluate normal and abnormal biomarkers and the underlying principles of human immunology.
Prerequisite: BIOL 2225.

title: Fundamentals of Pharmacokinetics and Dosage Forms
description: An introduction to the influence of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion on the time course of drug levels in body as well as fundamental principles in the design and manufacture of dosage forms.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology and Medicinal Chemistry
description: This course will provide an introduction to pharmacology, drug metabolism, toxicology, and elimination of drugs that act upon the autonomic nervous system.

title: Dosage Forms
description:
To provide students with an introduction to the fundamental principles of dosage forms, their design and the processes used in manufacturing and compounding drug products.
Prerequisite: 302 and 310.

title: Quality Control and Regulatory Affairs
description:
To provide students with an introduction to quality control and regulatory affairs associated with drug product development.
Prerequisite: 302 and 310 and 311.

title: Introduction to Pharmacogenomics
description: An introduction to how inherited variations in genes dictate drug response. Topics covered include basic principles of medical genetics, chemotherapy improvement through pharmacogenomics, as well as the ethical, legal, and social impact of pharmacogenomics.

title: Molecular and Cellular Pharmacology
description: An overview of principles and basis of modern molecular and cellular pharmacology.

title: General Toxicology
description: An in-depth introduction to the principles and concepts of toxicology. Categories: toxic chemicals, the manner of exposure to toxic substances, the environmental and biological effects and the laws and regulations will be considered.

title: Pharmaceutical Sciences and Toxicology Seminar
description:
Research seminars on current topics in pharmaceutical sciences and toxicology will be presented by students and faculty from within UNM and from invited speakers outside of UNM.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Pharmacy
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Problems in Pharmacy
description: Research and library problems in some phase of pharmacy. Not for professional students in the College of Pharmacy curriculum. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Problems in Pharmacy
description: Research and library problems in some phase of pharmacy. Not for professional students in the College of Pharmacy curriculum. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Pharmacy and Health Care Delivery Systems
description: Explores the current U.S. healthcare delivery system models from perspective of pharmacy with emphasis on the influences of government policy, regulatory oversight and financial implications.

title: Pharmacoepidemiology and Research Design
description:
Introduction to basic concepts of pharmacoepidemiology and research study designs used to evaluate health, disease, and drug utilization. Methods of critical evaluation, analysis of biomedical literature, identification of reliable and valid research outcomes are presented.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Evidence-Based Clinical Decision Making
description:
This course develops skills to determine validity, applicability and significance of published clinical research for potential application in optimizing patient care.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Introduction to Pharmacogenomics
description: An introduction to how inherited variations in genes dictate drug response. Topics covered include basic principles of medical genetics, chemotherapy improvement through pharmacogenomics, as well as the ethical, legal, and social impact of pharmacogenomics.

title: Safe Medications and Pharmacy Informatics
description:
This course provides a study of the source/existence of medication errors, reasons for these errors and suggested methods (including pharmacy informatics) to prevent them from occurring.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Research Design and Analysis
description: The course will provide training in research design and statistical methods used in conjunction with various studies. It will provide students with experience in the application of epidemiological and biostatistical methods available in SAS.

title: Ethics Clinical Trials-Informed Consent
description: The study of the history, ethical versus scientific methodological conflicts, and other issues that are generated by the conduct of randomized controlled clinical trials using human beings.

title: Regulatory Issues in Clinical Trials
description: The study of federal regulations and guidelines that govern the planning and conduct of randomized controlled clinical trails in humans with drugs and devices.

title: Fundamentals of Clinical Trials
description:
Advanced readings on topics relating to the pharmaceutical sciences in the area of the fundamental concepts and methods of designing, conducting, monitoring, and closing out multicenter clinical trials.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Multicenter Clinical Trials
description: The multicenter clinical trials (MCCT) experiential provides the pharmaceutical sciences graduate student applied training in multicenter clinical trials or cooperative studies.

title: Molecular and Cellular Pharmacology
description:
An overview of principles and basis of modern molecular and cellular pharmacology. 
{Spring}

title: General Toxicology
description: An in-depth introduction to the basic principles and concepts of toxicology. Categories of chemicals causing toxic effects, the manner of exposure to toxic substances, the environmental and biological effects, and the laws and regulations will be considered.

title: Seminar in Administrative Pharmacy
description:
This course will give the students experience in organizing and presenting their thoughts and interpretations on a selected subject. The seminar will provide the student with an opportunity to develop writing and formal oral presentation skills.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Seminar in Radiopharmacy
description:
Each masters candidate will be required to present a seminar on a topic of choice approved by his/her supervisor or selected by the supervisor.  May be repeated for credit, no limit.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Pharmaceutical Sciences and Toxicology Seminar
description:
Research seminars on current topics in pharmaceutical sciences and toxicology will be presented by students and faculty from within UNM and from invited speakers outside of UNM.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Environmental Disease
description: Advanced readings in topics relating to toxicology and environmental disease, including areas such as chemical teratogenesis, reactive oxygen species, respiratory toxicology, receptor-medicated toxicology and environmentally induced cancer. Prerequisite: 580. Restriction: permission of instructor. {Fall, Spring}

title: Seminar in Clinical Trial Design and Management
description: This course provides graduate students with a broad knowledge of methods, issues, and considerations pertaining to the design and management of clinical trials.

title: Research Problems in Pharmaceutical Sciences
description:
Research in pharmaceutical sciences. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Pharmaceutical Sciences
description: Advanced readings in topics relating to the pharmaceutical sciences in the areas of hospital pharmacy, pharmacy administration, radiopharmacy or toxicology. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: T: Pharmaceutical Science
description: Advanced readings in topics relating to the pharmaceutical sciences in the areas of hospital pharmacy, pharmacy administration, radiopharmacy or toxicology.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Dissertation
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Pharmaceutical Biochemistry
description: Structure, properties, biologic functions, applicable kinetics, and metabolic fate of macromolecules essential to life (proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, and nucleic acids). Application of these concepts to identify endogenous targets for drug therapy and rational drug design strategies.

title: Physical Pharmacy and Biopharmaceutics
description:
An introduction to physical pharmacy and biopharmaceutical principles impacting drug development and the performance of pharmaceutical dosage forms.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Aspects of Patient Care I
description:
The first in a series of six courses, students are introduced to prescription processing and dispensing as well as patient communication. Drug information retrieval, Top 200 drugs, pharmaceutical calculation, and medical terminology are included.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Public Health
description:
Presents New Mexico public health challenges in which pharmacists play a vital role in improving health of community and local population.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Fundamentals of Pathophysiology and Immunology
description:
The fundamental basics of human pathophysiology and diagnostic tests used to evaluate normal and disease-based biomarkers and the underlying principles of immunology are presented as foundation for in-depth organ-specific disease states discussed in subsequent courses.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Introduction to Pharmacy Practice and Communication
description:
This course will allow students to develop an understanding for the profession, insight into themselves as future health care professionals and enable students to learn the fundamentals of professional communication in healthcare settings.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Pharmacy and Health Care Delivery Systems
description:
Explores the current U.S. healthcare delivery system models from perspective of pharmacy with emphasis on the influences of government policy, regulatory oversight and financial implications.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Fundamentals of Pharmacokinetics and Dosage Forms
description:
An introduction to the influence of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion on the time course of drug levels in body as well as fundamental principles in the design and manufacture of dosage forms.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology and Medicinal Chemistry
description:
This course will specifically cover an introduction to pharmacology, basics of drug metabolism and the structure-activity relationships (SAR), toxicology and elimination of drugs that act upon the autonomic nervous system.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Aspects of Patient Care II
description:
The second in a series of six courses, students continue dispensing and professional communication skills. Introduction to Pharmacists’ Patient Care Process with self-care products. Basic drug information, Top 200 drugs and non-sterile compounding.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Introduction to Clinical Reasoning, Self-Care, and Non-Prescription Therapeutics
description: Introductory course to the Clinical Reasoning, Self-Care, and Nonprescription Therapeutics sequence presents pathophysiology, pharmacology, and therapeutics pertaining to self-care and non-prescription pharmacotherapy.

title: Introduction to Law, Ethics and Social Issues in Pharmacy
description:
Introduction to pharmacy law with considerations of professional ethics and social determinants of health that impact the professional practice of pharmacy.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Professional Development I
description:
Series of seminars with focus on professionalism, leadership development, career exploration and decision-making, post graduate education opportunities, and contemporary professional practice issues influencing patient care. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics I
description:
The first course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to use in general ambulatory care for cardiology, endocrinology, and pulmonary disease states.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Introduction to Nuclear Pharmacy
description: This course provides an overview of nuclear pharmacy as a practice specialty: contributions of the nuclear pharmacist and application of radioactive tracer techniques in the diagnosis and treatment of disease will be reviewed.

title: Aspects of Patient Care III
description:
The third in a series of six courses, students focus on the Pharmacists’ Patient Care Plan involving prescription medications, patient history and more complex patient cases.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Dosage Forms
description:
To provide students with an introduction to the fundamental principles of dosage forms, their design and the processes used in manufacturing and compounding drug products.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics II
description:
The second course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapy sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to use in general ambulatory care for endocrinology, musculoskeletal pain and psychiatry.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Pharmacoepidemiology and Research Design
description: Introduction to basic concepts of pharmacoepidemiology and research study designs used to evaluate health, disease, and drug utilization. Methods of critical evaluation, analysis of biomedical literature, identification of reliable and valid research outcomes are presented.

title: Professional Development II
description:
Series of seminars with focus on professionalism, leadership development, career exploration and decision-making, post graduate education opportunities, and contemporary professional practice issues influencing patient care. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics III
description:
The third course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapy sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to antibiotic treatment of bacterial infections.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Pharmacoeconomics
description:
The course covers fundamental concepts in pharmaceutical economic evaluations including decision model analysis. Provide a basic understanding of how to manage resources and optimize healthcare resource allocation with emphasis on drug treatments.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Evidence-Based Clinical Decision Making
description:
This course develops skills to determine validity, applicability and significance of published clinical research for potential application in optimizing patient care.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Aspects of Patient Care IV
description:
The fourth in a series of six courses, students focus on the Pharmacists’ Patient Care Plan involving prescription medications. Patient History and more complex patient cases.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Pharmacy Management and Operations
description:
Introduction to principles, skills, and practices relevant to successful business operation and management in a variety of pharmacy practice settings.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics IV
description:
The fourth course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to use in complex ambulatory care setting for a variety of disease states.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Introduction to Pharmacogenomics
description: An introduction to how inherited variations in genes dictate drug response. Topics covered include basic principles of medical genetics, chemotherapy improvement through pharmacogenomics, as well as the ethical, legal, and social impact of pharmacogenomics.

title: Introductory Spanish in the Pharmacy
description: This course provides the pharmacy student with the basic tools to communicate with Spanish-speaking patients. Pharmacy-specific vocabulary and helpful structures will be covered in addition to fundamental grammar and conversational skills.

title: Intermediate Spanish in the Pharmacy
description: A continuation of the introductory course. In addition to advancing the course participant's knowledge of basic medical terminology, the intermediate level introduces other relevant vocabulary topics for effective pharmacy communication in Spanish.

title: Professional Development III
description:
Series of seminars with focus on professionalism, leadership development, career exploration and decision-making, post graduate education opportunities, and contemporary professional practice issues influencing patient care. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics V
description:
The fifth course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapy sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to fungal and viral infections.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Safe Medications and Pharmacy Informatics
description:
This course provides a study of the source/existence of medication errors, reasons for these errors and suggested methods (including pharmacy informatics) to prevent them from occurring.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Aspects of Patient Care V
description:
The fifth in a series of six courses, students focus on the Pharmacists’ Patient Care Plan involving prescription medications. Patient History and more complex patient cases.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics VI
description:
The sixth course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to use in complex ambulatory care for neurological and psychiatric disease states.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics VII
description:
The seventh course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to use in treatment and management of oncological disease states.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Professional Development IV
description:
Series of seminars with focus on professionalism, leadership development, career exploration and decision-making, post graduate education opportunities, and contemporary professional practice issues influencing patient care. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics VIII
description:
The eighth course in the Integrated Pharmacotherapeutics sequence presents the pathophysiology, pharmacology, medicinal chemistry and pharmacotherapeutics as they relate to use in complex hospital care for cardiology, nutrition, critical care and clinical toxicology.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Pharmacy Continuing Professional Development Seminar
description: This course consists of a weekly series of seminars presented by pharmacists and other medical professionals throughout New Mexico. These seminars aim to increase student awareness and understanding of contemporary issues related to pharmacy practice, pharmaceutical industry, and health care.

title: Aspects of Patient Care VI
description:
The sixth in a series of six courses, students focus on the Pharmacists' Patient Care Plan involving prescription medications. Patient History and more complex patient cases.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Advanced Law and Ethics
description:
A review of state and federal statutes and regulations governing pharmacy within New Mexico. Compare and contrast current regulation with ethical decision making situations involving health care.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Clinical Capstone
description:
Students will demonstrate professional ability to integrate and apply knowledge of basic sciences, pharmacotherapy, pharmacy practice management, oral/written communication, literature evaluation, drug information retrieval, and pharmacoeconomics in management of patients with multiple medical conditions.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Research Project
description:
Student completes research project under direction of College of Pharmacy faculty.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Professional Development V
description:
Series of seminars with focus on professionalism, leadership development, career exploration and decision-making, post graduate education opportunities, and contemporary professional practice issues influencing patient care. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Substance Abuse Elective
description: This course emphasizes the most important themes and concepts in the field. Lectures/presentations will cover major categories of drug abuse (Opioids, Marijuana, Stimulants, Hallucinogenics, CNS depressants, Performance-enhancing drugs, OTC drugs, Herbals, Plants, and Tobacco/Caffeine/EtOH).

title: Pharmacy Practice Experiences
description: Rotations that allow students to participate in patient-centered care activities in different settings that are in-depth, structured, and carefully coordinated with other components of the curriculum and require active participation in patient care responsibilities.

title: Introductory Community Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
A four-week (40 hours/week) directed dispensing pharmacy experience. Students will be exposed to ambulatory patient care in a community pharmacy setting.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Introductory Institutional Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
A four-week (40 hours/week) directed dispensing pharmacy experience. Students will be exposed to in-patient care in an institutional pharmacy setting.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Nuclear Pharmacy Instrumentation
description: Structure and properties of atoms, radiation and radioactive decay, production of radionuclides, interactions of radiation with matter, with emphasis on instrumentation for radiation detection and measurement in a nuclear pharmacy or nuclear medicine environment.  Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Radiopharmaceutical Chemistry
description:
Introduces undergraduate students to inorganic chemistry as applicable to radiopharmaceuticals.  
Prerequisite: CHEM **302. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Radiopharmacy Health and Radiation Biology
description: Fundamentals of the biological effects of ionizing radiation on living systems, especially man; basic biological mechanisms which bring about somatic and genetic effects. Concepts of radiation protection, radiation dosimetry, radiation monitoring and x-ray health physics. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Direct Patient Care Experience I
description:
Students participate in direct-patient care interactions with practitioners and patients advancing patient welfare in authentic practice settings. Participation in Pharmacist Patient Care Process emphasizes patient data collection and assessment for possible medication-related problems.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Direct Patient Care Experience II
description:
Students participate in direct-patient care interactions with practitioners and patients to advance patient welfare in authentic practice settings. Pharmacist Patient Care Process emphasizes medication distribution systems and high quality, interprofessional, and team-based patient care.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Clinical Toxicology
description: Study of the acute toxicity in humans of common drugs, chemicals and household products; physical and laboratory assessment of common poisonings; development of clinical management plans and role of pharmacists in prevention of poisonings. P3 standing in College of Pharmacy.

title: Geriatric Medication Management - An Interprofessional Elective
description: A course designed to provide hands-on experiences with geriatric patients in senior centers. Students will work with Nurse Practitioners and nursing students to evaluate blood pressures, blood glucoses, disease states and medication therapy.

title: Advanced Cardiovascular Pharmacotherapy
description: This course is designed to give students an advanced knowledge and skill base in cardiovascular medicine and will include topics designed to allow the student to maximize the learning experience in the Cardiology APPE.

title: Geriatric Interprofessional Elective
description: This course involves the study of the treatment of common geriatric disease states in multiple care settings and involves case-based discussion with an interprofessional team of students and instructors. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Indirect Patient Care Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
This course is a supervised pharmacy experience involving no direct patient care. By observing and practicing, students will develop their skills to be practice- and team-ready.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Advanced Pharmacy Practice Seminar
description:
A hybrid seminar course addressing current updates and trends of best practices in pharmacy practice.

Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Physical Assessment for the Pharmacist
description: A physical assessment course to include instruction in performing physical assessment exams for all major organ systems, patient interviewing, and documentation for students who are interested in incorporating clinical pharmacy into their future practice.

title: Advanced Institutional Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
This course is a supervised pharmacy experience in an inpatient or institutional setting. By observing and practicing, students will develop skills to be practice- and team-ready.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Advanced Community Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
This course is a supervised pharmacy experience in an outpatient, community setting. By observing and practicing, students develop skills to be practice- and team-ready.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Ambulatory Care Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
This course is a supervised pharmacy experience in an outpatient setting. By observing and practicing, students will develop their skills to be practice- and team-ready.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: General and Adult Medicine Advanced Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
This course is a supervised pharmacy experience in an adult medicine setting. By observing and practicing, students will develop their skills to be practice- and team-ready.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

title: Direct Patient Care Pharmacy Practice Experience
description:
This course is a supervised pharmacy practice experience in an outpatient and/or inpatient setting. By observing and practicing, students will develop skills to be practice- and team-ready.
Restriction: admitted to Pharm.D. program.

description:
This course provides current and/or future pharmacy practice topics that are not otherwise provided within the required Doctor of Pharmacy course curriculum.

The topics covered are the following principles of fitness, health related and motor skill related components of fitness, weight control, common fitness injuries and stress management.

title: PA Foundations of Research Methodology
description: This course provides an overview of Evidence-Based Practice, informatics, and clinically relevant statistics. It covers research design evaluation and introduces public health, health policy, and healthcare systems, preparing students for roles in public health and diverse patient care.

title: PA Pediatric and Adolescent Medicine I
description: This course is an introduction to care of children from birth through adolescence. Lectures emphasize ethics, age-appropriate physical examination and history-taking, growth and developmental milestones, preventive care and anticipatory guidance, dysmorphology, social determinants of health, hematology, fevers, and poisoning.

title: PA Human Anatomy
description: This course provides an overview of basic anatomical concepts, normal human anatomy, and embryology, with a focus on their relevance to clinical practice.

title: PA Pediatric and Adolescent Medicine II
description: This course focuses on the principles of care of children from birth through adolescence including cardiovascular, pulmonary, and renal conditions. This course has a primary care focus in alignment with the PA Program’s mission and goals.

title: PA Pediatric and Adolescent Medicine III
description: This course provides the PA student with the principles of care of children from birth through adolescence focusing on gastrointestinal conditions and nutrition. This course has a primary care focus in alignment with the PA Program’s mission and goals.

title: PA Adult and Geriatric Medicine I
description: This course equips PA students with essential knowledge and skills for primary care of adult and geriatric patients. It covers major biological principles, including anatomy, physiology, pharmacology, and pathophysiology, and explores social, behavioral, and population perspectives.

title: PA Hematology
description: This course provides an overview of hematology, including hematopoiesis and hemostasis, and covers the pathophysiology, presentation, diagnosis, and management of common hematologic diseases.

title: PA Microbiology and Immunology
description: This course covers the human immune system, including innate, cellular, and humoral responses to infections, hypersensitivity, and autoimmune diseases. It also addresses fundamentals of infectious diseases, focusing on prevention, identification, and treatment of immunologic and infectious disorders.

title: PA Adult and Geriatric Medicine II
description: This course continues with principles of adult and geriatric health care, focusing on presentation, evaluation, and treatment of conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content for this age group.

title: PA Adult and Geriatric Medicine III
description: This course covers common primary care disorders in adult and geriatric patients, focusing on evaluation and management skills. It integrates with the current medical school block, emphasizing disease prevention, cultural issues, and chronic condition management.

title: PA Pharmacology I
description: This course introduces pharmacology and basic pharmacotherapeutics to first-year PA students. It emphasizes principles and mechanisms of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination (ADME), with a focus on clinical application in primary care.

title: PA Emergency Medicine I
description: This course introduces care in emergency departments and urgent care centers. It integrates with the current medical school block and emphasizes rapid evaluation and management of conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content.

title: PA Emergency Medicine II
description: This course continues education on care in emergency departments and urgent care centers. It integrates with the current medical school block and emphasizes rapid evaluation and management of conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content.

title: PA Pharmacology II
description: This course continues pharmacology and clinical pharmacotherapeutics for conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content. It builds on Pharmacology I principles, focusing on medication application and clinical use in primary care.

title: PA Pharmacology III
description: This course focuses on prototype drugs, including drug classes, generic names, organ system effects, metabolism, elimination, and drug interactions. It emphasizes the clinical application of these drugs for conditions covered in medical school content.

title: PA Emergency Medicine III
description: This course culminates education on care in emergency departments and urgent care centers. It integrates with the current medical school block and emphasizes rapid evaluation and management of conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content.

title: PA Pediatric and Adolescent Medicine IV
description: This course focuses on the principles of care of children from birth through adolescence focusing on neurological, psychiatric and behavioral disorders. This course has a primary care focus in alignment with the PA Program’s mission and goals.

title: PA Adult and Geriatric Medicine IV
description: This course focuses on adult and geriatric health care, emphasizing conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content. It develops problem-solving skills and compares care for adults and the elderly.

title: PA Clinical Skills I
description: This course, part of a series, teaches first-year PA students history taking, physical exams, and clinical reasoning. It includes lectures, practice sessions, and standardized patient experiences, fostering inter-professional understanding and teamwork.

title: PA Clinical Skills II
description: This course, part of a series, enhances first-year PA students' history taking, physical exam skills, and clinical reasoning. It includes clinical skills sessions, practice, and standardized patient experiences, and introduces the series of clinic exposures with Continuity Clinic 1.

title: PA Clinical Skills III
description: This course builds on prior Clinical Skills, including competency exams in organ system content from medical school, and includes a half-day per week clinical experience.

title: PA Pharmacology IV
description: This course continues pharmacology and clinical pharmacotherapeutics, applying principles from Pharmacology I to medications for conditions related to the organ system covered in medical school content. It emphasizes clinical application and drug use in primary care.

title: PA Clinical Skills IV
description: This course, part of a series, helps second-year PA students refine history-taking, physical exam skills, clinical reasoning, and oral presentations, preparing them for clinical year clerkships and the Final Summative Exam.

title: PA Clinical Seminar
description: This course introduces the Physician Assistant profession, covering anatomy, physiology, and population health topics such as epidemiology, health promotion, disease prevention, health policy, and models for change, with a focus on rural health in New Mexico.

title: PA Dermatology
description: This course covers major biological principles of dermatology, including anatomy, normal physiology, pharmacology, and pathophysiology.

title: PA Orthopedics
description: This course introduces students to common disorders of the musculoskeletal system via a mix of lectures, small group clinical case work, and hands-on workshops.  Students practice orthopedic physical exam maneuvers and interpret skeletal radiographs and other imagining modalities as applicable.

title: PA Neurosciences
description: This course provides a foundation in the basic medical and clinical science of the nervous system, with a focus on neurology, psychiatry, and behavioral medicine.

title: PA CV-Pulmonary-Renal
description: This course provides a foundation in the basic medical sciences of the cardiovascular, pulmonary, and renal systems, covering physiology, pathophysiology, diagnostics, and pharmacotherapeutics associated with common disorders of these organ systems.

title: PA Infectious Disease
description: This course provides an overview of the medical and clinical science of human infectious diseases, including viral, bacterial, fungal, and parasitic conditions. Emphasis is on preventing, identifying, and treating infectious disorders commonly seen by physician assistants.

title: PA GI, Nutrition, Metabolism (GINM)
description: This course provides basic medical and clinical foundations of the gastrointestinal (GI) system, nutrition, and metabolism (NM), including pathophysiology associated with common disorders. It emphasizes a primary care focus, aligning with the PA Program’s mission and goals.

title: PA Sexuality, Reproduction and Endocrine
description: This course provides a foundation in the basic medical and clinical science of human sexuality, the human reproductive system, and the endocrine system, including the pathophysiology associated with common disorders of these organ systems.

title: PA Clerkship Family Medicine
description: This supervised clerkship is a first-year field experience covering the healthcare needs of patients across the lifespan, with a focus on major aspects of primary health care.

title: PA Clerkship Women's Health and Prenatal Care
description: This supervised clerkship focuses on managing women’s health care in a family practice setting, including evaluation and management of common gynecological conditions and routine prenatal care.

title: PA Clerkship Pediatrics
description: This supervised clerkship in family practice-pediatrics focuses on developing skills in the healthcare of pediatric and adolescent patients.

title: PA Clerkship Behavioral Medicine - Psychiatry
description: This supervised clerkship focuses on the healthcare of patients with behavioral health issues commonly seen in primary care. Field experience includes both office and clinic/inpatient settings.

title: PA Clerkship Emergency Medicine
description: This supervised clerkship focuses on the triage, evaluation, and management of patients in the emergency setting.

title: PA Clerkship General Internal Medicine
description: This supervised clerkship focuses on the care of adult patients in both outpatient and inpatient settings, providing a foundation for subspecialty courses such as cardiology and endocrinology.

title: PA Clerkship Elective
description: This supervised clerkship offers students the opportunity to select a clinical field experience in an area of personal interest.

title: PA Clerkship Surgery
description: This supervised clerkship focuses on the care of surgical patients, including operating room experience, pre- and post-operative care, and patient assessment in office and inpatient settings.

title: PA Foundations of Medical Science
description: This course provides an overview of cellular and molecular function, genetic physiology and pathophysiology, and introduces pharmacology.

title: PA Clerkship Primary Care
description: This supervised clerkship integrates elements from Family Practice and specialty clerkships, enabling students to advance their clinical management skills and function at a higher level in patient care.

title: PA Program to Practice
description: This course allows students to demonstrate skills from the PA curriculum and readiness for the profession. It includes instruction on PA competencies, lectures, small group seminars, and a HRSA community health project using the PDSA cycle method.

title: Independent Study
description: The Physician Assistant Independent Study course allows students to focus on personalized areas of interest or challenge. Students develop tailored study plans with faculty, covering advanced topics or research, with regular evaluations to ensure alignment with PA program competencies.

title: Topics
description: The Physician Assistant Topics course addresses curricular needs essential for institutional and accreditation standards. It offers a comprehensive, adaptable curriculum, ensuring students are well-prepared for the requirements of their training and future professional practice.

title: Basic  Plumbing, Safey and Pipe Fitting
description: An introduction to basic plumbing, safety and pipe fitting. This course will introduce students to today’s complex and sophisticated plumbing systems and the career opportunities available. Major topics include safety, tools, materials, equipment, and procedures. The course will introduce students to installation and repair techniques as well as development in materials , fixtures and appliances. The program will cover both hands, the proper use of plumbing tools and customer service for employment skills.

title: Plumbing I
description: Covers orientation to the trade. Students will learn about materials used in the plumbing industry and the different types of plumbing fixtures. It includes task-oriented projects in which the students apply many of the skills and knowledge that are presented through the National Center for Construction and Education Research (NCCER) Plumbing Program.

title: Plumbing II
description: Provides students the opportunity to gain more practice in the skills and knowledge learned in Plumbing I. Students will install fixtures and run the various plumbing supply lines from Plumbing Level I. The course included hands on projects in which the students apply many of the competencies that have been presented through the National Center for Construction and Education Research (NCCER) Plumbing Program.

title: Soldering and Brazing
description: This course focuses on the joining methods of soldering & brazing which include, Safety and safe work practices; Theory of soldering and brazing; Types and uses of copper tube; Solders, brazing rod, and fluxes; Joint preparation and assembly; Heating equipment and tools; Soldered and brazed joints; and Performance tests for soldering and brazing.

title: Plumbing Code
description: In this course students will gain insight into the 2015 Uniform Plumbing Code and the 2015 New Mexico Plumbing Code. Regulations and guidelines for proper installation will be explored in preparation for the New Mexico State Plumbing License for Journeyman Certification which is a requirement within this program. Topics covered include Administration; Definitions; General Regulations; Plumbing Fixtures; Water heaters; Water Supply, Sanitary Drainage; Indirect Wastes; Vents; Traps and interceptors; Storm Drainage; and Fuel gas piping.

title: Foundations
description:
This course addresses foundations for patient care that will be further developed in subsequent courses. Content includes principles of exercise physiology, documentation, professionalism, ethics, kinesiology, pathology, pharmacology, imaging, learning styles, mind/body, and therapeutic interventions.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Musculoskeletal Intervention I
description:
Orthopaedic physical therapy treatment techniques including exercise based treatments, soft tissue techniques and physical agents including electrical, electromagnetic, mechanical modalities. 
Prerequisite: 505.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Musculoskeletal Concepts and Management I
description:
Philosophy, process and techniques of patient examination and evaluation, including patient interview, systems review, differential diagnosis and neuromusculoskeletal assessment. Therapeutic interventions, including modalities, orthotics, manual therapy, and therapeutic exercise will be addressed.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Musculoskeletal Concepts and Management II
description: This course introduces orthopedic physical therapy of ankle, foot, cervical spine, TMJ, shoulder, elbow, wrist, and hand, and serves as a foundation course for the remainder of the curriculum.  The course will enable the student to interview and evaluate a patient.

title: Musculoskeletal Intervention II
description:
This course addresses foundations for orthopedic treatment including exercise and physical agents. This class is designed around regional anatomy and follows both the anatomy and orthopedic evaluation courses. 
Prerequisite: 506L. 
Corequisite: 508L. 
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Clinical Education Experience I
description:
Nine-week clinical education experience provides opportunity to participate in screening, systems review, examination, evaluation, formulation, implementation/modification of plan of care, discharge procedures, scheduling, coordination of patient care activities, supervision of support personnel, documentation for patients/clients.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Professional Patient Management in Physical Therapy
description: The purpose of this course is to gain practice with effective communication techniques prior to the first clinical education experience. This course involves large and small group discussion and collaboration to develop professional behaviors.  Case-based learning is used to analyze problems that may occur within various PT relationships, with a focus on maintaining curiosity and objectivity to strategize and develop solutions.  This course will also orient students to the objectives and expectations of the first full-time clinical education experience.

title: Management of Integumentary Dysfunction
description: Introduce the student to the structure and function of the integumentary system, in order to understand the implications of integumentary dysfunction and/or assess risk of injury to the integumentary function. Restriction: Admitted to DPT Physical Therapy.

title: Human Anatomy I
description: This course will involve dissection and study of the human body.  Lecture material will highlight the nervous system; osteology of the lower extremity, and spinal column; the vascular system and the muscular system of the lower extremities, abdomen, neck, and back; the physiology of the cavities (cardiac, pleural and peritoneal).

title: Anatomy II
description: This course will involve dissection and study of the human body.  Lecture material will highlight the nervous system including the cranial nerves; osteology of the upper extremity, cranium, spinal column; the vascular system and the muscular system of the upper extremities, head and neck.

title: Evidenced Based Physical Therapy I
description: This course will teach the principles of evidence based physical therapy practice in patient/client management from the initial clinical question to the analysis of the targeted research.  The course will teach the student how to recognize quality in research, increasing the student’s understanding of and confidence in their interpretation of the research.  Students will learn how to critically appraise research, how to find answers to specific foreground questions, and how to interpret statistical data presented within all types of research. Initiation of “mini capstone” process that will complete in EBPT2.

title: Pharmacology for Physical Therapists
description: It is essential that a physical therapist be able to make appropriate treatment versus referral decisions with each patient evaluation because of increased responsibility in health management. A component of that is evaluation of the medications your patient is taking, consideration of whether medication side effects may contribute to the symptoms you observe and knowledge of side effects that impact rehabilitation treatments. Therefore, pharmacological treatment and mediation side effects will be addressed, including potential interactions between pharmacological agents and physical therapy practice. Medications will be discussed congruent with the respective physiological system.

title: Pathophysiology
description:
Study of the pathophysiology, clinical presentation and implications to physical therapy practice of common disease entities that affect the human body.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Prosthetics in Physical Therapy
description: The purpose for this course is to develop students’ clinical skills for caring patients with limb loss. The class emphasizes biomechanical concepts for pre- and post-prosthetic trainings as a part of the interdisciplinary approach for rehabilitation after amputations.

title: Management of Neurologic Dysfunction IV
description:
Students learn cognitive, observational, psychomotor skills necessary to examine, manage patients with neurologic dysfunction with an emphasis on adults with spinal cord injury, stroke, traumatic brain injury.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Management of Neurologic Dysfunction II
description: This course applies the concepts of neuroscience and neuropathology and illustrates the correlations with clinical practice.  The student will learn cognitive, observational, and psychomotor skills necessary to examine and manage adults with neurologic dysfunction with an emphasis on patients with stroke, Parkinson’s disease, and cerebellar dysfunction.

title: Management of Neurologic Dysfunction III
description:
Enhances content from 606L and 609L. Students learn cognitive, observational, psychomotor skills necessary to examine, manage patients with neurologic dysfunction with an emphasis on adults with spinal cord injury, stroke, traumatic brain injury. 
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Management of Neurologic Dysfunction I
description: This course introduces the student to neuroanatomy and neuropathology and illustrate the correlations to clinical practice. The student will learn cognitive, observational and psychomotor skills necessary to assess and manage adults with neurologic dysfunction. Principles of neurologic examination and evaluation, including neurologic tests and outcome measures, will be introduced.  Case studies will be presented throughout the course to highlight these clinical scenarios

title: Acute Care
description:
This course is designed to enable students to practice in an acute/critical care setting. Topics include cardiopulmonary rehabilitation and general acute care, including ICU equipment, lab values, pharmacology, evaluation, treatment and discarge planning.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Clinical Education Experience II
description:
Nine-week clinical education experience provides opportunity to participate in screening, systems review, examination, evaluation, formulation, implementation/modification of plan of care, discharge procedures, scheduling, coordination of patient care activities, supervision of support personnel, documentation for patients/clients.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Cardiovascular and Pulmonary Physical Therapy I
description: This course gives an overview of normal cardiovascular and pulmonary function, pathologies, examination and evaluation, and differential diagnosis. Labs will focus on developing cardiovascular and pulmonary related assessment techniques in order to perform a cardiovascular and pulmonary oriented patient examination. Successful completion of this course requires the ability to integrate and synthesize information from this course across multiple cardiac, pulmonary, and vascular diagnoses.

title: Biopsychosocial Practice
description:
The purpose of this course is to develop the student's awareness of psychosocial and cultural issues of the health professional and the patient, necessary to provide optimal care to patients. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Specialty Topics in Neurologic Physical Therapy
description: This course will look at specialty neurological physical therapy topics primarily focused on the vestibular system including extension into concussion management and EMG studies and interpretation.  The student will be working towards independent evaluation of patients with specialty diagnosis including vestibular diagnoses and post-concussion syndrome from a position driven evaluation with gestalt reasoning to determine the relevant areas of the evaluation that must be completed based on the history and what presents through the evaluation.  The emphasis of the course will be on diagnostic evaluation and treatment of specialty neurological topics.  Evaluation will include diagnoses including both central and peripheral vestibular disorders, post-concussion syndrome, and EMG studies along with the various evidence based treatments associated with these diagnoses.  As a clinical class, much time will be spent on problem solving real life clinical problems by using volunteer patients both from the class and from people associated with the class.

title: Special Topics in Biomechanics, Gait and Movement Analysis in Physical Therapy
description: This course will introduce fundamental biomechanics utilized in physical therapy practice. The primary emphasis is to obtain clinical decision-making skills to evaluate human movements related to the mechanical properties of motion at a joint, as well as forces and muscle actions. This course will provide students various lab activities utilizing a 3D motion capture system and other assessment tools (e.g., force platforms, pressure mapping and electromyography) as well as low-cost motion capture options.

title: Evidence Based Physical Therapy II
description: The primary emphasis of Evidence-based Physical Therapy is to provide the student with skills to become critical consumers of the rehabilitation literature and inform their clinical practice. EBPT2 builds on EBPT1 and expands the critical analysis process to various types of study designs. Students will review current published physical therapy studies and discuss strengths and risk of bias as it pertains to applying the study findings to PT practice. Students will learn how to calculate and interpret common statistics in physical therapy and epidemiological research.  The “mini-capstone” continues in EPBT2 with emphasis on synthesizing the information from the critical appraisal worksheets to write an informed discussion and clinical bottom line. Concepts learned in EBPT will be a cornerstone to understanding other research as students advance in the program.

title: Pelvic Health in Physical Therapy
description: The purpose of this course is to enhance the students’ knowledge of physical therapy evaluation, goal setting and treatments related to the following conditions: pelvic health/pelvic floor dysfunction (e.g. incontinence, pelvic pain, pelvic organ prolapse, prostate health); bowel, bladder and sexual function in the patient with neurologic dysfunction; perinatal health (e.g. pregnancy, intrapartum & postpartum care); transgender and sexual health; intimate partner violence and strategies for working with survivors of abuse.

title: Clinical Spanish in Physical Therapy
description: There are an ever-increasing number of people in the U.S. who only speak Spanish, and this trend is reflected in the patient population.  This course goes over health disparities related to language disconcordant care and best practices for working with a medical interpreter. This course will offer an overview of basic anatomy, basic medical vocabulary, grammar and conversation with an emphasis on verb tenses most necessary for communication with Spanish speaking patients.  Grammar will include verb conjugation into the imperative and present tense.  The expectation is not for students to develop Spanish fluency, but rather a basis of language skills that allows the student to communicate in accordance with their skill level, and as appropriate with the help of an interpreter. Students will develop an understanding how to safely work with patients who have limited English proficiency.

title: Pain and Differential Diagnosis in Physical Therapy
description: This course will address key reasoning process that underlie effective differential diagnosis. This course will build on examination and evaluations skills and integrate this with knowledge of pathology and pathogenesis in clinical populations. In this course you will learn to effectively screen patients for appropriateness of care (in an outpatient PT setting) and what referral and documentation steps must be taken if a patient is deemed inappropriate. Additionally, this course will also provide a basic overview of pain science, assessment and treatment of complex pain conditions.

title: Clinical Education Experience III
description:
Nine-week clinical education experience provides opportunity to participate in screening, systems review, examination, evaluation, formulation, implementation/modification of plan of care, discharge procedures, scheduling, coordination of patient care activities, supervision of support personnel, documentation for patients/clients.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Core Values in Physical Therapy
description: The purpose of this course is for learners to actively engage with the nine core values for the Physical Therapist as defined by the APTA: accountability, altruism, collaboration, compassion/caring, duty, excellence, inclusion, integrity, and social responsibility (see appendix for full document). This will be accomplished through service-learning and IPE experiences and active participation in scheduled seminars throughout the 3-year program.

title: Clinical Education Experience IV
description:
Ten-week clinical education experience provides opportunity to participate in screening, systems review, examination, evaluation, formulation, implementation/modification of plan of care, discharge procedures, scheduling, coordination of patient care activities, supervision of support personnel, documentation for patients/clients.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Cardiovascular and Pulmonary Physical Therapy II
description: This course educates students on cardiovascular and pulmonary interventions based off of pre-requisitive examination and evaluation skills learned in PT613. Labs will focus on performing, modification, and identification the appropriate patient to make use of cardiopulmonary and vascular interventions. Labs will focus on hands-on cardiopulmonary and vascular skills necessary for the entry level physical therapist, including the conduction of a patient treatment session. Successful completion of this course requires the ability to apply and adapt cardiovascular and pulmonary intervention principles across multiple cardiac, pulmonary, and vascular diagnoses.

title: Health, Wellness, and Fitness
description: This course will maximize the student’s understanding of health, wellness and fitness as it relates to their future clients and patients.  Students will also gain an in-depth understanding of the various issues in health promotion from a physical therapy perspective.

title: Pediatrics in Physical Therapy
description: This course focuses on application of current models of physical therapy assessment and intervention for typically developing children and children with various conditions that affect normal growth and development during infancy, childhood and adolescence.

title: Gerontology
description:
In-depth study of evaluation and clinical management of the geriatric patient/client across the continuum of care.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Administration and Supervision
description: This course will prepare students to practice in an increasingly complex health care delivery system. Emphasis includes personnel supervision, practice regulatory and legal requirement issues, reimbursement, litigation, and ethics related to service delivery.

title: Advanced Musculoskeletal Concepts
description:
This course will look at orthopaedic physical therapy of primarily the spine, with extension to the upper and lower extremity. The student will be exposed to the history of, theories behind and a limited scope of the skill of dry needling. The emphasis of the course will be on diagnostic evaluation and treatment using multiple approaches (Nordic, Cyriax, Australian, Paris, Greenman, Functional mobilization, Maitland). Treatment techniques covered will include an introduction to functional dry needling, mobilization, muscle energy and thrust, as well as therapeutic exercise as it relates to mechanical spinal, upper quarter, and lower quarter pain. As a clinical class, much time will be spent on problem solving real life clinical problems by using volunteer patients. 
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Capstone Project
description:
This course is the academic home for a comprehensive evidence-based practice project that definitvely, and in a completely supported manner, answers a focused clinical question. A comprehensive paper and platform presentation are required.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Board Preparation
description:
An intensive overview of test-taking and study strategies and review of all major content areas in the National Physical Therapy Exam.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Integrated Clinical Experiences
description:
This course is conducted at REACH clinic, a laboratory clinical setting, with a faculty mentor. Students give short presentations summarizing the presentation of their patients, the chosen interventions, and the plan of care and goals.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Physical Therapy Independent Study
description:
This course is a supervised independent study for students to complete an elective academic experience of relevance to physical therapy or to complete a course required for graduation, such as a clinical internship.
Restriction: admitted to D.P.T. Physical Therapy.

title: Special Topics in Physical Therapy
description: Content varies, students may be registered for several sections concurrently. Registration by approval of the Physical Therapy Program director. {Offered upon demand}

title: Physics You Need For Life
description:
Designed to provide non-science majors with a toolbox of physics concepts that enables them to make sense of the world
around them, from nature and its myriad puzzles to everyday technology to science policy.

title: Survey of Physics
description: Overview of the concepts and basic phenomena of physics. This course provides a largely descriptive and qualitative treatment with a minimum use of elementary mathematics to solve problems. No previous knowledge of physics is assumed.

title: Survey: Physics You Need for Life Laboratory
description: A series of laboratory experiments associated with the material presented in 1115. Two hours lab.

title: Introduction to Applied Physics
description: This course is designed for students who need more experience with force and motion before tackling 1230 or 1310. It also serves as a good refresher for students who let some time lapse between taking Physics I and II.

title: Physics of Music
description: Introduction for non-science majors to basic concepts, laws, and skills in physics, in the context of a study of sound, acoustics, and music.

title: Physics of Music Laboratory
description:
Experiments to accompany 1125. Two hours lab. 
Pre- or corequisite: 1125.

title: Algebra-Based Physics I
description:
An algebra-based treatment of Newtonian mechanics. Topics include kinematics and dynamics in one and two dimensions, conservation of energy and momentum, rotational motion, equilibrium, and fluids.

The sequence (PHYS 1230, 1230L, 1240, 1240L) is required of pre-medical, pre-dental, and pre-optometry students. Only 1230 and 1240 are required of pharmacy students.

Credit for both this course and PHYS 1310 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Algebra-Based Physics I Laboratory
description: A series of laboratory experiments associated with the material presented in 1230. Three hours lab.

title: Problems in Algebra-Based Physics I
description:
This is a supplemental course for 1230.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Corequisite: 1230.

title: Algebra-Based Physics II
description:
The second half of a two semester algebra-based introduction to physics. This course covers electricity, magnetism and optics.

Credit for both this course and PHYS 1320 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Algebra-Based Physics II Laboratory
description: A series of laboratory experiments associated with the material presented in 1240. Three hours lab.

title: Problems in Algebra-Based Physics II
description:
This is a supplemental course for 1240.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: 1240.

title: Calculus-Based Physics I
description:
A calculus-level treatment of classical mechanics and waves, which is concerned with the physical motion concepts, forces, energy concepts, momentum, rotational motion, angular momentum, gravity, and static equilibrium.

Credit for both this course and PHYS 1230 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Calculus-Based Physics I Laboratory
description: A series of laboratory experiments associated with the material presented in 1310. Students will apply the principles and concepts highlighting the main objectives covered in coursework for 1310. Three hours lab.

title: Problems in Calculus-Based Physics I
description:
This is a supplemental course for 1310.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: 1310.

title: Calculus-Based Physics II
description:
A calculus-level treatment of classical electricity and magnetism. It is strongly recommended that this course is taken at the same time as 1320L.

Credit for both this course and PHYS 1240 may not be applied toward a degree program.

title: Calculus-Based Physics II Laboratory
description: A series of laboratory experiments associated with the material presented in 1320. Students will apply the principles and concepts highlighting the main objectives covered in coursework for 1320. Three hours lab.

title: Problems in Calculus-Based Physics II
description:
This is a supplemental course for 1320.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: 1320.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Calculus-Based Physics III
description: This course, the third in the calculus based sequence for science and engineering students, is a study of optics and topics in modern physics.

title: Calculus-Based Physics III Laboratory
description:
Covers topics in geometrical optics, wave optics and modern physics at the calculus level. Lab activities mirror and enhance lecture topics. Hands-on experiments involving data collection and analysis give students a better conceptual framework for understanding physics. Geometrical and wave optical phenomena are deeply probed. Three hours lab. 
Pre- or corequisite: 2310.

title: Problems in Calculus-Based Physics III
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to 2310. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: 2310.

title: Computational Physics
description:
This class is designed as an introduction to programming for the undergraduate physics major. The class begins with no assumption of prior programming experience. An emphasis will be on building strong programming skills using the MATLAB programming environment. Applications and examples will include data analysis (curve fitting and optimization), simulating physical systems, solving systems of linear equations and Monte Carlo techniques.
Prerequisite: 2310.
Pre- or corequisite: MATH **316.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Physics and Astronomy
description:
Advanced study of concepts of physics and astronomy, designed especially for science teachers and other non-traditional students. Cannot be used to satisfy major or minor program requirements for physics or astrophysics degrees. 
Prerequisite: 1115 or ASTR 1115 or NTSC 1110.

title: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics
description:
Concepts of heat and thermodynamics; large numbers and probability distributions; spin, oscillator, and gas systems; simple interacting systems, Fermi and Bose statistics. 

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Introduction to Photonics
description:
Geometrical optics; wave optics; lasers, nonlinear optics. 
Prerequisite: 2310.

title: Optics Lab
description:
Laboratory experiments in geometrical optics, diffraction, prisms, gratings, microscopy and imaging, polarization, interference and interferometry, and laser operation.  
Prerequisite: 2310.

title: Analytical Mechanics I
description:
Dynamics of particles and rigid bodies, harmonic motion, gravitation, Lagrange's and Hamilton's equations, moving coordinate systems. Students are highly recommended to take **366 as a corequisite.
Prerequisite:  MATH **316.

title: Analytical Mechanics II
description:
Mechanics of continuous media, rotations of rigid bodies, small oscillations, nonlinear and chaotic motions.
Prerequisite: **303 and (**366 or MATH **312).

title: Junior Laboratory
description:
Contemporary electronics. One lecture, 3 hours lab. Completion of **330 is highly recommended.
Prerequisite: 2415.

title: Junior Laboratory
description:
Experiments in modern physics and experimental methods. One lecture, 3 hours lab. Completion of **330 is highly recommended.
Prerequisite: 2415.

title: Problems in Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to **301. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: **301.

title: Problems in Analytical Mechanics I
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to **303. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: **303.

title: Problems in Analytical Mechanics II
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to **304. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: **304.

title: Geophysics
description:
Applications of gravity, magnetics, seismology, heat flow to the structure, constitution and deformation of the earth. Related aspects of plate tectonics and resource exploration. 

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Introduction to Modern Physics
description:
Special relativity; quantum effects; introductory quantum mechanics; atomic and subatomic physics; instruments of modern physics. 
Prerequisite: 2310.

title: Problems in Introduction to Modern Physics
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to **330. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: **330.

title: Mathematical Methods of Physics
description:
Vector calculus, partial differential equations, complex numbers, tensor analysis, Fourier series and transforms, special functions, and their application to physics.
Prerequisite: 2415 and MATH **316.

title: Seminar
description:
Student presentations, both extemporaneous and prepared, of undergraduate physics problems. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Electricity and Magnetism I
description:
Electrostatics; dielectric materials; magnetostatics; magnetic materials. 
Prerequisite: **366 or (MATH 311 and MATH **312).

title: Electricity and Magnetism II
description:
Electromagnetic induction; conservation laws; propagation, reflection, and refraction of electromagnetic waves; wave guides; dipole radiation; relativistic fields.  
Prerequisite: *405.

title: Problems in Electricity and Magnetism I
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to *405. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.
Corequisite: *405.

title: Problems in Electricity and Magnetism II
description:
Problem solving and demonstrations related to *406. 
Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Corequisite: *406.

title: Introduction to Solid State Physics
description: Free electron gas, energy bands, crystals, semiconductors, metals, elementary excitations, superconductivity.

title: Introduction to Quantum Information Science
description: An introduction to the mathematical formalism of quantum information science, with an exploration of the key results from its application to quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum metrology.

title: Introduction to Subatomic Physics
description:
Introductory topics in elementary-particle physics and nuclear physics, with examples and applications to high-energy physics and astrophysics such as cosmic rays, fixed-target experiments, lepton and hadron colliders, stellar physics, supernovae and cosmology.

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Problems
description:

title: Particle Physics I

title: Introduction to Quantum Information Science
description: An introduction to the mathematical formalism of quantum information science, with an exploration of the key results from its application to quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum metrology.

title: Laser Physics II
description: This course covers advanced topics in laser and optical physics that includes (but not limited to) semiconductor lasers, detection and noise, extreme wavelength generation, and ultrafast metrology.

title: Quantum Optics
description: Study and manipulation of quantum coherence with electromagnetic fields. Quantum coherent spectroscopy; photon statistics and nonclassical light; open quantum systems; decoherence; special topics.

title: Nonlinear Optics
description: General concepts, microscopic approach, nonlinear optical effects and devices.

title: Advanced Topics in Modern Optics
description: Possible topics include dye lasers, solid-state lasers, novel lasers, interaction between intense lasers and matter, advanced nonlinear optics spectroscopy.

title: Quantum Computation
description: This course explores the concepts and mathematical techniques underlying quantum com-putation. Topics include quantum entanglement, quantum cryptography, teleportation, models for quantum computation, quantum algorithms, quantum error correction, and fault-tolerant quantum computation.

title: Principles and Platforms of Quantum Technology
description: Quantum Technology harnesses the unique features of quantum mechanical systems, such as superposition and entanglement, for devices that may revolutionize computing, communication, and sensing. This course provides a deep dive into several platforms used by practicing Quantum Technology researchers.
