description:
This laboratory course is designed to offer students exposure to the wide variety of research that is typically conducted in the field of clinical psychology. It will teach students how to read and critique the relevant literature in an area and how to design solid studies to answer specific research questions. 
Prerequisite: 2510 and 332.

title: Evolution and Human Behavior
description:
This course provides an introduction to the evolutionary study of human behavior, from key biological concepts to current theories and findings on the evolution of psychological processes. 
Prerequisite: 2250.

title: Cognitive Neuroscience
description: Explore how the brain drives perception, memory, emotions, and actions in this introduction to cognitive neuroscience.

title: Human Neuropsychology
description:
The analysis of brain-behavior relationships regarding affect and higher cognitive functions (language, memory, spatial reasoning) in humans.  
Prerequisite: PSYC 2250.

title: Neuroscience of Aging and Dementia
description:
General survey of topics in the cognitive and brain sciences of aging and dementia. Topics include the etiology of dementia subtypes, treatment, management, and the socioeconomic impact of an increasingly older population demographic.
Prerequisite: 2250.

title: Introduction to Forensic Psychology
description:
This is an advanced psychology course designed for those with an interest in the field of forensic psychology. Forensic psychology applies psychological art and science to legal and quasi-legal problems.

Prerequisite: 332.

title: Drugs and Behavior
description:
Study of the pharmacological action and physiological and psychological effects of drugs of abuse including stimulants, depressants, narcotics and hallucinogens.  
Prerequisite: PSYC 2250.

title: Human Learning and Memory
description:
How humans acquire and use knowledge. Theoretical and applied issues discussed around the topics of memory structures, attention, forgetting, mnemonics, imagery and individual differences in memory.  
Prerequisite: PSYC 2220 or PSYC 2270.

title: Psychology of Perception
description:
Study of the methods organisms use to gain information about objects. The sensory processes are discussed as a basis for description of more complex perceptual phenomena.  
Prerequisite: PSYC 2220 or PSYC 2270.

title: Psychology of Language
description:
(Also offered as LING 367/567) 
Theoretical and methodological issues in psycholinguistics, including comprehension, speech perception and production, language acquisition, bilingualism, brain and language, reading. 
Prerequisite: ANTH 1115 or LING 2110 or LING 301 or PSYC 2110 or PSYC 2120 or PSYC 2220 or PSYC 2250 or PSYC 2270.

title: The Psychology of Horror
description:
This course explores the emotion of horror both from the standpoint of theoretical and empirical work on emotion and from the standpoint of critical approaches to the phenomenon of horror in art. 
Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Cultural Psychology
description: Impact of culture on human behavior, learning, personality and other selected topics is examined. Course emphasizes critical analysis, discussion and writing about cross-cultural research and theory.

title: Psychology of Gender
description:
Survey of research and theory on gender-role stereotypes and gender differences in such contexts as interpersonal relations, the family, the work force, mass media, mental and physical health.
Prerequisite: PSYC 1110.

title: The Psychology of Love
description:
An in-depth examination of the universal human experience of love. This course explores the full range of theories on love, including biological, taxonomical, implicit, developmental, cultural, and evolutionary. PSYC 2120 is recommended.
Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Junior Honors Seminar
description: Discussion of the history and systems of psychology, philosophy of science and research methodology, particularly as related to current topics in psychology.

title: Junior Honors Seminar
description: Continuation of 391. Prerequisite: 391. {Spring}

title: History of Psychology
description: An introduction to the major developments and individuals in the history of psychology. Prerequisite: any 300-level psychology course

title: Crisis Worker Practicum
description:
Training/experience at Agora Crisis Center. Can lead to national certification. Some weekend, evening and holiday hours required. Minimum commitment: two semesters, weekly four-hour shift. Must be 18 and deemed eligible by the Agora Executive Committee.  
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Psychological Research with Diverse Populations
description:
This course introduces students to various structural factors that may be challenging for different populations, and different research methods and approaches to engaging and working with diverse populations in culturally appropriate and effective manners.
Prerequisite: 302.

title: Treatment of Addictions
description:
This course will provide an overview of evidence-based approaches to the treatment of alcohol and other substance use disorders. 
Prerequisite: 332 or 347 or 430.

title: Applied Clinical Experience in Addictions Counseling Field Work
description:
This field experience at an addiction agency teaches student to apply classroom learning to practice situations. A licensed counselor supervises. 
Prerequisite: 332 and 335 and (347 or 430). Pre- or corequisite: 313 and 411. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Health Disparities
description:
This course introduces students to various significant factors that affect health outcomes for a population differently that the general US population, and culturally appropriate research approaches in working with diverse populations experiencing a disparity.
Prerequisite: 302.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Developmental Psychology
description:
Investigation of the theoretical bases and critical issues in the area of developmental psychology.  
Prerequisite: PSYC 2120.

title: Child Language
description: Theories, methodologies and findings in child language, from birth to late childhood. Emphasizes implications of child language data for linguistic and psycholinguistic theories. Topics: biological foundations; pre-linguistic communication; phonological, syntactic, semantic and pragmatic development; bilingualism.

title: Alcohol Use and Alcohol Use Disorders
description:
This advanced psychology course is for those interested in the nature of and prevention and treatment of alcohol use disorders and alcohol-related problems.   
Prerequisite: 332.

title: Depression: Diagnosis, Treatment, Prevention
description:
This seminar examines research on prevention, diagnosis and treatment of clinical depressions and mania. 

Prerequisites: 2250 and 332.

title: Behavior Therapies
description: A survey of clinical behavior therapies, including techniques based upon learning theory, self-control, cognitive and social psychological principles. Emphasis is upon treatment outcome research and the practical application of methods to clients' life problems.  Prerequisite: 332.

title: Child Psychopathlogy
description:
Theories and practices related to an understanding of children and adolescents who deviate from normal development either intellectually, educationally, emotionally, physically or in some combination. Relevant family variables are considered.  
Prerequisite: 2120 and 332.

title: Neuroscience of Sleep and Dreaming
description: Introduction to current concepts and neuroscientific research on sleep and dreaming. The roles of rapid eye movement (REM) and non-REM sleep in learning and memory, mood regulation, and mental health are explored.

title: Sleep: Scientific Investigation
description:
This seminar explores research on sleep incorporating molecular biology, math modeling, electrophysiology, comparative biology, developmental psychology, circadian physiology, and anthropology. Students read original research reports and discuss research methods. Background in neuroscience and mathematics necessary. 
Prerequisite: 302 and 2250.

title: Psychobiology of Emotion
description:
The course reviews the psychological science of emotion, particularly the application of objective physiological and neural measurements. Topics will include historical perspective, neuroscience of emotion, physiology of stress, disorders of emotion, and more. 
Prerequisite: PSYC 1110.

title: Advanced EEG Analysis in MatLab
description: The goal of the course is to provide a conceptual, mathematical, and pragmatic understanding of neural time series data, especially in regard to electroencephalographic (EEG) signals.

title: Developmental Neuroscience Laboratory
description:
In DNL we aim to understand the integrative complexity of developmental and disease-related changes in the brain and behavior in the context of epigenetics as measured by neuroimaging (MRI, MEG, EEG) and neuropsychological measures. 

Prerequisite: 332. 

Pre- or corequisite: 2250.

title: Introduction to Neuroscience Research
description: Provide an overview of the principles and methods used in neuroscience research. Students will gain an understanding of how neuroscience research is conducted and the techniques used by neuroscientists to answer research questions.

title: Functional Neuroanatomy
description: The basics of human neuroanatomy and brain networks are described based on data recorded by functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), diffusion tensor imaging (DTI), positron emission tomography PET, electroencephalography (EEG), magnetoencephalography (MEG) and transcranial stimulation

title: Special Topics in Psychology
description: Study of any psychological topic not otherwise included in the curriculum upon expression of mutual interest by students and faculty.

title: Special Topics in Psychology Laboratory
description:
Psychology laboratory topics not otherwise included in the curriculum. Labs offered upon the expression of mutual interest by students and faculty. 
Prerequisite: 2510.

title: Positive Psychology
description:
This will examine theory and research regarding the development of human strengths at an advanced undergraduate level. The course will focus on strengths that promote better functioning including creativity, optimism, wisdom, courage, love, and spirituality. 
Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Positive Psychology Laboratory
description:
This is an advanced lab course teaching how positive psychology theory and research can be applied to improve well-being and success. This is a lab that includes individual and group presentations and several papers.
Prerequisite: 454. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Gorilla Observation Laboratory
description:
The purpose of this course is to teach students the basic skills needed to take systematic, reliable observational data on captive Lowland Gorillas living at the Albuquerque Biopark.
Prerequisite: (302 or *400 or 2110 or 2220 or  2270 or 2510) and 1110. 
Restriction: junior or senior standing.

title: Psychology of Prejudice
description:
This course introduces students to the concept in how stereotypes and prejudice are formed, maintained, and changed, and the effects of prejudice towards an underrepresented minority group within a larger society. PSYC 374 is recommended.
Pre-requisite: 2110 or 2120.

title: Senior Honors Seminar
description:
Experimental methods and laboratory techniques. Senior thesis based on independent research. Three hours lab.  
Prerequisite: 392. {Fall}

title: Senior Honors Seminar
description: Continuation of 491. Three hrs. lab. Prerequisite: 491. {Spring}

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Statistics
description: Frequency and probability distributions; sampling distributions and point estimation; central tendency, variability and z scores; the normal distribution and the central limit theorem; the logic of hypothesis testing; correlation and regression; multiple regression. Corequisite: 503L. {Fall}

title: Design and Analysis of Experiments
description: Introduction to the logic of experimental design and to experimental designs commonly used in psychology and the corresponding analyses. Corequisite: 504L. {Spring}

title: Advanced Statistics Laboratory
description: Computational techniques for statistical methods introduced in 501. Emphasis placed on the use of a computerized statistical package, e.g., SPSS. Corequisite: 501 or permission of instructor. {Fall}

title: Design and Analysis of Experiments Laboratory
description: Practical issues related to material introduced in 502. Emphasis placed on use of a computerized statistical package, e.g. SPSS. Corequisite: 502 or permission of instructor. {Spring}

title: Research Seminar
description: Facilitates development of active research in first-year graduate students. Presentations include 1) research lectures by faculty and graduate students; and 2) research proposals by class members, critiqued by instructor and classmates.

title: Seminar in Mathematical Psychology
description: Discussion of recent research in various areas of mathematical psychology, including behavioral decision theory and mathematical learning theory.

title: Teaching Seminar
description: The course is designed to prepare graduate students, both conceptually and practically, for teaching, with the construction of a professional teaching portfolio as its goal.

title: Psychological Research with Diverse Populations
description: This course introduces students to various structural factors that may be challenging for different populations, and different research methods and approaches to engaging and working with diverse populations in culturally appropriate and effective manners.

title: Advanced Health Psychology
description: This course will examine research and theory on important issues in health psychology including stress, health behaviors, and managing chronic disease. Learning tools include analyzing, synthesizing, and integrating readings and discussing them in class.

title: Health Psychology Interventions
description: This course will examine research and theory on application of psychological interventions to health problems including coping with illness and health behavior change.  The interventions will include stress management, mediation, and cognitive behavior therapies.

title: Health Disparities
description:
This course introduces students to various significant factors that affect health outcomes for a population differently that the general US population, and culturally appropriate research approaches in working with diverse populations experiencing a disparity.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Seminar on Cognitive Development
description: A seminar covering theory and research on the development of cognition, organized around Piaget's constructivist model of cognitive development and subsequent challenges, both theoretical and empirical, to that model.

title: Pre-Clinical Practicum
description: This is a core course required for doctoral students in clinical psychology.  The purpose of this course is to introduce you to concepts and basic skills that will prepare you for practicum experiences.  It is intended as a general introduction to concepts, practices and skills that are common to all methods of psychotherapy, and will be complimentary to the individual and group supervision you will be receiving.

title: Seminar in Psychopathology
description:
A research-bases course that provides a comprehensive study of abnormal behavior. It stresses diagnosis and assessment of psychopathology and examines various theories of etiology. Recommended treatments are mentioned briefly.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Psychological Evaluation: Cognitive and Neuropsychology Functions
description: Provides an introduction to intelligence testing, contemporary factors influencing intellectual performance, and clinical interpretation of cognitive tests. The neuropsychological implications of cognitive deficits are reviewed, along with different approaches to neuropsychological assessment.

title: Introduction to Clinical Science
description:
This course is intended to serve as an introduction to the issues and literature concerned with the science of clinical psychology.
Restriction: admitted to Ph.D. Psychology.

title: Child Psychopathology
description: Theories and practices related to an understanding of children and adolescents who deviate from normal development either intellectually, educationally, emotionally, physically or in some combination. Relevant family variables are considered.

title: Biological Bases of Behavior
description: Provides an introduction to basic aspects of neuroscience; e.g., historical perspectives, neurocytology, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, neuropharmacology, neuroanatomy. In depth critical discussion of fundamental and current topics.

title: Introduction to Functional Neuroimaging
description:
Provides an introduction to functional neuroimaging, from its history to the present. Technical aspects of commonly used neuroimaging methods will be discussed, along with their interpretation and application to questions in psychology and medicine. 
Restriction: Psychology graduate students only.

title: Seminar in Recovery of Function and Epilepsy
description: Focuses on the literature and current experiments on epilepsy and functional recovery, the two major problems following traumatic brain injury or stroke. Mechanisms of these processes and clinical advancements will be discussed.

title: Functional Neuroanatomy
description: The basics of human neuroanatomy and brain networks are described based on data recorded by functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), diffusion tensor imaging (DTI), positron emission tomography PET, electroencephalography (EEG), magnetoencephalography (MEG) and transcranial stimulation

title: Graduate Problems
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Cognitive Processes I
description: Surveys the major topics and issues in lower order cognitive processes. Includes coverage of fundamental theoretical and empirical work in sensory detection, attention, perception, and motor control.

title: Seminar in Thought and Language
description: (Also offered as EDPY, LING 565) The role of language in human cognition is approached from a sociocultural framework. Topics: semiotic systems, languages of the mind, categorization, problem solving, and cognitive pluralism.

title: Seminar in Psycholinguistics
description:
(Also offered as LING 568)
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Multivariate Statistics
description: Course provides overview of multivariate (MV) methods often used in psychology: data screening; multiple regression, MANOVA, MANCOVA, discriminant analysis, exploratory factor analysis, multiway frequency logistic models; brief introductions to structural equation models and multi-level models. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Latent Variable Modeling
description: The goal of this course is to cover a range of latent variable models that can be used to characterize unobserved variables, including factor analysis, factor mixture modeling, and latent growth and growth mixture models.

title: Case Conference Practicum
description: A bimonthly seminar learning experience where clinical graduate students meet with faculty to present and discuss ongoing, interesting or challenging cases. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Culture and Human Development
description: This course introduces students to concept and method in cultural psychology and developmental science, focusing on how development proceeds within a variety of cultural contexts and at various levels of psychological functioning.

title: Seminar in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy
description: Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

title: Psychotherapy Practicum I
description:
Graduate students in the doctoral program in clinical psychology provide psychotherapy to clients in the UNM Psychology Clinic under the supervision of the clinical faculty.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Ethics and Professional Issues in Clinical Psychology
description: This seminar is principally concerned with ethics in psychology research, service delivery, training and teaching. Topics in career development and contemporary controversies in professional psychology are also dealt with.

title: Diversity Multicultural Perspectives in Clinical Psychology
description:
This course provides an overview of multicultural counseling including the principles of understanding one's values and biases, understanding and respecting the client's worldview, and using culturally appropriate interventions.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Empirically Supported Treatments
description: The purpose of the seminar is to provide a broad overview of the empirically-supported treatments movement and to teach student how to effectively deliver empirically-supported treatments for specific psychological disorders.

title: Seminar in Cognition, Brain and Behavior
description: Critical examination of recent empirical and theoretical articles on behavioral/cognitive neuroscience topics selected by students.

title: Psychobiology of Emotion
description: The course reviews the psychological science of emotion, particularly the application of objective physiological and neural measurements. Topics will include historical perspective, neuroscience of emotion, physiology of stress, disorders of emotion, and more.

title: Advanced EEG Analysis in MatLab
description: The goal of the course is to provide a conceptual, mathematical, and pragmatic understanding of neural time series data, especially in regard to electroencephalographic (EEG) signals.

title: Special Topics in Psychology
description: Study of any psychological topic not otherwise included in the curriculum upon expression of mutual interest by students and faculty. {Offered upon demand}

title: Clinical Internship
description: Available only to students who have successfully completed their dissertations. This is a one-year, full-time external clinical internship in which students provide treatment, assessment, and other relevant professional services under intensive and direct professional supervision.

title: Dissertation
description: Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Special Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Radiation Control Technology Boot Camp
description:
Twelve-week boot camp-style course designed to prepare students for a career in radiation control technology. Topics include monitoring environmental radioactivity levels, responding in emergencies, and performing decontamination procedures.
Students must be accepted into an approved Radiation Control Technology Apprenticeship program with local employer.
Corequisite: 1110L.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Radiation Control Technology Boot Camp Laboratory
description:
Students will learn how to use appropriate tools and practices to monitor environmental radioactivity levels, respond in emergencies, perform decontamination procedures, and other job related tasks to radiation control technology.
Students must be accepted into an approved Radiation Control Technology Apprenticeship program with local employer.
Corequisite: 1110.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Fundamentals of Radiographic Imaging
description: This course provides students with an in-depth knowledge of radiographic exposure techniques and the factors affecting radiographic image quality. It covers digital imaging with related accessories and emphasizes radiographic critique for diagnostic quality control. Additionally, the course reinforces radiologic fundamentals, introducing instrumentation and technology used in digital imaging. Topics include capturing, processing, displaying, and managing digital images, as well as exposure techniques and exposure indicators.  Laboratory activities are provided to reinforce radiographic concepts.

title: Radiographic Positioning I
description: This course covers the fundamental principles and techniques of diagnostic radiography, including radiographic procedures, patient positioning, terminology, and the mechanics of image production.  Students will learn and apply these concepts to the upper and lower extremities, chest, abdomen, and pelvis/hip.  The course emphasizes radiographic anatomy, particularly osteology and arthrology, as it relates to routine radiographic procedures.

title: Radiographic Positioning I Laboratory
description: Applied clinical skills Laboratory in radiographic procedures and positioning concepts, techniques, terminology, and mechanics related to upper and lower extremities, chest, abdomen, and pelvis/hip.

title: Radiographic Positioning II
description: Continuation of Radiographic Positioning I, including a study of the osteology and arthrology of the thorax, vertebral column, skull, facial bones, and sinuses. Discussion and demonstration of the related standard and special radiographic projections represent the fundamentals of radiographic anatomy, positioning, and terminology used in routine fluoroscopic procedures of the gastrointestinal and genitourinary tract, with discussion and demonstration of the related radiographic projections.

title: Radiographic Positioning II Lab
description: Continuation of Radiographic Positioning I. Applied Clinical Skills Laboratory: Includes vertebral column, skull, facial bones, sinuses, gastrointestinal, urinary, and fluoroscopic procedures.

title: Introduction to Radiologic Technology and Patient Care
description: This course provides a foundational overview of the radiology profession, encompassing basic patient care skills, professional standards, and ethical/legal principles. Students will learn essential skills such as patient transportation, vital sign assessment, and effective communication, while also being introduced to specific radiographic patient care techniques, including history taking, immobilization, managing medical emergencies, and the administration of contrast media and radiopharmaceuticals. Additionally, the course will cover core concepts of radiation protection and basic and specialized procedures, all within the framework of professional ethics and terminology.

title: Introduction to Radiologic Technology and Patient Care Lab
description: This course provides a foundational overview of the radiology profession, encompassing basic patient care skills, professional standards, and ethical/legal principles. Students will complete skills laboratory and simulation exercises to demonstrate competency in patient transportation, vital sign assessment, venipuncture, and effective communication, while also being introduced to specific radiographic patient care techniques, including history taking, immobilization, managing medical emergencies, and the administration of contrast media and radiopharmaceuticals.

title: Introduction to Radiation Safety for Radiologic Technologists
description: This course is an introduction to radiation protection topics that are common to Radiography and Nuclear Medicine. Topics to be covered include radiation units, radioactivity, radiation sources, background radiation, biological effects of radiation, dose limits, radiation shielding, methods of dose reduction, and regulations.

title: Radiation Biology and Radiation Protection
description: This course explores the biological effects of ionizing radiation on human cells and tissues, including genetic, somatic, short-term, and long-term effects. It covers radiation measurements, policies, and protection measures for technologists, patients, and others, emphasizing minimizing exposure and basic protection methods. The course also reviews current regulations and recommendations by the NRCP and ICRP.

title: Radiographic Image Critique
description: This course reviews radiographs, evaluating anatomy and technical quality, including positioning and technique. Students learn to make adjustments and reinforce concepts through classroom activities.

title: Radiographic Special Modalities
description: Introduction and discussion of various special procedures and modalities, including but not limited to: interventional radiography, computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, nuclear medicine, radiation therapy, and ultrasound.

title: Radiographic Physics and Equipment
description: This course offers a comprehensive exploration of the physical principles of diagnostic radiography, covering radiologic physics, imaging theory, and quality assurance. Students will examine atomic structure, electromagnetism, X-ray production and interaction, exposure factors, scatter control, and image characteristics. The curriculum also includes image production factors related to digital fluoroscopy, X-ray circuitry, and an introduction to advanced imaging modalities such as mammography, CT, and MRI.

title: Clinical Experience I
description: Clinical Experience: Students will complete administrative duties, perform diagnostic radiography exams, and provide patient care under the direct/indirect supervision of a qualified registered radiologic technologist as required and assigned.

title: Clinical Experience II
description: Clinical Experience: Students will complete administrative duties, perform diagnostic radiograph exams, and provide patient care under direct/indirect supervision of a qualified registered radiologic technologist as assigned. Evening/weekend, CT, and OR rotations will be offered where available.

title: Clinical Experience III
description: Clinical Experience: Students will complete administrative duties, perform diagnostic radiography exams, and provide patient care under the direct/indirect supervision of a qualified registered radiologic technologist as required and assigned. Evening/weekend, CT and OR rotations will be offered where available.

title: Clinical Experience IV
description: Continuation of Clinical Education III. Students will complete administrative duties, perform basic diagnostic radiograph exams, and provide patient care under direct/indirect supervision of a qualified registered radiologic technologist as assigned. Evening and OR rotations will be offered where available.

title: Clinical Experience V
description: Students will complete administrative duties, perform basic diagnostic radiography exams, and provide patient care under the direct/indirect supervision of a qualified registered radiologic technologist as required and assigned. Evening, OR, and specialized modality rotations will be offered where available.

title: Radiologic Technology Capstone
description: This course provides a comprehensive review of the material required to pass the ARRT certification exam in radiography. Individualized study plans are made through the collaboration of the student and instructor and consist of study and test-taking strategies, formative and summative learning activities, and reflection assignments. Course content includes professional behaviors, resume writing, interview skills, and professional digital networking.

title: Patient Care
description:
This course acquaints the student with nursing procedures and techniques emphasizing the role of the diagnostic imager as a member of the healthcare team.
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Patient Care Lab
description:
This course covers the laboratory activities associated with 330.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: MRI Safety
description: This is an introductory course designed to develop the knowledge and practical skills required to ensure safe operation within the MRI environment. The course emphasizes identification of MRI-related hazards, implementation of established MRI safety protocols, and maintenance of controlled MRI safety zones. Students examine key risk areas associated with static magnetic fields, gradient fields, and radiofrequency energy, with a focus on risk prevention and mitigation strategies.

title: MRI Image Identification
description: This course provides an in-depth study of cross-sectional anatomy as demonstrated on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Emphasis is placed on anatomical structures, spatial relationships, and imaging landmarks essential for accurate MRI interpretation and protocol selection.

title: MRI Protocols I
description: This introductory course is designed to develop the knowledge and practical skills required to ensure safe operation within the MRI environment. The course emphasizes identification of MRI-related hazards, implementation of established MRI safety protocols, and maintenance of controlled MRI safety zones.

title: MRI Protocols II
description: This course is a continuation of RADS 342 MRI Protocol I course, building on MRI protocol design with an emphasis on MRI safety, and collaboration with the healthcare sedation teams. The foundation of protocol development, MRI physics, pulse sequences, advanced techniques such as BLADE and GRAPPA, and receiver bandwidth optimization is covered.

title: MRI Image Analysis
description: This course is a comprehensive study of MRI imaging parameters, pulse sequence categories, and artifact recognition with an emphasis on practical application of identifying MRI images. Students will develop technical proficiency and critical thinking skills through hands-on activities, assignments, and assessments.

title: Healthcare Delivery and Compliance
description:
Students are introduced to the history and current trends of the American Healthcare system. Course material will reflect the current healthcare system and the effect the roles have on improving the system.
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Medical Language Systems Review
description:
This self-study course reviews the major systems of the human body, using a programmed textbook/workbook. The workbook format is combined with simple, non-technical explanations of medical terms and descriptions of anatomy, physiology, and pathology.
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Topics in Healthcare Ethics and Diversity
description:
Students will participate in discussion and reflection on current and landmark healthcare cases. Course activities are designed to allow students to examine and explore ethical issues in the medical field.
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Introduction to Research and Medical Imaging
description:
The course will focus on the development of a Healthcare research question, completion of an annotated bibliography and a final literature review. This includes exercises in formatting, paraphrasing and avoiding plagiarism.
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Community Engagement and Service Learning
description:
The course offers students the opportunity to engage in community service and field experience. Students are exposed to the theories and practices of community service, information literacy, patient care, team building and leadership activities. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences and permission of instructor.

title: UNM Radiologic Sciences Independent Study course
description: This course is designed to provide students with a versatile educational experience in either (BSRS), CT, MRI and or Nuclear Medicine programs. This course offers students the flexibility to emphasize either clinical competency or didactic instruction based on their individual learning preferences and course objectives.

title: Physics of Computed Tomography
description:
Course provides instruction in physics and instrumentation related to computed tomography including the history of the development of computed tomography, system operation and components, image formation and reconstruction, characteristics of image quality, and artifact recognition/reduction. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences or CERT Computed Tomography or CERT Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

title: Physics of Computed Tomography II
description:
A continuation of RAD 410 further providing advance instruction in physics and instrumentation related to computed tomography, including the history of the development of computed tomography, system operation and components, image formation and reconstruction.
Prerequisite: 410.  
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Computed Tomography Physics Review
description: Comprehensive examinations and topic review will take place throughout the semester. The registry review examinations will encompass the listed topics for students to demonstrate a basic knowledge of computed tomography and physics.

title: Leadership and Professional Communication in Radiologic Science
description: This upper-division course prepares students for leadership roles in medical imaging by exploring ethical leadership, governance structures, and quality management systems. Students will examine leadership styles and models, organizational and jurisdictional governance, and strategies for team development and performance improvement. The course also introduces foundational concepts in budget management and public health, including epidemiology and its relevance to imaging services. Through case studies, simulations, and collaborative projects, students will develop the skills necessary to lead effectively, manage resources, and contribute to population health initiatives in diverse healthcare environments.

title: Computed Tomography Clinical I
description:
Course provides clinical instruction in computed tomography including the system operation and components, image formation and reconstruction, characteristics of image quality, and artifact recognition/reduction. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Computed Tomography Clinical III
description:
Course provides additional clinical instruction in computed tomography including the system operation and components, image formation and reconstruction, characteristics of image quality, and artifact recognition/reduction. 
Prerequisite: 420 and 421. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: MRI Advanced Scanning Seminar
description: This course builds upon foundational MRI physics and clinical scanning skills, and introduces students to complex imaging applications, advanced pulse sequences, and specialized protocols used in current MRI practice. Emphasis is placed on professional conduct, interdisciplinary communication, and patient safety, particularly in advanced and sedated scanning environments.

title: MRI Special Topics
description: This course introduces students to an in-depth study of advanced MRI protocol design with emphasis on patient-centered imaging, safety, and clinical decision-making.

title: MRI Research Seminar
description: This course is designed for students to experience MRI scanning in the research environment. This rotation introduces students to MRI research environments with an emphasis on patient-centered protocol design, professional conduct, and research fundamentals.

title: Physics of Magnetic Resonance Imaging I
description:
Course provides additional instruction in physics and instrumentation related to magnetic resonance imaging including the history of the development of magnetic resonance imaging, system operation and components, image formation and reconstruction, characteristics of image quality, and artifact recognition/reduction. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences or CERT Computed Tomography or CERT Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

title: Physics of Magnetic Resonance Imaging II
description:
Course is a continuation of RADS 450. 
Prerequisite: 450. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences or CERT Computed Tomography or CERT Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

title: MRI Physics Review
description:
Comprehensive examinations and topic review will take place throughout the semester. The registry review examinations will encompass the listed topics for students to demonstrate a basic knowledge of Magnetic Resonance Imaging and physics
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: MRI Clinical I
description:
Course provides clinical instruction in magnetic resonance imaging including system operation and components, image formation and reconstruction, characteristics of image quality, and artifact recognition/reduction. 
Corequisite: 450. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: MRI Clinical II
description:
Course is a continuation of RADS 460. 
Prerequisite: 460. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences or CERT Computed Tomography or CERT Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

title: MRI Clinical III
description:
Practical experience in the performance of magnetic resonance imaging studies under direct supervision of certified technologists and staff radiologists. Includes competency examinations, image evaluation, patient care assessment, imaging protocols, and safety issues.
Prerequisite: 461.  
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: MRI Clinical IV
description: This clinical course provides students with hands-on experience in MRI clinical environments, emphasizing patient-centered care, professional conduct, and practical application of imaging protocols. Students will complete clinical site orientations, participate in supervised clinical classes, and receive feedback through direct observation.

title: Human Cross Sectional Anatomy
description:
Course examines three-dimensional relationships of skull, brain, CNS, thorax, abdomen, and pelvis, correlating this information with imaging modalities (CT, MRI, Nuclear Medicine).
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Radiologic Sciences.

title: Human Cross Sectional Pathology
description:
Course examines a basic understanding of the principles of pathology relating to imaging science and the radiographic appearance of specific diseases and processes within the body systems.
Restriction: enrolled in the Radiologic Sciences program.

title: Introduction to Population Health
description: Introduction to Population Health provides a population perspective on the multi-level (ecologic) determinants of health, disease and injury; the programs, policies and services that protect and promote health and prevent disease; and the analytic tools used by population health professionals.

title: Global Health Challenges and Responses
description: Global health is concerned with developing theories and methods to understand the roots of social, economic, political and environmental determinants of health, with a focus on the nature of health inequalities across the world.

title: Introduction to Community Health Work
description: This course provides an overview of history and introduction to the profession, including scope of practice, roles, competencies, attributes and qualities. It provides practice-based guidelines for professional conduct, ethics and boundaries, confidentiality and informed consent.

title: Clinical Basics for Frontline Health Workers
description: Basic skills Preparation for working in clinical and home health settings: vital signs, blood glucose monitoring, foot exams, calculating BMI, OSHA standards, medical terminology, documentation, standing orders, opioid overdose reversal, and Basic Life Support.

title: Mental Health First Responder
description:
Students will learn to offer initial help in a mental health or substance use crisis through a 5-step action plan, with the ultimate goal to connect persons to appropriate professional, peer, social, and self-help care.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Fundamentals of One Health
description: Introduction to One Health and its collaborative approach to design and implement research programs, policies and legislation that covers multiple sectors to achieve better health outcomes which includes: Introduction to biological disease basis, including cellular, physiological, genetic, immunologic, and environmental determinants. Pathologic mechanisms and host susceptibility for infectious and non-infectious diseases of population health importance including disease prevention/control delivered in a team-based format.

title: Community Health Work: Frameworks, Principles, and Practices for Direct Service
description: This course provides a broad introduction to public health as the framework for providing direct services to individuals, with focus on the ecological model, health equity, and healthcare systems.

title: Health Coaching and Health Promotion
description: This course provides a strong foundation in health coaching and health promotion. It offers strategies and skills for supporting behavior change including action planning, motivational interviewing, and communication skills.

title: Health Education and Facilitation
description: This course provides an overview of the major approaches, models, skills, and knowledge needed to plan, facilitate, and evaluate basic health education presentations for groups in clinical and community settings.

title: Community Health Outreach, Assessment and Advocacy
description: This course provides an overview of various approaches, models, methods, and considerations for conducting home visiting, health outreach, community organizing, and advocacy for improving community health.

title: Population Health: Introduction to Social, Cultural, Behavioral Theory
description:
Examine and apply social, cultural, and behavioral theories and models that inform and strengthen population health practice. Theories are used to diagnose health and social problems and plan interventions to improve the public's health.

Prerequisite: 101 and 102.

title: Introduction to Environmental and Occupational Health
description:
Environmental and occupational health and safety; including regulatory processes, compliance, pollution prevention, drinking water, wastewater management, solid/hazardous waste, air quality, food protection, zoonotic and vector-borne disease control, radiation, injury prevention.
Prerequisite: 101 and 102.

title: Health Systems, Services and Policies
description:
Increase the analytical capacity and critical thinking in regards to the US health system, services and related policies. Reinforce the students' sense of social responsibility and further their understanding of population health approaches. 
Prerequisite: 101 and 102.

title: Fundamentals of Health Care Finance
description:
This course will introduce basic concepts of health care finance: access, affordability, quality, and portability.
Prerequisite: 101 and 102.

title: Special Topics
description: Topics in population health that are not covered in the existing curriculum that are mutually agreed upon by students and faculty. May be repeated for credit provided the subject matter varies.

title: Population Health Research Methods
description: This course provides students with an introduction to the process of conducting population health research. Students will learn how to construct a research question, develop hypotheses, and how to design a study that fits the research question and adequately tests those hypotheses.

title: Essentials of Epidemiology: The Language of Population Health
description: Essentials of Epidemiology: The Language of Population Health, provides students with an introduction to the analytic tools and methods used to quantify and understand population health problems.  We will read and discuss how epidemiologists monitor, quantify and map disease, and critically assess the impact of medical treatments and public health interventions. We will learn how to calculate rates and risks, to use online sources of population health data, to interpret epidemiologic measures, and to create effective tables and graphs to convey scientific information.

title: Health Data Systems
description:
This course introduces key concepts and issues surrounding the adoption and use of information systems within public health and health service organizations. Current issues surrounding the gathering, storage, and usage of health information will be discussed. 
The success of this course relies heavily upon how engaged participants are in the various components of the course. The role of the instructor is to facilitate and guide learning through class discussions, activities, and feedback. The course favors an active role for participants over the more passive role taken in a lecture-oriented format. Be prepared to engage the course material and each other to draw on assigned readings and your experiences—whether those experiences are drawn from a summer internship or work experience from outside the population health space.  We will work from the premise that all participants bring important knowledge, skills, experiences and insights to the course that we can draw upon to create a successful collaborative learning experience.

title: Population Health Management
description: This course will focus on applying public/population health concepts and skill sets to practical scenarios. Throughout the semester, we will watch interviews with several experienced professionals who will share their insights in population health management. Consequently, whether you aspire to pursue a career in academia as an instructor or researcher or intend to practice in the real world of public health, healthcare, community-based services, or a related private enterprise, the concepts and skill sets we will explore in this course will be invaluable in your future endeavors. Please be advised that a diverse range of concepts will be covered throughout the semester. They are not presented in a sequential manner.

title: Population Health Evidenced-Based Practices
description: Population Health Evidenced-Based Practices introduces students to core concepts in population-based primary and secondary prevention strategies, settings, populations, and population health issues. Students will critically examine evidence-based guidelines used to inform population health interventions, systematic reviews, resources for best and promising practices, in addition to other sources of evidence. Students will be exposed to principles of community and clinical practice.

title: Assessment and Planning
description:
Processes for planning health systems (services and population health outcomes) comprise of a range of activities that share the goal of improving health outcomes while improving the efficiency, quality and effectiveness of providing health-related services, many of which may be outside the venue of traditional health services. Health systems’ planning is a process to identify and develop health- enhancing interventions. It does this by creating an actionable link between needs and resources.  Processes for assessing population health systems’ needs will be addressed. This course examines how health systems tackle both downstream and upstream determinants of health. Population health planning compares two concepts: first, it moves away from the idea that health services alone can produce the desired improvement in illness of the population; but it also acknowledges that health systems have a responsibility to collaborate with other partners to attain health and wellness goals and population health processes. Health systems planning has the most potential for payoff in improved health as it can include health services, community services (health-related and those unrelated to health), and government agencies (local, county, state etc.) and requires collaboration among those entities to achieve ultimate impact.

-	Health service: any service (i.e. not limited to medical or clinical services) aimed at contributing to improved health or to the prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation of sick people. 
-	Health system: (i) all the activities whose primary purpose is to promote, restore and/or       maintain health; (ii) the people, institutions and resources, arranged together in accordance with established policies, to improve the health of the population they serve, while responding to people’s legitimate expectations and protecting them against the cost of ill-health through a variety of activities whose primary intent is to improve health.

title: Program Evaluation
description:
Building off PH 421, this course will describe and apply the implementation and evaluation processes for health systems change to protect and promote population health. We will cover the final 4 steps in the Health Planning Overview – the Eight Step Process (this class will address Steps 5-8) 

Step 1. Organizing and Planning
Step 2. Engaging the Community/Mobilizing
Step 3. Conduct Community Health Needs/Assets Assessment 
Step 4. Prioritize Issues
Step 5. Develop Measurable Goals and Objectives
Step 6. Develop Community Health Improvement Plan
Step 7. Implement and Monitor the Plan
Step 8. Evaluate Processes and Outcomes

title: Special Topics in Population Health
description: Variable topics in population health provides students to work with faculty on topics that are not part of the curriculum.

title: Introduction to Maternal and Child Public Health
description: This course focuses on the public health concepts related to determinants of health status and health disparities; behavioral, cultural, social, and political theories of disease and health inequities; prevention and health promotion; community-based interventions; and health policy.

title: Maternal Child Health Seminar I
description: This seminar focuses on the description and history of Maternal and Child Health programs, Title V programs (national and state), the populations served by Title V programs along with their specific needs, the life course perspective, and the developmental cycles.

title: Maternal Child Health Seminar II
description: This course focuses on career paths and the variety of professions working in maternal child health, policy initiatives in maternal child health on the national, state and local level, and evidence-based research on best practices in maternal child health programs.

title: Maternal Child Health Seminar III
description: MCH Seminar focuses on MCH in local communities.  Because New Mexico is primarily a rural/ frontier state with many diverse communities and high rates of poverty and Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs), this seminar will focus on the needs of communities, the services available, and the challenges involved in receiving services.

title: Population Health Capstone
description: The Population Health Capstone provides students an opportunity to earn 6 credits via fieldwork and reflective written and on-line assignments. This asynchronous class is intended to support an intensive population health placement working in a population or public health venue.

title: Ecosystems, Food, Farming, and People's Health
description: This course introduces the complex interactions among Earth systems, food production, and population health and explores the potential social and health benefits and challenges of the organic/sustainable movement as an alternative to globalization. Guest speakers include experts from a variety of disciplines and backgrounds.

title: Population Health Independent Study
description:
Students work individually with faculty members on projects with individual supervision.
Prerequisite: 101 and 102.
Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Determinants and Equity in Public Health
description:
Concepts of public health related to determinants of health; cultural, social and political concepts of disease; disease prevention; health promotion, including individual behavior change and community based intervention; health policy. 
Restriction: admitted to M.P.H. Public Health.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology Public Health
description: This course is an introduction to the basic epidemiologic methods used in population and public health.  This is a graduate level course intended for Master of Public Health students and others who would like to learn epidemiologic concepts and study designs in public health.  For those planning to participate in the epidemiology concentration of the MPH, this course serves as a prerequisite for additional advanced courses.

title: Environmental-Occupational Health
description: Applies the public health perspective to environmental and occupational disease. Students will learn to apply the ecologic principles of agent, host and environment to diseases associated with exposures to the physical environment and chemical contaminants. Prerequisite: 501.

title: Health Care Systems
description: This course provides an overview of the US health system and the main policies that shaped it, including the complex infrastructure of public health and the health care system, analyzing their historical development, organization, financing, and delivery of services; its performance compared to other health care systems; the key interest groups that shape the health policies; and the main policy challenges, scenarios for improvement, and proposals for change.

title: Public Health and Health Care Management
description: This course will focus on the core functions of public health and healthcare management and the leadership and management skills needed to achieve defined goals of public health programs. The role and elements of professionalism and ethics will be integrated throughout the course.

title: Global Health
description: This master’s level course provides an overview of the core topics and perspectives on global health, including the global conditions and structural determinants of health, the policies that shaped the field, the governance and cooperation systems, and the complex itinerary of the health systems in high and LMIC.

title: Public Health Seminar
description:
A graduate seminar and journal club focusing on a wide range of PH topics. Speakers present original research. Journal club guides students to critically assess literature. Two semesters required for credit.
Prerequisite: 502.

title: Health Care Law and Ethics
description: This course provides an overview of healthcare law and ethics with a primary focus on state and federal jurisdictions. Major legal and ethical concepts and their impact on the healthcare industry and the practice of public health will be explored. Various legal topics will be explored through the lens of the healthcare setting.

title: Social Epidemiology
description:
Social Epidemiology provides students with principles and methods of design, conduct analysis, and interpretation of epidemiologic research using a social epidemiology approach.
Prerequisite: 502 with a grade of "B" or better. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Infectious Disease Epidemiology
description: This course introduces students to concepts, methods, and theories of infectious disease epidemiology.  This is a graduate level course intended for Master of Public Health students and others who would like to learn about infectious diseases.

title: Perinatal Epidemiology
description: Review of a wide range of topics central to perinatal epidemiology. Highlighted topics will include conception and early fetal loss, design issues in studies of adverse reproductive outcomes and epidemiologic aspects and public health approaches to prevention of congenital malformation. Prerequisite: 502.

title: Public Health Research Methods
description: Quantitative, qualitative and mixed methods of applying and understanding the scientific inquiry used in health and social sciences research. Identify health-related problems and formulate questions that can be translated into sound research designs.

title: Epidemiology Data Analysis
description:
Students will learn how to conduct a careful epidemiologic data analysis. The focus of the course is developing the practical and critical thinking skills to conduct an epidemiologic data analysis. This course is required for epidemiology concentration students. 
Prerequisite: 502, 537, 538, 539.

title: Introduction to Biostatistics in Public Health
description: This course provides an introduction to biostatistics with a focus on its application in public health. Topics covered include random events, basic probability theory, sampling, estimation, hypothesis testing, categorical data analysis, nonparametric tests, correlation, simple linear regression, and One-Way ANOVA. Emphasis is placed on understanding statistical concepts and their practical implementation in public health research and practice.

title: Advanced Epidemiology Methods
description:
This course is designed to provide students with a greater depth of knowledge of the principles and methods central to the design, conduct, analysis, and interpretation of epidemiologic work.

Prerequisite: 503.

Restriction: permission of advisor.

title: Epidemiology and Biostatistics Lab
description:
Covers basic statistical methods, including statistical summaries and inference. Methods of summarizing data include graphical displays and numerical summaries. Statistical inference includes hypothesis testing and confidence intervals. Methods for continuous and categorical data are studied. 
Prerequisite: MATH 1220 with a grade of "B" or better.

title: Advanced Biostatistics
description:
Covers basic models used in the statistical analysis of studies in the medical sciences and public health field, with an emphasis on epidemiology. Linear regression, analysis of variance, logistic regression, and survival models are studied. 
Prerequisite: 538.

title: Interventions for Health Equity
description: An exploration of rational health planning methods. Methods will be applied in the development of a health program plan within a social context where public health planning actually occurs. Involves the development of a realistic program plan addressing a health-related problem and writing a proposal for funding.

title: Health Policy, Politics and Social Equity
description: Provides students with understanding of factors within and outside of health care system. Reviews social policy in addressing coverage of uninsured, improving population health and reducing social inequities. Examination of frameworks and politics of policy programs.

title: Public Health Program Planning and Evaluation
description: Effective program evaluation is a systematic way to improve and account for public health actions by involving procedures that are useful, feasible, ethical, and accurate (CDC, 1999). This course introduces students to the theories, principles, methods, and procedures to plan and evaluate public health promotion and education programs. Topics include the review of public health program planning, the connecting between program planning and evaluation, types of evaluation, development of evaluation research questions, evaluation designs, measurement, data collection, statistical analysis, research ethics/IRB and reporting and dissemination.

title: Community Participatory-Based Research
description: Presents historical background, underlying assumptions, principles and steps for conducting CBPR. Introduces indigenous and decolonizing theories and methodologies of CBPR. Develops perspective on roles of culture, race, gender, and power in academic-community research partnerships.

title: Conducting Research with Marginalized Populations
description: There is a pressing need to meet the health and social needs of diverse populations, particularly those who experience prejudice, discrimination and racism; barriers to education, public services and decent housing; the burden of underemployment, inequalities and isolation. These populations experience considerable disparities that undermine our communities. Their needs can only be addressed through more integrated research approaches that inform effective practices and programs. We should not assume that traditional research methods would work with every group; in fact, we know that they do not.

title: Special Topics in Public Health
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Welcome to the Frontline: essential Public Health Roles for MD's
description: This course will prepare medical students and residents for their essential roles in the public health system, including public health surveillance, vital data collection, emergency preparedness, and health policy and advocacy. Students will meet experts in each of these four areas and demonstrate competency in each essential role through completion of assessments and demonstrated proficiency in related core skill sets. During the semester, students will plan and present a 30-minute seminar or recruit an expert speaker to present a 30-minute seminar, on a topic of their choice related to one of the essential roles. Each student will also create a 4-6 page paper or a 1-2 page issue or policy brief. A rubric will be provided for the seminar assignment and writing assignments.

title: Public Health and Health Care Communication
description: Health communication is the study of how health information is generated and disseminated and how that information affects individuals, community groups, institutions and public policy.

title: Introduction to Maternal and Child Public Health
description: Introduction to Maternal & Child Public Health focuses on the public health concepts related to determinants of health status and health disparities; behavioral, cultural, social, and political theories of disease and health inequities; prevention and health promotion; community-based interventions; and health policy. It includes historical, theoretical, skills-based, and practical approaches using case examples drawn from major health and health dis-parities problems in New Mexico and the United States.

title: Maternal Child Health Seminar I
description: The MCH Seminar I focuses on the description and history of MCH, Title V programs (national and state), populations served by Title V programs along with their specific needs, life course perspective, and developmental cycles.

title: Maternal Child Health Seminar II
description: Maternal Child Health Seminar 2 focuses on career paths and the variety of professions working in maternal child health, policy initiatives on the national, state and local level and evidence-based research best practices.

title: Maternal Child Health Seminar III
description: This seminar focuses on MCH in local communities. The impacts of poverty on women, children and families will be examined through readings, webinars, trainings, videos, discussions, reflections, and guest lectures.

title: Indigenous Health Policy
description: This course will integrate physical, cultural, social, political, intellectual and emotional wellbeing of Indigenous populations globally. This course examines the health as both a collective and individual inter-generational continuum that links to health status, health practices, health data, health equity, health policy and health systems. This will include relevant health care, communities, traditional healers, health policy and stakeholders. This course will examine health from a historical, present and future context while integrating emerging issues that impact health.

title: Health Systems and Globalization
description:
This seminar will examine international health care systems to identify commonalities and unique differences in structure and outcomes. Particular attention will be directed toward the financing, payer structure, delivery system models, health care equity, and what can be transferred across international boundaries. In addition, the impact global factors on health care national schemes will also be discussed.  

Advisor permission required

title: Independent Studies
description: Students work individually with faculty members on projects with individual supervision.

title: Master of Public Health Culminating Experience
description:
The Master of Public Health Culminating Experience is designed to integrate knowledge and skills of the core MPH and concentration competencies by applying these skills and knowledge to address relevant public health problems. Students also discuss and reflect on their own growth as public health professionals.
Prerequisites: 501, 502, 510, 538, 552, 554.

Restriction: admitted to M.P.H. Public Health.

title: Professional Paper
description: The professional paper allows the student to engage in analyzing or solving a real public health problem. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Public Health Practicum
description:
Individually arranged field experience to develop and refine professional public health skills. Offered on a CR/NC basis only. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only. Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Special Topics in Advanced Epidemiology
description: Special topics in advanced epidemiology for doctoral students in the Epidemiology Concentration.

title: Causal Inference Methods in Epidemiology
description: Causal inference is a movement in science spanning epidemiology, economics, statistics, political science, psychology, and many other academic disciplines. At its heart, causal inference seeks to ask when an “association” that is found in a research study can be considered “causal”. Since the 1980’s, the causal inference movement has introduced many innovations covered in this class, including advances in study design theory, potential outcomes notation, and directed acyclic graphs. In this course students will learn the history and uses of these innovations through the lens of epidemiological research.

title: Social Epidemiology Public Health
description: In this course we will explore epidemiological methods through the lens of social epidemiology. For the purposes of this course “social epidemiology” will be defined as the use of epidemiological methods to understand the relationship between the social determinants of health and health outcomes. I have structured the course so that for the beginning part of the course we explore a different topic area in social epidemiology week by week, followed by a discussion of interventions and study designs toward the end of the course. This course is not comprehensive of all social determinants and is meant to be an introduction to the field of social epidemiology.

title: Biostatistics Interdisciplinary Capstone Course
description: Students will be trained to members of a multi-disciplinary research team and generate study designs including statistical power calculation and analysis plans for research grant proposals; conduct analysis, and communicate results for dissemination.

title: Critical Methodologies and Social Justice Interventions
description: This course is designed to challenge the student to examine the correlation between the values of social justice, the literature on the social determinants of health (or causes of the causes, i.e. the environment in which a person lives, works, & plays), health equity and poor health outcomes, focusing on New Mexico. The course will explore the intersectionality of culture, race, class, gender, education, and other forms of identity, from a socio-political context, with implications for policy, practices, and programs that can transform socio-environmental conditions towards health.

title: Culture, Meaning and Power in Health Equity
description: In one of his seminal essays on culture, the anthropologist Clifford Geertz underlined the conviction that there is virtually no part of human life that is not shaped by culture. He wrote, “what man [sic] is may be so entangled with where he is, who he is, and what he believes that it is inseparable from them.” Yet, much contemporary research—including the science of health equity—still largely reduces culture to a basic set of characteristics to be controlled for or adapted to. This course will draw on perspectives across disciplines, including philosophy, anthropology, sociology, and community psychology, to explore how culture and power shape the health of individuals and populations, as well as our efforts to achieve health equity.

title: Theory-Based Intervention Research
description: Theory plays a pivotal role in the research process, serving as a foundation for justifying the selection of a topic and the development of research questions, design approaches, and analysis plans. Social and behavioral science theories have been instrumental in advancing public health research and practice. This course is designed for graduate students and investigators planning to conduct population or clinical research within a public health approach, offering a comprehensive overview of fundamental conceptual and methodological considerations through a health equity lens.

title: Introduction to Qualitative Methods
description: PH 654 is a course created for the COPH PhD in Health Equity Sciences program and focused on qualitative research and dissemination for health equity.  This course will introduce and explore qualitative methods including the major traditions, principles, and methods of qualitative inquiry. The focus of this course will provide PhD students with an opportunity to apply qualitative research design and knowledge development to assignments and develop critical skills to critique peer-reviewed qualitative research publications through a public health and health equity lens.

title: Community Based Participatory Research Lab
description: Provides Community Based Participatory Research doctoral concentration students with a mentored research lab to apply their understandings of community engaged and participatory theories, principles, design and methods in a concentration or community engaged research project.

title: Doctoral Special Topics
description: This is a special topics course created for the PhD in Health Equity Sciences.

title: Global Health
description: Global Health (International Health in its origins and, for many, still the appropriate concept to use), as a field of study, policy development, and practice, has been historically shaped by colonial expansion and industrialization forces supporting what became the global capitalist economy. Medicine, public health, and anthropology sciences accompanied this process by lessening a variety of ill outcomes through epidemics control and dissemination of Western clinical practices. The end of World War II marked the beginning of a jolted transition to decolonization movements in Africa, the rebuilding of Europe, and the age of the Cold War. This era, the era of multilateralism, brought legitimacy for international cooperation in development (including health), the creation of the United Nations and the World Health Organization. In the 1980s, global neoliberalism and the rise of private authority changed global governance and power relations. The International Monetary Fund and the World Bank led structural adjustments across low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), and privatizations profoundly impacted the health and social protection systems in most countries, molding new global health policies and, at the same time, generating widespread resistance by social movements and some global health and development players. This dynamic, intensified by the COVID-19 pandemic, persists until today, with ups and downs of all types, but within a trend characterized by the exacerbation of inequities between and among countries, regions, and entire continents. Of all aggravated inequities, health stands out.

title: Advanced Health Policy Analysis
description: This course discusses and explores theoretically driven methods in applied policy analysis through equity and social justice lens. Students will learn the application of five-steps in policy analysis from the approach of evidence informed policy making.

title: Doctoral Seminar
description: General Seminar course for the PhD in Health Equity Sciences.

title: Doctoral Dissertation Hours
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Industrial Robotics Operations
description: This course covers basic robotics operations, including robotics system components, peripherals, robot set-up, programming, production, and robotics operation safety practices.  Prerequisite:  CS 151L and ELCT 163.

title: Advanced Industrial Robotics
description: This course covers industrial robotics programming and intelligent visual recognition software. Students will work with robots, peripheral equipment, and workspaces specific to industrial manufacturing robots.  Prerequisite:  201.

title: Programmable Logic Controllers
description: This course explores the many aspects of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) from basic concepts to system level applications, wiring, programming, and industrial applications.  Prerequisite:  ELCT 163.

title: Robotics Synthesis
description: This is a capstone course in robotics. Students will integrate and demonstrate all previously gained robotics, electronics, manufacturing, CADD, and programming skills to produce a comprehensive robotic project.

title: Topics
description: This is a topics course in robotics.

title: Introduction to World Religions
description: This course introduces major world religions and the scholarly methods of the academic study of religion. Religions covered may include Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, Daoism, Judaism, Christianity, Islam and/or New Religious Movements.

title: Introduction to the Bible
description: An introductory study of the structure and content of the Hebrew and Christian Scriptures. This class provides the context and reading skills for study and investigation of the Bible and its influence upon western culture and religion.

title: Hebrew Bible
description: An introduction to the history, beliefs, practices, and development of the Hebrew and later Jewish religion as reflected in the Hebrew Biblical Scriptures, using a historical and critical approach, with attention given to understanding its socio-cultural and political environment.

title: Religion, Health and Medicine
description:
Religion, Health, and Medicine introduces students to how people’s religious beliefs and practices influence their
perspectives on health and their approaches to medical care and treatment.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Eastern Religions
