description: Provides an academic overview of major religious traditions of Asia (mainly India, China, and Japan), which may include Hinduism, Buddhism, Sikhism, Jainism, Confucianism, Daoism, Shinto, and/or Shamanism as well as popular, tribal, or new religions. Students will be assigned both primary and secondary texts.

title: Western Religions
description: This is a survey course that will cover major religious traditions of the West, including the three Abrahamic religions (Judaism, Christianity, and Islam) and other religious systems. The course will focus on how each tradition has developed historically and how it exists in the world today.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Black Liberation and Religion
description: (Also offered as AFST 303) Students will be introduced to the Black experience, which necessitates the redefinition of God and Jesus Christ in the lives of Black people as the struggle for transcendental and political freedom.

title: Introduction to Islam
description: This course examines the life and role of the Prophet Muhammad, the message of the Qur'an, and the history of the theological, philosophical, legal, and mystical dimensions of Islam to the present.

title: Islamic Fundamentalism
description: An examination of the social, political, and cultural aspects of fundamentalism in the contemporary Muslim world.

title: Islamic Mysticism
description: An introduction to the origin and development of mystical aspects of Islam, commonly known as Sufism. The course examines themes such as Sufism vis-à-vis Islamic orthodoxy, mystical experience, the literary heritage of Sufism, Sufi organizations.

title: Radical Religions and American Transformations
description:
(Also offered as RELG 315)

This course examines how religion has informed radical visions for a more equal, free, inclusive and fair society, and motivated people to different types of radical action, intended to make those visions reality.

title: Sex and Gender in Ancient Religion
description:
(Also offered as CLST 319)
This course examines issues pertaining to gender and sexuality in ancient Mediterranean religions and cultures, with special attention paid to Greco-Roman religion, Judaism, and Christianity.

title: Magic in Ancient Religion
description:
(Also offered as CLST 320)
This course examines the perception and reality of magic in the ancient Mediterranean world by examining relevant texts, spells, and relics to situate it within the practice of ancient religion.

title: Apocalypse in the Ancient World
description:
(Also offered as CLST 321)
This course examines the development of the genre of apocalypse by reading several early Jewish apocalypses before examining the lasting influence of apocalypticism in one particular Jewish sect, Christianity.

title: History of Christianity to 1517
Introduction to Buddhism
description: A historical survey of Buddhist practices, beliefs, material culture, institutions, and figures from the time of the historical Buddha through to the contemporary period.

title: Goddesses and Gods of India
description: This course examines Hindu scriptures and practices to understand how deities and their relationships with men and women, high and low castes, and the natural and social worlds have changed over time.

title: Mysticism: East and West
description: This course introduces students to mystical texts from a variety of religious traditions, and exposes students to philosophical and psychological questions that arise in the study of these types of texts. The course begins with an examination of definitions of mysticism, then moves on to look at specific themes found in mystical texts. The second part of the class involves asking critical questions about the nature of mystical experience, with an eye towards careful academic reflection on these matters.

title: Topics in Religious Studies
description: Studies in major religious figures or movements. Topic varies.

title: Religion and Literature
description: An introduction exploring relationships between the literary and religious traditions. {Fall}

title: Catholicism in America
description: This course introduces students to the history of Roman Catholicism in the United States, emphasizing the interaction of Catholicism with politics and culture, and the religious lives of ordinary Catholics.

title: Atheism: Trends and Critiques
description:
This class surveys classical and contemporary currents in atheism. We will consider different definitions and types of Atheism, current demographic and psychological profiles of atheists, and key atheistic critiques of arguments for the existence of God.
Prerequisite: 1110 or 2120.

title: Religion in American History
description: (Also offered as HIST 441) This class will cover the rise and development of the nation's religious groups, from first contact to the present day. The focus will be on the social impact of the groups and how they influenced the development of American life.

title: Seminar in Religious Studies
description:
Major religious figures or movements. Topic varies.  
Prerequisite:  one Religious Studies course.

title: Theories of Religion
description:
An examination and critique of influential 19th-, 20th-, and 21st-century scientific attempts to explain religion in contrast to supernaturalist theories. Also considered are some postmodernist and evolutionary psychology perspectives on religion.
Prerequisite: one upper-division course in Religious Studies.

title: Asian Studies Thesis
description: Supervised research in one or more disciplines leading to an undergraduate thesis for the major in Asian Studies.

title: Dante in Translation
description: (Also offered as ITAL *475) Principally the Vita Nuova and the Divine Comedy.

title: Independent Studies
description:
Faculty-supervised individual study in an area of special interest not readily available through conventional course offerings.
Restriction: permission of program chairperson.

title: Advanced Seminar in Religious Studies
description: Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

title: M.A. Problems
description: Tutorial arrangement with a member of the graduate faculty.

title: Introduction to Signed Language
description: Signed languages and signing systems frequently used by deaf people are discussed. Cross-linguistic and cross-cultural information on ASL will be discussed. Focus is on ASL, signed English and fingerspelling, and the most common manually coded signed systems. In addition to the acquisition of basic vocabulary and grammar, this course emphasizes the knowledge of the characteristics, values and cultural and linguistic diversity of the Deaf Culture.

title: Fingerspelling
description: This course will assist the student in acquiring fluent fingerspelling through the use of intense receptive and expressive drills. Lexical borrowing and the semantic and morphological categories involved in restructuring English finger spelled citation forms will be studied. Recordings of a variety of fingerspelling styles will be presented to ensure that the students acquire a comprehensive background. Students will also be recorded to allow for self-analysis of their fingerspelling skills.

title: Lexical Semantics for Transliteration
description: Articles, videotapes, and supplemental materials will be used to demonstrate Various codes for English; these codes may be contrasted with the natural language of ASL. Periodically, students will be videotaped to aid in the analysis of their transliteration production.

title: American Sign Language I
description: This course in American Sign Language is designed to develop the students’ receptive, expressive and conversational skills. Students will also develop narrative skills in application of these receptive and expressive skills. ASL vocabulary and linguistic features are introduced, as well as fundamental concepts about the Deaf community and culture.

title: American Sign Language II
description: A continuation course that builds on concepts and skills developed in 2210. Students gain further exposure to ASL structure and grammar, and Deaf culture and the Deaf community. Emphasis is on increasing students’ ability to comprehend other signers and express themselves with more elaboration when conversing or presenting in ASL.

title: Structure of American Sign Language
description:
Examines linguistic research on signed languages, primarily ASL: phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax and semantics. Also covers signed language sociolinguistics, psycholinguistics, language acquisition (first and second) and neurolinguistics. 

Restriction: permission of instructor.

Graduate students should enroll in the SIGN 505 section.

title: American Sign Language III
description:
Designed to help students improve their expressive skills and general conversational competence in ASL relative to phonology, lexical items, syntax and discourse. Focuses on semantic appropriateness and accuracy of particular lexical items, appropriate use of non-manual behaviors and the use of context to determine meaning.  

This a starred (*) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program. A graduate student enrolled in a starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: American Sign Language IV
description: Intensive practice involving receptive/expressive skills in complex grammatical structures, dialogue and storytelling. Intensive study of transcription techniques and their applications to ASL research and documentation.

title: Language and Culture in the Deaf Community, Part 1
description:
An introduction to Deaf culture. Examines the language, education, social and political aspects and art forms of Deaf people from an anthropological point of view. 

Graduate students should enroll in the SIGN552 section.

title: The Interpreting Profession
description:
Addresses the mental processes essential to interpretation and transliteration. In addition to exercises used to develop interpreting strategies such as memory retention, message analysis, decalage, etc., the student is introduced to the interpreter's Code of Professional Conduct and business practices of the professional interpreter. 
Prerequisite: 2130 and 2150 and *310 and *352 and LING 2110. 
Restriction: permission of program coordinator.

title: Consecutive Interpretation
description:
Theory and practice of consecutive interpretation. Topics: message analysis, attention, cultural mediation, reducing interference from the source language. Equal time is spent with ASL and English texts. 
Prerequisite: *360. 
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Signed Language Interpreting and permission of program coordinator.

title: Simultaneous Interpreting
description:
Theory and practice of simultaneous interpretation. Topics: control of source-language input, team interpreting, self-monitoring and repair, preparation, providing feedback and special situations such as interpreting in medical settings.  
Prerequisite: 411. Restriction: admitted to B.S. Signed Language Interpreting degree program, permission of program coordinator.

title: Signed Language Interpreting Research
description:
A detailed study of current trends and practices in signed language interpreting and evaluation, along with similarities  and differences between signed language and spoken language interpreting. Introduction to interpreting process models and  assessment models and discussion of current research in the field of interpreting. Students will conduct a small-scale research project and participate in a debate of issues surrounding the interpreting profession.  
Restriction: admitted to B.S. Signed Language Interpreting degree program, permission of program coordinator.

title: Practicum in Signed Language Interpreting
description:
Supervised practicum interpreting and transliterating in a variety of community and academic settings, including elementary through post-secondary classrooms, medical situations, vocational rehabilitation, platform and television interpreting and so forth. Supervised preparation for future private practice employment. 
Prerequisite: *360. Restriction: admitted to B.S. Signed Language Interpreting degree program, permission of program coordinator.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Structure of American Sign Language
description:
Examines linguistic research on signed languages, primarily ASL: phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax and semantics. Also covers signed language sociolinguistics, psycholinguistics, language acquisition (first and second) and neurolinguistics. 

Restriction: permission of instructor.

Graduate students should enroll in the SIGN 505 section.

title: Language and Culture in the Deaf Community, Part 1
description:
An introduction to Deaf culture. Examines the language, education, social and political aspects and art forms of Deaf people from an anthropological point of view. 

Graduate students should enroll in the SIGN552 section.

title: Russian I
description: This is an entry level Russian course for students with no previous exposure to the language. The purpose of this beginning course is to develop listening, speaking, reading and writing skills to communicate at a basic level. After the completion of the course, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Novice-Mid level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages proficiency scale.

title: Russian II
description: This is an entry-level continuation course for students that have completed 1110. The course will use a communicative approach to cover the fundamentals of basic vocabulary, grammar, conversation and culture. After the completion of the course, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Novice-High level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages proficiency scale.

title: Russian III
description: This course is for students who have completed 1110 and 1120. It is designed to further the understanding of the Russian language and culture and continues the development of the four language skills (listening, reading, writing, and speaking). After the completion of the course, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Intermediate-Low level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages proficiency scale.

title: Russian IV
description: In this course, students continue to expand and refine their proficiency skills in listening, reading, writing, and speaking Russian. It is designed for students who have completed Intermediate Russian I. After the completion of the course, students will be able to perform in specific situations at the Intermediate-Mid level on the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages proficiency scale.

title: Workshop on Russian Language and Culture
description: Intensive practical training in Russian language and culture.

title: Advanced Russian I
description: Vocabulary building, basic grammar review and special attention to idiomatic Russian.

title: Advanced Russian II
description: Emphasis on all four language skills, especially reading. The structure of Russian is reviewed in detail.

title: Topics in Russian Literature and Culture in Translation
description: (Also offered as COMP, ENGL 338)  Study of individual authors, genres, and/or periods of Russian literature and culture in translation.

title: Russian Culture and History through Film
description:
(Also offered as FDMA 339; HIST 335) 
In this course we study films and read secondary sources from the Soviet and post-Soviet eras (with English subtitles) and examine how they comment on current Russian social and cultural issues. Taught in English.

title: Topics in Continuity and Change in Russian Culture
description: (Also offered as COMP 340) Topics will deal with individual authors, genres, periods or themes.

title: Russia Today I
description: Study of present-day life in Russian as represented in popular media, including TV and film.  Aimed at students who wish to improve their Russian language skills.

title: Seminar in Russian Literature and Culture
description: Topic will deal with individual authors, genres or periods. Taught in English and/or Russian. Repetition unlimited if content/topic changes.

title: Undergraduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Reading and Research for Honors
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive paper or research proposal that integrates knowledge attained through coursework, research, and experience.
Open to juniors and seniors as approved by Russian faculty. Students will study one aspect of the field with a member of the Faculty Committee.

title: Honors Essay
description:
A faculty-taught course emphasizing writing, critique and revision within the context of a particular genre or academic discipline.
Open only to seniors enrolled for departmental honors.

title: Education of the Exceptional Person
description:
This course is an introduction to special education with information regarding characteristics of individuals.
A survey of the characteristics and educational needs of exceptional children. Includes definition, etiology, characteristics
and various educational alternatives for each of the exceptionalities.

title: Exploring Disability, Diversity, and Practice Across Learning Environments
description: This course is an introduction to special education with information regarding characteristics of individuals with exceptionalities, special education terminology, evidence-based instructional strategies, diversity of students with exceptional needs, relationships between personal and cultural perspectives, and legal policies pertaining to exceptional students’ rights.

title: Introduction to Human Services and Social Work
description: This course is for students who are interested in social welfare issues and/or are considering entering a social service profession. The course presents an overview of social problems, issues and trends, and the network of social agencies developed to address these concerns. The course examines the influence of personal and professional values and ethics on the helping relationship. The concept of social welfare will be discussed from a social work perspective (with an emphasis on social justice), and students will gain a basic understanding of social work in U.S. society, social work career opportunities, and contemporary issues facing social workers. Approaches relevant to work with individuals, families, groups and communities are presented, with special emphasis on Hispanic and Indigenous populations of New Mexico and the Southwest.

title: Human Behavior and the Social Environment
description: This course introduces a combination of human development across the lifespan and theories commonly used in social work to understand this development. Students will learn generalist practice concepts in understanding human behavior and how social workers apply this to practice. Through readings, case studies, and discussion, students should leave this course with a better understanding of underlying social work theories and how to use this in their practicums and beyond.

title: Social Work Practice Skills with Individuals, Families, and Groups
description: This generalist practice course on practicing social work is focused on teaching practical skills to students for their practicum and beyond. Students will learn engagement skills with an emphasis on Motivational Interviewing, as well as get an introduction to conducting biopsychosocial assessments and writing treatment plans. Students will also get exposure to a wide variety of therapy and case management interventions that can be used with individuals, families, and groups. Through readings, demonstration videos, and classroom skills practice, students should leave this course feeling more prepared for providing direct services to clients.

title: Anti-Oppressive Social Work Practice in New Mexico
description: This signature course of the Master’s program introduces students to anti-oppressive practice and then applies this to practice in New Mexico through exploring historical and present-day oppression and injustices against minoritized communities, as well as strengths and resilience in these communities. This course relies heavily on students reflecting on their own identities, experiences with oppression, and privileges, as well as having curiosity about communities they are not a part of. Through readings, videos of individuals sharing lived experience, and robust classroom discussion, students should leave this course with a better understanding of how to approach social work practice in a humble and anti-oppressive way.

title: Social Work History, Ethics, and Professionalism
description: This course is focused on orienting students to professional expectations and obligations of social workers. Students will learn about the National Association of Social Work’s Code of Ethics and examining ethical dilemmas, including historical ethical issues in social work. Students will also get an introduction to professional writing and documentation. Through readings, reflecting writing assignments, and video presentations by the class, students should leave this course with a better understanding of ethical and professional social work practice.

title: Generalist Social Work Practicum and Seminar I
description: This course introduces generalist social work practice through supervised fieldwork in an approved community agency, along with a weekly seminar to support connecting field experiences with classroom learning. Students will apply ethical practice, diversity, and social work competencies by applying classroom knowledge in real-world settings.

title: Research Methods in Social Work
description: This course introduces students to ways that social workers commonly conduct qualitative and quantitative research, including identifying problems to be researched, forming a hypothesis, measurement, sampling, and design. Students will also examine the role of practitioner researchers, and the strengths and challenges of incorporating research into everyday practice. Through readings and classroom activities, students should leave this course with greater understanding of how and why research is conducted in social work.

title: Direct Practice Skills Lab
description: This practice course focuses on giving students a highly interactive experience practicing engagement, assessment, and intervention skills through practicing skills with peers and with simulated clients. Students will also learn to constructively evaluate themselves and others through group debriefs; students are expected to be active observers when they are not part of a demonstration. Students should leave this course feeling more confident about their ability to consistently use social work practice skills in the field.

title: Social Work Practice with Communities and Organizations
description: This practice course is focused on understanding engagement, assessment, intervention, and evaluation at the organizational and community levels. This includes assessing strengths and challenges of organizations, learning conflict management skills, understanding basics of community organizing and program evaluation, and applying generalist practice social work theories to larger systems. Through readings, discussion, and skills practice, students should leave this course with a better understanding of practitioners’ roles in engaging with macro practice.

title: Social Work Policy and Advocacy
description: This course is focused on introducing students to policy that impacts everyday social work practice, such as minimum wage, health insurance, housing access, and public assistance programs. Students will learn the history of these policies; examine their impact on individuals, groups, families, organizations, and communities; and discuss methods of changing these policies. Through readings and a community-based project, students should leave this course with a better understanding of the relationship of policy and practice and the role of social workers as advocates.

title: Generalist Social Work Practicum and Seminar II
description: This is the second field education course that builds upon the foundation established in Generalist Social Work Practicum and Seminar I. Students continue their placement in an approved community agency, taking on more advanced responsibilities in service delivery and professional practice, as well as continue with weekly seminars to support learning.

title: Trauma and Trauma-Informed Care in Social Work
description: This course for all MSW students provides an in-depth look at these common and complex experiences, including historical trauma, and best practices for taking trauma into account in social work services. Students draw on understanding from previous courses regarding oppression and violence to advance their knowledge on ways larger systemic issues can impact behavioral and physical health. This course includes social work’s specific contributions to the trauma-informed literature, as well as how to apply social work’s person-in-environment perspective to understand trauma. Through readings, videos, and reflective writings, students should leave this course feeling more knowledgeable about the complexity of trauma and ways to avoid re-traumatization.

title: Crisis Intervention Skills in Social Work
description: This practice course for all MSW students advances students’ skills with brief interventions in a variety of situations, including assessing safety concerns like suicidality and substance overdose, understanding interpersonal violence, and learning the basics of disaster mental health. While the skills learned in this course can apply to any setting, the class has special attention on social workers providing services outside of office settings. Additionally, students will receive specific instruction on their role as social workers in responding to crises. Through readings, demonstration videos, and classroom skills practice, students should leave this course feeling more confident about responding to individuals, families, and groups in crisis, as well as engaging briefly in a variety of situations.

title: The DSM and Psychopharmacology for Social Workers
description: This course for all MSW students covers common diagnoses in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM), including history and ethical concerns around methods and purpose of diagnosis and the role of social workers as advocates. Students also learn medication basics as non-prescribers and understanding the role of social workers around medications in interdisciplinary teams. Through readings, videos, and case studies, students should leave this course feeling more confident with diagnosis and understanding an individual’s medications.

title: Clinical Social Work Therapy Skills and Techniques for Youth and Adults
description: This first practice course in the Clinical Social Work track provides students with a comprehensive overview on common therapeutic modalities used throughout the lifespan focused in social work values, ethics, and a person-in-environment perspective. Students build on previous practice skills and theories to advance their knowledge on the underlying reasons for different modalities, assessing what modalities to use, and using these skills in practice. Through readings, demonstration videos, and classroom skills practice, students should have more confidence in using therapeutic modalities in everyday clinical practice that align with the core principles of social work and effectively respond to diverse client systems.

title: Medical Social Work Practice Across the Lifespan
description: This first practice course in the Medical Social Work track will provide students with a comprehensive overview on health and wellness across the lifespan that can contribute to need for accessing medical social work services, including life-altering illnesses like cancer and kidney failure, chronic illnesses like diabetes, and both congenital and acquired disability. Students will build on previous practice skills and theories to advance their knowledge on specific interventions used in medical social work practice and assessing when to use these. Through readings, demonstration videos, and classroom skills practice, students should leave this course feeling more confident about using medical social work interventions in everyday clinical practice.

title: Clinical Social Work Practicum and Seminar I
description: Students are placed in clinical placements—such as behavioral health centers, outpatient programs, or private practice settings—where they develop foundational skills in clinical assessment, diagnosis, treatment planning, and evidence-based intervention. Supervised practice emphasizes ethical practice, therapeutic alliance, use of diagnostic criteria, and cultural competence. The weekly one-hour seminar provides a forum for students in reflecting on the practicum experience, exploring ethical dilemmas, and integrating theory and practice in clinical social work settings.

title: Medical Social Work Practicum and Seminar I
description: Students are placed in approved medical settings—such as hospitals, or clinics —where they engage in supervised social work practice focused on patient care coordination, discharge planning, psychosocial assessments, navigating resources and interdisciplinary collaboration. Emphasis is placed on applying classroom knowledge to address the biopsychosocial needs of patients and their families, navigate healthcare systems, and advocate for equitable health outcomes. Students have a weekly seminar to support learning in these medical settings.

title: Advanced Policy for Social Workers
description: This course for all MSW students will advance student critical thinking on complex and challenging policy topics, such as insurance, incarceration, and immigration. This will include examining the history, challenges, and attempts at addressing these challenges, including learning to read and discuss legislative proposals. Through readings and robust classroom discussion, students should leave this course with greater understanding of how to engage with policy in everyday practice.

title: Applied Research in Social Work
description: This course for all MSW students advances student understanding of their role as practitioner researchers, including identifying practice issues to research, critically analyzing methods and significance of existing research, and reflecting on how research can inform practice. Students come out of this course with a research paper on a practice topic of interest that they present to their peers to collectively advance the cohort’s knowledge.

title: Differential Diagnosis and Complex Cases in Clinical Social Work
description: This advanced clinical social work course prepares students to navigate complex case studies through a biopsychosocial and person-in-environment lens. Building on prior knowledge of assessment and diagnosis, the course emphasizes clinical decision-making in contexts involving multiple co-occurring mental health conditions, structural inequities, and social determinants of health. Students engage with case-based learning using a social work lens to identify and respond to psychosocial needs, while integrating social work ethics, anti-oppressive practice, and strengths-based interventions. In this course, students will strengthen their ability to engage, assess, and intervene in complex clinical settings with cultural humility and a commitment to social justice.

title: Social Work Interventions in Death and Dying
description: This advanced practice course in the Medical Social Work track builds on previous learning about medical social work interventions to explore the specific and challenging topic of working with individuals, families, and groups through death and dying at any stage of life. This includes life reviews, in-depth exploration of grief and loss, and understanding relevant policy and perspectives regarding advance directives and medical aid in dying. The goal of this course is to prepare students for advanced practice in medical social work by developing the knowledge, skills, and self-awareness necessary to work effectively with death, dying, grief, and bereavement. Through readings, video demonstrations, and classroom skills practice, students should leave this course feeling more capable at addressing end-of-life in medical social work practice.

title: Special Topics in Clinical Social Work
description: This seminar for students on the Clinical Social Work track is designed to deepen students’ understanding of advanced clinical social work through exploration of specialized topics, integration of research into practice, and preparation for the transition to professional clinical roles. Students will strengthen their clinical reasoning by engaging critically with peer-reviewed literature and applying research findings to practice settings. The course fosters professional identity development, encourages interdisciplinary dialogue, and supports career readiness by cultivating advanced communication, self-assessment, and presentation skills. Upon completion, students will be better equipped to contribute to the field as ethical, reflective, and research-informed clinical social work professionals.

title: Special Topics in Medical Social Work
description: This seminar for students on the Medical Social Work track is designed to deepen students’ understanding of advanced medical social work through exploration of specialized topics, integration of research into practice, and preparation for the transition to professional medical social work roles. Students will strengthen their clinical reasoning by engaging critically with peer-reviewed literature and applying research findings to medical practice settings. The course fosters professional identity development, encourages interdisciplinary dialogue, and supports career readiness by cultivating advanced communication, self-assessment, and presentation skills. Upon completion, students will be better equipped to contribute to the field as ethical, reflective, and research-informed medical social work professionals.

title: Medical Social Work Practicum and Seminar II
description: This course is the second in the medical social work field education sequence and provides students with advanced opportunities to apply and refine clinical and systems-level skills in healthcare settings. Building on Medical Social Work Practicum and Seminar I, students take on greater responsibility in areas such as psychosocial assessment, discharge planning, care coordination, patient and family support, interdisciplinary teamwork, and advocacy within complex healthcare systems. Students continue having a weekly seminar to support their learning.

title: Introduction to Sociology
description: This course will introduce students to the basic concepts and theories of sociology, as well as to the methods utilized in sociological research. The course will address how sociological concepts and theories can be utilized to analyze and interpret our social world, and how profoundly our society and the groups to which students belong influence them. Students will be given the opportunity to challenge their “taken for granted” or “common sense” understandings about society, social institutions, and social issues. Special attention will also be paid to the intimate connections between their personal lives and the larger structural features of social life. In addition, the implications of social inequalities, such as race/ethnicity, gender, and social class will be central to the course’s examination of social life in the United States.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Introduction to Criminal Justice Systems
description:
This course provides an introduction to social issues that are currently affecting the criminal justice system in the United States. The course will cover the history of the U.S. criminal justice system and how our system compares with other countries. We will address how the U.S. criminal justice system attempts to create and preserve a balance between sustaining order, maintaining individual rights, and promoting justice. Important themes also include, but are not limited to discussions of how crime and delinquency are measured, key correlates of crime, sociological approaches to researching crime, sociological theories of crime, the quality of crime data in the U.S. and how it is used to make public policy decisions, and the causes and consequences of mass incarceration in the United States.
Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Sociology of Deviance
description:
This course is designed to provide an overview of the study of deviance and social control from multiple sociological perspectives. The instructor will present how sociologists research deviance and social control and the ethical issues involved in studying human subjects involved in these activities. The course also examines central sociological theories for understanding the causes of deviant behavior.
Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Contemporary Social Problems
description: This course studies the nature, scope, and effects of social problems and their solutions. The course will concentrate on sociological perspectives, theories, and key concepts when investigating problems, such as inequality, poverty, racism, alienation, family life, sexuality, gender, urbanization, work, aging, crime, war and terrorism, environmental degradation, and mass media. This course is designed to build students’ sociological understanding of how sociological approaches attempt to clarify various issues confronting contemporary life, as well as how sociologists view solutions to these problems.

title: The Dynamics of Prejudice
description: This course is designed to help students understand how attitudes and beliefs of individuals shape intergroup relations and their impacts on the daily lives of individuals as well as the effects that these beliefs have on the larger social structure of American society. We will examine how profoundly our society and the groups to which we belong, influence us and our beliefs and ultimately how these beliefs shape prejudice in our society. In this course, students are encouraged to challenge ideologies that are considered "common sense" or that are taken for granted and this in turn will allow them to critically engage issues in society such as racism, classism, sexism, and will leave with an understanding on how privilege affects our views on disability, LGBTQ issues, religion and immigration. Rather than investigating these themes in the abstract, students will identify and unpack how these larger structural issues play integral roles in their everyday lives, interactions, and existence. Ultimately, this course aims to address the social inequalities that exists in our society as a result of prejudice and will challenge students to identify and engage in strategies to work towards changing these aspects of society.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Environmental Sociology
description:
Examination of humans and the environment from an ecological perspective. Focus on industrial and economic growth, natural resources development, environmental values and movements, resource management, and comparative perspective on people's relationship to the environment. 

Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Nonviolent Alternatives
description: This course studies the dynamics of violence and nonviolence at the interpersonal, institutional, cultural, and global level from a variety of disciplinary perspectives.

title: Sociology of Gender
description:
How and why societies create gender categories. How do definitions of “masculinity” and “femininity” vary? What are the costs and benefits of being male or female in contemporary American society?

Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Sociology of Aging and the Aged
description:
Descriptive and theoretical study of the social situation of older persons in contemporary industrial societies; the impact on societal institutions of an increasing percentage of older citizens.  

Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Causes of Crime and Delinquency
description:
A survey of criminological theories exploring why some people are more likely to engage in crime than others and why crime rates vary over time and space and across social groups. Attendant policy issues will also be discussed. 

Prerequisite: 2120 or 2210.

title: Couples, Family and Friendship
description: An overview of sociological research on the strongest social relationships and primary social groups: those of love, family and friendships.

title: Social Movements
description:
The study of riots, disturbances, social movements and other forms of contentious collective behavior. Strategies of conflict and conflict resolution are considered.  
Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Sociology of Medical Practice
description: An introduction to the delivery of health care in the U.S. and selected other countries is pursued with an emphasis on the interaction of patients, professionals and health care institutions.

title: Health and Social Inequalities I
description: This course explores how social inequities in education, housing, employment, health care, safety, resources, money, and power contribute to health disparities among refugee and immigrant populations and multilevel approaches to reducing these inequities and disparities.

title: Health and Social Inequalities II
description:
This community-engaged course provides students the opportunity to apply knowledge about social inequalities and health to working with refugee and immigrant community members to create change that improves mental health and well-being and reduces inequities.

Prerequisite: 346.

title: Sociological Theory
description:
The study of how theory is constructed and applied by sociologists including traditions from 19th century sociological founders and contemporary theoretical formulations. 

Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Introduction to Research Methods
description:
A survey of the major methods of social research: foundations of social research, research design, sampling and measurement, quantitative and qualitative research methods and data analysis.

Prerequisite: 1110.

title: Sociological Data Analysis
description:
Introduction to quantitative sociological data analysis using basic statistics (both descriptive and inferential) and to the use of statistical software to analyze empirical data.

Prerequisite: 380 and MATH 1350.

title: Special Topics in Sociology
description:
This course will explore contemporary issues in sociology. Topics will vary. For detailed, course specific descriptions, see the department website.
Course may be repeated without limit provided the topics differ.

title: Advanced Undergraduate Workshop in Sociology
description:
First of a six-credit hour Sociology Honors Thesis Program; students develop skills to write an honors thesis proposal; culminates in the completion of a senior honors thesis written in SOCI 499.

Prerequisite: 380.

Restriction: permission of Department Honors Coordinator.

title: Social Justice in Criminology: Retributive Punishment and Social Justice Alternatives
description: This class explores the underlying logics of punishment and retributive justice that are foundational to the U.S. criminal legal system. It examines how these logics perpetuate racial, class, and gender injustice. This course explores various social justice-oriented alternative responses to crime/harm, including restorative justice and transformative justice.

title: Social Change and Popular Music
description: Examines the social and cultural construction of popular music and how it reflects, influences, and resists social change by situating music within key transitional moments of American history.

title: Sociology of Police and Social Control
description:
Study of the relationship between society and law enforcement agencies, including the societal context of policing and how law enforcement impacts society. Discussion of law enforcement practices, training and management; the interface of police and communities; historical and contemporary models of policing; and efforts at police reform. 

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Sociology of Corrections
description:
Study of the perspectives of corrections, its relationship to other criminal justice agencies, various forms sentencing and punishment, corrections administration and issues in the field. Visits may be made to several facilities. 

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Inequality and Power
description: This course focuses on the contemporary dynamics of social inequalities and power in US society. It lays particular emphasis on racial, gender, and economic oppression while outlining how these intersect with other systems and institutions.

title: Sociology of Law
description:
Social science perspectives of the law, legal institutions and the impact of law on behavior. Topics include theories of law and legality; comparative legal systems; lawyers, judges and juries; and the use of social science in the courts.

Prerequisite: 312 or 371.

title: Selected Topics in Criminology
description:
This course will explore contemporary issues related to criminology and the criminal legal system. Topics will vary. For detailed, course specific descriptions, see the department website. 
Course may be repeated without limit provided the topics differ.

title: Peers, Groups, and Gangs
description:
This course introduces social network analysis as a tool for studying topics related to the social aspect of criminal and delinquent behavior: delinquent peers, co-offending, and organized criminal groups.

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Race and Inequality
description: An exploration of empirical studies and theories on the social construction of racial and ethnic inequality at the individual, institutional and structural levels in the U.S. and/or global context.

title: Women, Gender, and Crime
description:
This course will outline similarities and differences in offending patterns across males and females and discuss various explanations for these differences. Discussions will also focus on the dynamics of female offending, the formal social control of female offenders and the role of women in the correctional system. 

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Race, Class and Crime
description:
This class will examine the relationships between race, ethnicity, socio-economic status and involvement in criminal behavior, focusing on the influence of structural, cultural and historical influences. We will also explore contemporary criminal justice issues pertaining to race and class. 

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Crime Through the Life Course
description:
Causes and consequences of offending at various stages in the life course, focusing on the ways in which adolescent and adult roles, responsibilities and opportunities shape aggregate and individual level patterns of involvement in juvenile delinquency and adult criminality. 

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Drugs, Crime and Social Control
description:
Study of the development of social policies concerning illicit substance use; its impact on social behavior; strategies for prevention and intervention with substance use; investigation, adjudication and supervision of drug offenders; and the relationship between criminal justice, education, public health and government policies. 

Prerequisite: 312.

title: Sociology of Madness
description: Different historical and cultural frameworks for defining, making sense of, and responding to madness are examined. The social causes and social distribution of madness are critically examined.

title: Intersectionality: Race, Gender, Class for Social Policy
description: Focuses on how race, gender, class, ethnicity and inequalities interact in society. Students will apply intersectional epistemologies for research and policy-relevant solutions in education, health, housing, environmental justice, criminal justice, employment, and media.

title: Games and Society
description: A hands-on seminar about using games and simulations to conduct social science, and using Sociology to study games and gaming cultures. Students work collaboratively in groups to play games, design games and simulations, and evaluate them.

title: Community Organizing and the Struggle for Justice in America
description:
How can people use democratic power to create a good, or at least better, society? We answer this question by studying the political practices of community organizing and the ideas of democratic theory.
Prerequisite: 1110 or PCST 1110 or RELG 1110.

title: Sociology & Criminology Internship: Field Observation and Experience
description: This course is both a field observation course and an opportunity to gain experience in the field, in the form of an internship or working in the community. It is designed to help students make the connection between the academic setting of the university and the activist, corporate, government, and service settings in the larger community.

title: Directed Study
description: Tutorial arrangement for investigation of selected issues in sociology. Specific arrangements must be made with a member of the Sociology department faculty responsible for supervising the work. Arrangements are normally made at least one semester in advance.

title: Directed Study in Criminology
description: Tutorial arrangement for investigation of selected issues in criminology. Specific arrangements must be made with a member of the Sociology department faculty responsible for supervising the work.

title: Senior Honors Thesis
description: For departmental honors students only. By arrangement with department's Honors and Awards Committee and approval of the Chairperson.

title: Classical Sociological Theory
description: Advanced study of selected classical theorists.

title: Social and Political Movements
description: Examination of historical, theoretical and empirical materials on the character and dynamics of social and political movements.  Includes consideration of the global context of contemporary social and political movements.

title: Constructing and Analyzing Contemporary Sociological Theory
description: Survey of contemporary theory, with a focus on constructing theory. Includes analysis of functional, interactionist, institutional and world-systems theory.

title: Special Topics in Criminology
description: Content varies. Explores select substantive and theoretical topics in criminology. Topics may include communities and crime, gender and crime, contemporary research in criminology.

title: Racial and Ethnic Relations
description: Historical and comparative analysis of race and ethnic relations in the U.S., with comparative reference to Western Europe, Latin America, Asia. Origins and maintenance of slavery; minority community development; causes and consequences of prejudice.

title: Topics in the Sociology of Race and Ethnicity
description: Study of theory and research in one area of the sociology of race and ethnicity chosen by the instructor. Content varies from semester to semester, may be repeated with different content.

title: Proseminar
description:
Introduces incoming graduate students to each of the department's regular faculty members and their work.

Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Special Topics in The Sociology of Health and Medicine
description: Special topics in the sociology of health and medicine. Credit can be earned more than once, as the topic and content will vary by instructor.

title: Special Topics in the Sociology of Gender
description: Special topics in the sociology of gender. Credit can be earned more than once, as the topic and content will vary by instructor.

title: Social Science Writing and Publishing
description: This course emphasizes writing and publishing skills, the publishing process for the social sciences and how to produce constructive peer reviews. Students will prepare one of their existing manuscripts for submission and publication.

title: Sociology Teaching Seminar
description: This course emphasizes teaching skills. Topics include syllabus development, lecture preparation and delivery, interactive learning methods, and grading approaches. We explore course policies and classroom practices that prevent common problems while enhancing the learning environment.

title: Problems
description: Tutorial arrangement with a member of the graduate faculty.

title: Problems
description: Tutorial arrangement with a member of the graduate faculty.

title: Methods of Social Research
description: Analytical examination of traditional methodological issues including measurement, experimental design, sampling, theory construction, role of statistics and nature of probability.

title: Advanced Social Statistics I
description: Covers the statistical methods most commonly used in social science research, from basic descriptives to multiple regression. No prior statistical training is necessary, and the emphasis is on practical use in conducting social research.

title: Advanced Social Statistics II
description:
Continuation of prerequisite. This course covers additional methods for quantitative social research, including models for discrete outcomes (Logistic, Poisson, etc), missing data analysis, and multilevel models. 

Prerequisite: 581.

title: Qualitative Research Methods
description:
Intensive practicum on research fieldwork, including research design, human subjects review, the ethics/politics of fieldwork, and fieldwork implementation. Focuses on ethnographic and interview methods; some attention to focus groups and archives. 

Prerequisite: 580.

title: Special Topics in Sociology
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Professional Paper
description:
Student works under faculty supervision toward completion of the professional paper requirement for a Plan II master's degree. Paper must be of professional quality and in a format suitable for publication. 

Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Reading in Sociology
description:
Student works under faculty supervision in preparation for the Ph.D. comprehensive exam.

Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Dissertation
description: Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a doctoral dissertation. Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Spanish I
description: Designed for students with little exposure to Spanish, this course develops basic listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills and basic intercultural competence in interpretive, interpersonal and presentational modes of communication at the Novice Level of proficiency based on ACTFL guidelines. During this course, students perform better and stronger in the Novice-Mid level while some abilities emerge in the Novice High range. This is an introductory course aimed at helping the student to communicate in Spanish in everyday familiar situations via recognition and production of practiced or memorized words, phrases, and simple sentences.

title: Spanish II
description: Designed for students with some degree of exposure to Spanish in high school and/or at home, this course continues to develop basic listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills and basic intercultural competence in interpretive, interpersonal and presentational modes of communication at the Novice High Level of proficiency based on ACTFL guidelines, although a few abilities may emerge in the Intermediate Low Level. Students in this course communicate in Spanish in familiar topics using a variety of words, phrases, simple sentences and questions that have been highly practiced and memorized.

title: Spanish for Heritage Learners I
description: This is a beginning-level Spanish course designed for students who have a cultural connection to the Spanish language. Some students have had very little exposure to the language and enter the class to develop beginning-level skills. Other students may have grown up hearing the heritage language in the community and may understand some Spanish and speak at a basic level as a result. The objective is to draw upon the connection to the heritage language as a source of motivation and engagement for our learning communities. At the same time, we build upon the language base that students may already have as a result of their heritage learner experience in order to develop new proficiencies in Spanish and reactivate the Spanish that students have learned previously. By the end of this course, students will be able to describe their home, campus surroundings and common activities including cultural traditions. At the same time, students gain cultural competency and develop a critical understanding of their linguistic and cultural background.

title: Spanish for Heritage Learners II
description: A second semester class designed for students who have developed some basic Spanish proficiency from previous classes and/or from community experiences. This course provides students with the opportunity to develop their proficiency in the four language skills (speaking, listening, reading, and writing). Class activities are designed to strengthen oral communication skills (speaking and listening) through a variety of group activities. By the end of the course students will be able to understand and produce narrations of past events in oral and written Spanish. In order to foster a desire to revitalize and maintain the Spanish language in the US context we attempt to raise students’ critical awareness of what it means to be part of a specific speech community.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Spanish III
description: This course is based on the integration of learning outcomes across interpretive, interpersonal and presentational modes of communication at the Novice High Level of proficiency based on ACTFL guidelines. Students accomplish real-world communicative tasks in culturally appropriate ways as they gain familiarity with the target culture(s). This is an intermediate course aimed at helping the student to communicate in Spanish on familiar topics about self, others and everyday life at the same time that they recognize and handle short social interactions in interactions in everyday situations by asking and answering a variety of questions.

title: Spanish IV
description: This course is based on the integration of learning outcomes across interpretive, interpersonal and presentational modes of communication at the Intermediate High Level of proficiency based on ACTFL guidelines. Students accomplish real-world communicative tasks in culturally appropriate ways as they gain familiarity with the target culture(s). This is an intermediate course aimed at helping the student to communicate in Spanish on familiar topics about self, others and everyday life at the same time that they recognize and handle short social interactions in interactions in everyday situations by asking and answering a variety of questions.

title: Introduction to Public Speaking in Spanish
description: This course aims to help undergraduate students feel more comfortable become more effective “public speakers” and more critical “public receivers” of information in Spanish. The primary focus of the course is the practice of techniques to lessen speaker anxiety and to enhance speaker presentations in public settings with multiple audiences in mind. This course has been designed for undergraduates who speak Spanish as a daily basis with their own families and/or other personal networks in addition/outside educational settings.

title: Spanish for Heritage Learners III
description: A third semester course designed for students who have been raised in a Spanish-speaking environment and speak, or understand, some Spanish as a result of hearing it in the home, and in the community by family, friends, and neighbors. Students in this course will continue to develop their ability to narrate events in the past and will be able to describe hypothetical situations. Students will also develop their ability to express wishes, desires, and necessities. This course will help the student build confidence in their Spanish abilities and expand the language use in the areas of writing, reading, oral production and listening comprehension. In order to foster a desire to revitalize and maintain the Spanish language we attempt to raise students’ critical awareness of wider issues facing Spanish speakers in the US context.

title: Spanish for Heritage Learners IV
description: A fourth-semester course designed for students who have been raised in a Spanish-speaking environment and speak, or understand, Spanish as a result of having heard it in the home and in the community. It is also for students with a cultural connection to heritage language speech communities or who have achieved proficiency from study in previous courses. This course will help the student build confidence in their Spanish abilities and expand the language use in the areas of writing, reading, oral production and listening comprehension. In addition to scaffolding skills that students already have, in this class they will expand their ability to describe abstract and hypothetical situations. Students will write essays, reaction papers, and creative pieces. Students will also examine formal and informal contexts of language use in speaking and writing. By studying the cultural and historical background shared by students as part of the program, students will develop an increased critical awareness of Spanish language speech communities.

title: Introduction to Medical Spanish
description: This course is designed with a communicative-based methodology (task-based oriented) and engages students in the practice of the four language skills (listening, reading, writing, speaking) using medical-related vocabulary for intermediate-level Spanish proficiency.

title: Topics
description: A course exploring a topic not covered by the standard curriculum but of interest to faculty and students in a particular semester.

title: Topics in Hispanic Culture and Language
description: Taught in Spanish (required for major study).Emphasis on oral and written expression based on a theme or language related topics (literature, culture, civilization, translation, commercial, etc.)

title: Developing Spanish Writing Skills
description: Taught in Spanish (required for major study). Emphasis on developing Spanish written expression.

title: Brazilian Literature and Culture in Spanish
description: Introduces students to Brazilian literature, film, and cultural production through readings and materials presented in Spanish. The course emphasizes Brazil’s dialogue with the wider Latin American world and examines how writers, filmmakers, and artists have represented the country’s diverse regional, linguistic, and social realities. Taught entirely in Spanish, this course is designed for students who wish to expand their understanding of Latin America beyond the Hispanic world while strengthening their skills in literary and cultural analysis.

title: Medical Spanish and Public Health
description: Students continue developing language skills through a comprehensive introduction to the field of medical Spanish terminology, heath communication and public health; conducting medical encounters in Spanish through a portfolio of clinical cases.

title: Health and Healing in Hispanic Literature
description:
Introduction to literary and textual analysis from perspectives of health, illness, and medicine. Students develop the practice of close reading of literary and cultural texts and improve attention, representation, and affiliation in human relationships.
Credit for both this course and SPAN 307 may not be applied toward a degree program.
Prerequisite: 301.
Pre- or corequisite: 302.

title: Introduction to Hispanic Literature
description:
Examination of selected Spanish and Spanish-American literary texts representing old and new literary currents. Special attention will be given to stylistics and the analysis of style and literary language. 
Credit for both this course and SPAN 306 may not be applied toward a degree program.
Prerequisite: 301. 
Pre- or corequisite: 302.

title: Topics in Health and Culture in the Hispanic World
description: Special topics related to cultural expressions of health and illness, health care communication and intercultural competence in Spanish-speaking and Hispanic contexts.

title: Introduction to the Sound Patterns of Spanish
description: Students will learn fundamental concepts related to the sound patterns of Spanish, such as Phonetics, Phonology, Laboratory Phonology, and Sociophonetics and will receive hands on training in a laboratory setting.

title: Introduction to Spanish Linguistics
description: An introduction to the phonology, morphology, syntax and dialectology of the Spanish language.

title: Spanish Grammar in Society
description:
Analysis of Spanish grammatical structures and the current and historical sociopolitical factors that shape how we use them.

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Survey of Chicano Literature
description:
Study of the major genres of Chicano literature (novel, short story, essay, poetry and drama), with emphasis on post-1960s literature.  
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Spanish of the Southwest
description: Focusing on Spanish speakers of the Southwest, this course surveys research in linguistic history, sociolinguistics, sociology of the language, and Spanish as a heritage language. This is a seminar style class in which students conduct authentic sociolinguistic research.

title: Southwestern Hispanic Folklore
description: Folkways of Spanish-speaking people of American Southwest: language, customs, beliefs, music, folk sayings.

title: Survey of Spanish Peninsular Literature I
description:
A survey of Spanish literature from the 11th to the 17th century. 

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Survey of Spanish Peninsular Literature II
description:
A survey of Spanish literature from the 18th, 19th and 20th centuries.

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Cervantes: The Quijote
description:
Detailed analysis of the Quijote and treatment of its place in world literature.
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Topics in Spanish Peninsular Culture and Literature
description:
Topics will deal with individual authors, genres or periods.

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: Spanish American Literature Survey I
description:
A historical survey of the literary canon in Spanish America from Colonial times through 19th-century Romanticism. 
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Spanish American Literature Survey II
description:
Continuation of **431. A survey of the literary canon in Spanish American from Modernismo through contemporary times.
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Modern Spanish American Poetry
description:
A survey course covering Spanish American poetry from Modernism to the present. 
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Mexican Literature
description:
Study of readings in Mexican literature emphasizing Mexico's contribution to Hispanic American literature from pre-Colombian to contemporary times. Examination of diverse genres in Mexico's literature. 
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Topics in Spanish American Culture and Literature
description:
Topics will deal with individual authors, genres or periods.
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: Topics in Hispanic Linguistics
description:
Deals with different areas, approaches and issues.
Prerequisite: **350 or 351.

title: La Cultura Avanzada de Nuevo México
description: We read and analyze literature, history, folklore, criticism and oral texts that will help us gain a critical perspective on the contemporary and past cultures of New Mexico.  A central concept is that culture is a verb and that cultural artifacts are social behaviors.

title: Topics in Southwest Folklore-Literature
description:
Study of oral and literary genres and periods, including Chicano theater, Hispanic New Mexican literature, Chicano writers, poetry, folk music, orality in folk and Chicano narrative.
Prerequisite: (302 or AP Spanish Language and Culture =>4) and (306 or 307 or AP Spanish Literature and Culture =5).

title: El Arte de la Escritura
description:
Topics explore a particular genre, period, writer, region, and/or literary movement. Topics vary.
Prerequisite: 307 or 306.

title: Topics in Cultural Geographies in the Spanish-Speaking World
description:
Topics explore relationships between space, place, and cultural expression. How does cultural expression fix, reinforce or deconstruct socio-symbolic spaces such as kingdom, nation, favela, or prison? What meanings are shaped by conquest, migration or globalization?
Prerequisite: 306 or 307.

title: Topics in Race, Ethnicity, Class, and Gender
description:
Topics examine the construction, representation, and contestation of identity, considering race, ethnicity, class, gender and/or their intersection. Focus may be on representation, performance or the emergence of new subjectivities.
Prerequisite: 306 or 307.

title: Topics in Social Justice, Rights, and Insurgency
description:
Topics study intersections between the humanities and political processes, including regime shift, insurgency, and social movements. Literature, film, and performance are examined as spaces of decolonization and contestation of state, nation and hegemonic groups.
Prerequisite: 306 or 307.

This is a double-starred (**) level course and may be taken for graduate credit by students enrolled in a graduate program outside of the department. A graduate student enrolled in a double-starred course numbered below 500 may be required to complete extra work.

title: La literatura de Nuevo México
description: Beginning in the 16th century, the course progresses chronologically, moving from the colonial period, through the 19th century and into the present day. Using readings, class collaboration, discussions and exams as our tools, we will construct a historical and political context that will support how we read these literary and cultural works.

title: Undergraduate Independent Study
description: Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Reading and Research for Honors
description:
A faculty-supervised course culminating in a comprehensive paper or research proposal that integrates knowledge attained through coursework, research, and experience.
Open to juniors and seniors approved by Honors Committee.  Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Honors Essay
description:
A faculty-taught course emphasizing writing, critique and revision within the context of a particular genre or academic discipline.
Open only to seniors enrolled for departmental honors.  Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Proseminar:  Research and Critical Methodology
description: Introduction to fundamentals of literary analysis: defining a research question; gaining access to resources; selecting approaches to texts; citing bibliographic data according to current MLA guidelines.

title: Seminar in Ibero-American Studies
description:
(Also offered as LTAM 504)
Course emphasizes investigation, evaluation, and discussion of areas of specialized knowledge or inquiry relevant to the profession or field of study.

title: Seminar in the Spanish Peninsular Picaresque Novel
description: The study of Lazarillo de Tormes, Guzmán de Alfarache, El buscón and other 17th-century picaresque novels.

title: Seminar in Spanish Peninsular Poetry
description: Courses ranging from post-Romanticism (Becquer, Castro), the “Generation of ‘98” (Machado, Unamuno), Jiménez’s “pure poetry,” the fusion of tradition and avant-garde aesthetics in the “Generation of ‘27,” to the post-war poets and more recent tendencies.

title: Seminar in Twentieth-Century Spanish Peninsular Theater
description: Modern and contemporary drama of Spain from Benavente to the present. Close study of the works of major playwrights and trends in dramatic criticism and theatrical production. Readings in theater theory.

title: Approaches to Language Teaching Methodology
description: Study of approaches to language teaching methods. Focuses on the practical application of its results to the teaching of Spanish and Portuguese. Required of all Teaching Assistants in the Department of Spanish and Portuguese.

title: The Sound Patterns of Spanish
description:
Students will gain a strong understanding about the sound patterns in different Spanish varieties, how the patterns are represented in the mind, how to describe them and carry out acoustic analyses. 
Prerequisite: **350 or 351 or LING 301 or LING 302 or LING 303 or LING 304 or SHS *303.

title: Seminar in Hispanic Sociolinguistics
description: Linguistic variation in relation to internal, social, regional and situational factors. Topics include variation theory, language contact, language and gender, and language planning. The practical application of sociolinguistic approaches will be introduced.

title: Seminar in Southwest Spanish
description: Research seminar covering all aspects of Chicano Spanish: linguistic structure, regional and social variation, bilingualism, maintenance and shift, English influence, etc. Prerequisite: 351 or LING 301.

title: Seminar in the Language of Spain or Spanish America
description:
An advanced course providing students with the opportunity to develop expertise in linguistic analysis. A broad range of branches are covered, including sociolinguistics, discourse analysis, phonetics, mophosyntax, semantics and psycholinguisticis.
Prerequisite: 351 or LING 301.

title: Graduate Problems
description:
Individual research into an area proposed by the student and conducted under the direction of a faculty member. 
Restriction: permission of instructor.

title: Childhood Bilingualism
description: This course examines various aspects of bilingual language acquisition, including phonology, morphology, syntax, and semantics. Several prominent theoretical questions are explored: Do linguistic systems develop similarly among monolingual and bilingual children? How do the linguistic systems interact, e.g., are linguistic forms transferred, do children code-switch like adults, etc.? What happens to children’s first language when they acquire a second language?

title: Topics in Southwest Hispanic Literature
description: Study of literary genres and periods, including Chicano theater, narrative, poetry, women's writing, etc.

title: Topics in Southwest Culture and Folklore
description: Study of oral genres and folkways of Spanish-speaking people of the American Southwest and appropriate theoretical approaches.

title: Master's Thesis
description:
Faculty-supervised investigative study that results in the development and writing of a master’s thesis.
Offered on a CR/NC basis only.

title: Literary Theory
description: This course will offer either an overview of critical theory or an in-depth treatment of a critical school or individual theorist.

title: Seminar in Spanish Peninsular Literature
description: Topics may include, but are not limited to, Medieval Witchcraft, Golden Age Prose, Love and Death in Spanish Literature, 19th-Century Novel, Generation of 1898 Prose, Women in Literature and Film, Women Writers.

title: Seminar in Spanish American Literature
description: Topical seminars geared to doctoral students, emphasizing the literature of one country or region (e.g., Argentine novel), one genre (e.g., romantic poetry), the literary essay, essential or complete works of one author or trend (e.g., the dictator novel).

title: El Arte de la Escritura
description: Topics explore a particular genre, period, writer, region, and/or literary movement. Topics vary.

title: Topics in Cultural Geographies in the Spanish-Speaking World
description: Topics explore relationships between space, place, and cultural expression. How does cultural expression fix, reinforce or deconstruct socio-symbolic spaces such as kingdom, nation, favela, or prison? What meanings are shaped by conquest, migration or globalization?

title: Topics in Race, Ethnicity, Class, and Gender
description: Topics examine the construction, representation, and contestation of identity, considering race, ethnicity, class, gender and/or their intersection. Focus may be on representation, performance or the emergence of new subjectivities.

title: Topics in Social Justice, Rights, and Insurgency
